text_initial: Yes, Social media is absolutely making people more stupid on the whole. People have learned not to think for themselves. They have learned to get all information on life from the internet and from watching videos, seeing articles on social media etc and assuming that the information they are being presented is true. They don't take the time to fact check themselves or the information that is being given to them and therefore begin to adopt beliefs that are not true and do not actually represent accurate depictions of certain events.
text_all: Yes, Social media is absolutely making people more stupid on the whole. People have learned not to think for themselves. They have learned to get all information on life from the internet and from watching videos, seeing articles on social media etc and assuming that the information they are being presented is true. They don't take the time to fact check themselves or the information that is being given to them and therefore begin to adopt beliefs that are not true and do not actually represent accurate depictions of certain events.  If we were to actually gather data, I don't think it would support the argument that social media has made people smarter. I think we would find out that people all too frequently are taking things they see on social media as the truth and therefore they are being fed misinformation from multiple different angles. People become so engrossed in what they are doing that they are unlikely to be taking the time to actually research what they are being told and find out if the information is truly accurate. They take "influencers" for example and assume that because they have some level of popularity that they must know what they are talking about and are telling people the truth. They themselves start to spread this information to others and wind up making the masses less intelligent on the whole than they were before. I stand by my position that misinformation is being spread rampantly, whether intentionally or just purely through ignorance. Too often people just don't know any better and will spread things they see via social media to others on the assumption that because their friend/family member saw it and posted it, that it must be true. We need people to think for themselves and take the time to go through the information they are being fed and actually did into what is true. Social media is often not the real world and doesn't always give an accurate depiction of what the real world is actually like or what is actually going on. News and information spread easier because of social media, but if what is being spread isn't accurate or useful, you would have a hard time convincing me that people are suddenly smarter because of something they saw from a 12 year old on Tik Tok

text_initial: Social media is not making people more stupid, but allows them to research topics and actually become smarter as they can interact better with those around them. Reasoning to show an example of this is how people who have not taken a traditional college or schooling route are able to make a lot of money without ever entering the working world. The idea of the influencer might seem somewhat disingenuous or vanity filled on the surface, but at the same time, it is a growing profession that can absolutely pay off as we have seen with many people. Social media is something that has taken giant steps and leaps to grow, and it will only do so because of its popularity and ability to focus on intellectual and sophisticated interaction, which then allows people to develop entrepreneurial skills. Therefore, the proof that social media is making people stupid is quite the opposite. It's making them smarter.

Social media aids in the formal conversation and discussion of varying ideas and topics. Diversity of thought is seen abundantly across social media platforms and because of this, people are learning from one another and are crafting in creating new ideas, innovations, and ways of pursuing wealth and happiness. This could only be done by a group of people who have the ability and the smarts to go against social norms and expectations. In this case, social media has made people smarter because they are willing to challenge the typical ways of communicating and job searching and stand by the millions of dollars they have made as they continue to do so. In my closing argument, I would certainly like to stress the fact that while what we see on social media might appear alien or abnormal, it is certainly worth noting that it now has become trendy and is taking over as a whole because the lifestyle and glamour that it provides is not out of reach any longer. The walls and odds might be stacked against someone who did not go to school or who does not have wealthy parental figures. With the invention of social media, everyone not only has an equal chance at life and accomplishing their natural talents, but those high walls are now taken down as the playing field is now level and even. Social media is now becoming reality whereas before it was only a small reflection of it.

text_initial:
Different countries have different needs and capabilities. It is not fair for all countries to have the same policies as it will effect many countries differently than others.

Some countries have contributed far more to global warming than others, so it is not fair to put the same restrictions on all countries.

International policies infringes upon the right of a country to make decisions for themselves.

Policies will hurt some countries more economically than others, causing them to be at a huge disadvantage.
text_all:
Different countries have different needs and capabilities. It is not fair for all countries to have the same policies as it will effect many countries differently than others.

Some countries have contributed far more to global warming than others, so it is not fair to put the same restrictions on all countries.

International policies infringes upon the right of a country to make decisions for themselves.

Policies will hurt some countries more economically than others, causing them to be at a huge disadvantage. These international agreements will be political in nature and be more beneficial for some countries than others. Those with the most power will dictate the rules to make the policies advantageous to them and try to use them to hurt their enemies. This will end up causing more problems than solutions and may lead to wars. These policies are unlikely to take into account the differences between countries and their priorities. They also will not likely address the fact that some countries contribute far more to the problem than others. This only sounds good in theory, but in practicality this is not how things will play out. Politics will get involved and governments will try to abuse these policies in order to make money and hurt their political opponents. This will make the divide even larger and we will end up further behind than we already are.  There is simply no trust among nations, and without the trust, these policies serve to do nothing except waste money, time, and resources.

text_initial: The US should provide military aid to Ukraine against Russia for a number of reasons.  The first reason is a moral argument.  Russia is the aggressor country and is killing and raping innocent people.  They are targeting infrastructure and grain silos to try to freeze and starve Ukraine to death.  The US also was a signatory to the Buddhapist Accords which says that the US will agree to help Ukraine in it's defense for Ukraine giving up it's nuclear weapons.\n\nThe US has stockpiled tons of weapons during the Cold War in order to defeat Russia should there be a conflict between the two countries.  These weapons are close to being decomissioned which costs a lot of money.  It costs us less money to send these to Ukraine.
text_all:
The US should provide military aid to Ukraine against Russia for a number of reasons.  The first reason is a moral argument.  Russia is the aggressor country and is killing and raping innocent people.  They are targeting infrastructure and grain silos to try to freeze and starve Ukraine to death.  The US also was a signatory to the Buddhapist Accords which says that the US will agree to help Ukraine in it's defense for Ukraine giving up it's nuclear weapons.

The US has stockpiled tons of weapons during the Cold War in order to defeat Russia should there be a conflict between the two countries.  These weapons are close to being decomissioned which costs a lot of money.  It costs us less money to send these to Ukraine. Russia is trying to run out the clock so that people lose patience with Ukraine.  If Russia controls Ukraine, the US is going to have much more serious issues to worry about in addition to the one's at home.  For example, Putin will next move into Maldova.  Then it will be on to the Baltics and then to Poland.  The Baltics and Poland are both part of NATO, which will get us in a direct war.

Appeasement never works with dictators.  We tried this with Hitler during WW2 to give him Czechlovakia and he just kept going.  We tried this with Putin in 2014 with Crimea and the Donbass.  Dictators keep going so stop them now before it costs us more in the long run. American's should not fund dictator countries.  America should try to fund the opposition.  That way dictators all over the world will not be emboldened to invade or commit human rights abuses.  Putin's biggest fear is that he will end up like Gadaffi - dead in a ditch, which is how all dictators should end up.  All of our foreign policy problems are due to dictators - China, Russia, North Korea, Venezuela, the Middle East, Cuba.

text_initial: I believe that social media is making people less intelligent. I think that as social media has become more prevalent in use, the age of use of social media has gotten lower and lower to the point where social media is now being used by children who should not be using it. Due to this, the children are using social media for large amounts of time. In my opinion, this time could be better spent practicing reading, math, and other skills they should be honing in school. Instead, most children with access to social media are using their devices much more than they should be. This is causing stunts in their development, which is literally contributing to them becoming less intelligent than the last generation. If someone uses their time to practice a skill versus looking at a screen for 30 minutes a day, believe the person practicing the skill would end up being more intelligent, and therefore I believe my argument is proven true.
text_all: I believe that social media is making people less intelligent. I think that as social media has become more prevalent in use, the age of use of social media has gotten lower and lower to the point where social media is now being used by children who should not be using it. Due to this, the children are using social media for large amounts of time. In my opinion, this time could be better spent practicing reading, math, and other skills they should be honing in school. Instead, most children with access to social media are using their devices much more than they should be. This is causing stunts in their development, which is literally contributing to them becoming less intelligent than the last generation. If someone uses their time to practice a skill versus looking at a screen for 30 minutes a day, believe the person practicing the skill would end up being more intelligent, and therefore I believe my argument is proven true. I agree with you about the fact that they can access information from anywhere. However, we are talking about social media and not the internet in general. On social media, fake information and fake news is now becoming prevalent and if individuals are getting their information strictly from social media, they may not be getting correct information. Gaining knowledge is a good indicator of intelligence, but what if the information is incorrect? If I am learning a ton of math in school, but it is the wrong math with the wrong answers, am I still getting smarter? No would be the answer in that situation. Just because we are able to engage with others does not mean we are getting correct information.  I stand firm in my argument. Anyone can impersonate anyone on social media. If I reach out to an "expert" for an answer to something and I receive the incorrect answer, does that mean I am making myself more intelligent? No. That means I am thinking I am gaining knowledge when I am, in fact, not gaining anything. And then I may go on to spread that bad information to others thinking it is true, which is in fact affecting other's intelligence as well. If I am looking to learn something, I absolutely do not go onto social media looking for an answer. I would look to the internet and go after verified and reputable scholarly sources. Do you have any proof of your claim? No.

text_initial: Social media is not making people stupid. Social media provides individuals with more access to information that ever before. It allows them to talk to people all over the world. It allows them the ability to learn about anything they can think of. It gives them experience with different cultures, along with being able to hear different opinions and information. It allows them to engage with others on any topic and hear many different points of view. It provides a vast array of experiences that can make an individual more aware of the world at large.
text_all: Social media is not making people stupid. Social media provides individuals with more access to information that ever before. It allows them to talk to people all over the world. It allows them the ability to learn about anything they can think of. It gives them experience with different cultures, along with being able to hear different opinions and information. It allows them to engage with others on any topic and hear many different points of view. It provides a vast array of experiences that can make an individual more aware of the world at large.  Social media is all about how you use it. Anyone can use it to just mess around with nonsense. The people who do this would probably waste their time anyway. Social media is the ultimate access to experiences and information. If you want to learn how to do something you can ask experts. If you want to know about something you can join a group and talk others about it. It is full of different cultures and different people who can offer unique experiences and opinions. It allows you to open your mind to possibilities. You can learn skills using social media, even if that skill is just how to best communicate with others different from yourself.  Yes social media does spread some misinformation but so does the internet in general. It is up to the user to be smart about where they get their information. As long as a user is logical they will be able to wade through the misinformation. Social media more than anything allows you the unique experience to interact with the world at large. It allows you to learn about people from anywhere, and everywhere. It provides you will access to information about anything you want to learn about. It allows human connection so that you can learn more about yourself and about the world. Any hobby you can think of has a fanbase you can join to expand your own understanding and knowledge.

text_initial: Arts Education is just as important, if not more important, as Science and Math in school. While math and science give us the skills and tools to understand various life aspects, art goes much deeper. Literary and musical arts, in particular, allow us to comprehend other cultures and think outside the box. It's what allows us to be creative and have emotions. It's what makes us feel unique and important. It gives us purpose.
text_all: Arts Education is just as important, if not more important, as Science and Math in school. While math and science give us the skills and tools to understand various life aspects, art goes much deeper. Literary and musical arts, in particular, allow us to comprehend other cultures and think outside the box. It's what allows us to be creative and have emotions. It's what makes us feel unique and important. It gives us purpose.  I agree that science, technology, and math create an obedient and disciplined employee; one that will become one of the many cogs of the machine. However, there's no freedom and choice in these fields. To be creative, you need choice and free thinking. And these are the foundation of innovation and invention. Without them, you will simply follow the demands of others. I can agree that STEM (science, technology, economics, and math) are fundamental to the growing challenges of the world. However, without the study of arts, I don't believe students will be prepared or the many challenges they'll face out in the real world. We couldn't survive without both, if I'm being honest. But I would prefer our next generation of workers to have a better understanding of life than they do of trigonometry.

text_all: I will argue that the U.S. shouldn't be siding with Ukraine in its war with Russia or providing direct military aid. Although it is possible to agree that Ukraine was unfairly attacked by Russia, the U.S. should stay out of interfering with world conflicts directly and focus its efforts at issues at home instead. The U.S. frequently has to interpret international law to see that it aligns with the actual facts of what happened and gauging whether the act of aggression - if it's determined to have take place - represents a direct threat to either the U.S. as a country or the alliances to which the U.S. belongs. In this case, the aggression has taken place entirely in Europe and represents no threat to our country. Additionally, several European nations interpreted Russia's actions differently, so the decision to support Ukraine isn't uniformly accepted and should be further considered in detail. As I had mentioned before, the U.S. has to balance its commitment to global democratic values with the realities of global politics. International peace could be better served by economic pressures levied against Russia and its allies, as well as better ties to Russia's current silent partners, such as China and India. If we could successfully pressure China to step down its support of Russia, Russian campaign against Ukraine might fall apart without direct U.S. military intervention and international peace will prevail through diplomacy.

text_initial: The US should ban fossil fuels as individuals' efforts to combat climate change might not be enough (recycling, using public transportation). While banning fossil fuels might not be sufficient for reversing climate change, it can make a difference in reducing the effects of climate change. Climate change needs to be combated on a individual, local, national, and corporate level to see real change. If not, future generations may not have a planet, biodiversity will decrease, and natural resources will continue to be depleted. The US should specifically have a responsibility as Americans tend to be significant emitters because of our lifestyle.
text_all: The US should ban fossil fuels as individuals' efforts to combat climate change might not be enough (recycling, using public transportation). While banning fossil fuels might not be sufficient for reversing climate change, it can make a difference in reducing the effects of climate change. Climate change needs to be combated on a individual, local, national, and corporate level to see real change. If not, future generations may not have a planet, biodiversity will decrease, and natural resources will continue to be depleted. The US should specifically have a responsibility as Americans tend to be significant emitters because of our lifestyle.  A ban would be something to work towards eventually. This can be done in phases to reach the long-term goal so fossil fuels aren't eradicated cold turkey. By starting these habit changes eventually, we can start relying on other resources to wean ourselves off. Relying too much on one thing alone can be negative as fossil fuels themselves likely have a shorter shelf life than we think. For example, we should increasingly rely on solar energy and clean energy resources.  The US has a responsibility to combat climate change as we have more resources than other nations, have a larger spotlight on us on a global scale, and we tend to be bigger emitters because of daily lifestyle choices. Without efforts, our planet has a ticking timer and this needs to be taken seriously. Other countries are more advanced when it comes to adopting solar and wind power. While we have other resources available, we should take advantage as we're not in a position where fossil fuels is the only thing we're relying on and haven't found substitutes.

I think we can agree that a ban would be something to work towards eventually. \nMy original argument, to not force a ban immediately, seems to be the logical conclusion at this point.

text_initial: Is artificial intelligence good for society? The short answer is Yes. Artificial intelligence or AI will bring many changes to the world that will make things better, including medical innovations, improving public health, increasing productivity for ordinary people, and making it easier for governments and officials to detect and respond to longterm trends. Artificial intelligence can also help us to fight climate change which is a global problem. Overall, I see AI as something that should not be marginalized in its advancements if we want to move things forward as a society. Ultimately, it will be embraced across the world in all different countries so rather than considering whether or not it is good for society, we should focus on how to implement AI ethically. With the proper rules and safeguards on AI as agreed upon by world governments, we can make sure that AI is indeed good for society.
text_all: Is artificial intelligence good for society? The short answer is Yes. Artificial intelligence or AI will bring many changes to the world that will make things better, including medical innovations, improving public health, increasing productivity for ordinary people, and making it easier for governments and officials to detect and respond to longterm trends. Artificial intelligence can also help us to fight climate change which is a global problem. Overall, I see AI as something that should not be marginalized in its advancements if we want to move things forward as a society. Ultimately, it will be embraced across the world in all different countries so rather than considering whether or not it is good for society, we should focus on how to implement AI ethically. With the proper rules and safeguards on AI as agreed upon by world governments, we can make sure that AI is indeed good for society. AI isn't not the problem when it comes to threats to job security, socio-economic inequalities, and personal privacy risks. In fact, it is the lack of policy in place to defend against and alleviate the harms that AI could potentially cause. It is up to government regulators to make it a matter of policy to ensure that businesses do not use AI in a manner than can harm people. All of these threats are entirely preventable. First, it will start with ensuring that AI is democratized and that this powerful technology is not maliciously implemented by the rich and powerful. The argument that governments will struggle to efficient implement restrictions on AI could be resolved by the government itself monitoring these tools and making sure that companies are not violating people's rights and causing inequality. Furthermore, the government should implement an AI tax, for example, on businesses that earn the majority if not all of their revenue from leveraging AI. If the governments put restrictions in place that do not have loopholes then there would be a better end result.

text_initial: I am in favor of the proposition that every citizen should receive a basic income from the government. It's true that we deal with rising costs of living and every person has various skillsets and abilities, in regards to the kind of jobs they can acquire. Receiving a basic income can eliminate the process of applying for jobs and acquiring further education to obtain a higher paying job. Receiving a basic income can ensure that everyone receives the same pay and there would be less cases of discrimination and socioeconomic inequality. There wouldn't be a need to divide society into different classes because everyone's income would be the same. Receiving basic income can reduce poverty, improves mental health, increases purchasing power, especially amongst the poor and it is motivational.

I think a basic income can provide people with the opportunity to grow and the time to take care of themselves. It can encourage people to not be so dependent because they have the resources to do what they feel is best. That argument is conditional on many things. It wouldn't necessarily disincentivize personal growth - it really depends on the disposition of the individual. Furthermore, inflation existed even before the idea of Basic Income came about. I disagree that it would cause inflation. Inflation is caused by money being pumped into the economy. However, I believe a Basic Income can stimulate the economy by increasing purchasing power and encourages people to spend. They also have the freedom to spend it on entrepreneurship opportunities.

text_initial: Animals should certainly be used for scientific research. The benefits of animal research are far-reaching, especially when it comes to searching for cures and treatments for disease. The use of animals gives researchers an ethical way to effectively test various treatments on living mammals that serve as stand-ins for their human counterparts. The results from these experiments are much more reliable than "modeled" research whose results may not be achieved in the real world.\n\nThere are ethical concerns - no animals should be mistreated or abused during the process. There should be no pain infl
text_all: Animals should certainly be used for scientific research. The benefits of animal research are far-reaching, especially when it comes to searching for cures and treatments for disease. The use of animals gives researchers an ethical way to effectively test various treatments on living mammals that serve as stand-ins for their human counterparts. The results from these experiments are much more reliable than "modeled" research whose results may not be achieved in the real world.\n\nThere are ethical concerns - no animals should be mistreated or abused during the process. There should be no pain infl Animals can feel pain, and steps should be taken to mitigate any pain infliction. However, animals are not people. Nature is ruthless, and animals taking advantage of other animals (for example, as food) is rampant throughout the animal kingdom. The use of animals for a benevolent purpose is easily enough to counterbalance any ethical concerns about their use in testing.  Testing on humans is far more unethical. Humans have relationships that are far more complex, and much stronger, than any bonds animals are capable of forming. Animals have no legal rights in the same vain - after all, we kill them for food, use them for labor, own them as pets, and kill pests when it suits us. They are a lesser form of life, and are far more expendible in this way.

text_initial: Animals are living beings that can feel pain.  They are unable to consent to their bodies being used for scientific research.  Their quality of life may be significantly reduced through being used for scientific research.  It's irrational to set different standards for how we treat people versus how we treat animals, because our souls don't get to choose the species of the Earthly vessels we inhabit.  Animals may be less capable of problem-solving and other types of advanced cognition compared to humans, but this doesn't necessarily mean that their experience of the world is less vivid, or less profound.  All living beings should be treated with respect and dignity, and animals should be included in that.
text_all: Animals are living beings that can feel pain.  They are unable to consent to their bodies being used for scientific research.  Their quality of life may be significantly reduced through being used for scientific research.  It's irrational to set different standards for how we treat people versus how we treat animals, because our souls don't get to choose the species of the Earthly vessels we inhabit.  Animals may be less capable of problem-solving and other types of advanced cognition compared to humans, but this doesn't necessarily mean that their experience of the world is less vivid, or less profound.  All living beings should be treated with respect and dignity, and animals should be included in that.   You suggest that animals can be used for testing without being mistreated or abused.  If that were the case, then why would they need to be used at all?  Why couldn't humans be used instead?  The implied reason for using animals in scientific research is that any harm which would otherwise be inflicted upon a human, is instead inflicted upon a being which doesn't have the legal right to say no to being experimented upon.   The idea that personhood should not be extended to animals is phrased here in a way that makes it sound like a statement of fact:  "Animals are not people."  However, in the same breath, my opponent states that the animal kingdom is a ruthless place.  This, again, is a statement of opinion.  Animals are altruistic sometimes.  We should hold ourselves to a high standard when treating animals.

text_initial: There should be mandatory quotas for women in leadership positions because it is the only reliable way to encourage companies to be more diverse when hiring or promoting employees. Without mandatory quotas companies will be free to lie or manipulate the data in favor of the status quo. Quotas can be set based on population data to make sure the targets do not discriminate against other groups.\n
text_all: There should be mandatory quotas for women in leadership positions because it is the only reliable way to encourage companies to be more diverse when hiring or promoting employees. Without mandatory quotas companies will be free to lie or manipulate the data in favor of the status quo. Quotas can be set based on population data to make sure the targets do not discriminate against other groups.\n That argument would be believable if the current system was actually based on a meritocracy. Companies have been given plenty of chances to diversify their hiring practices but have historically passed over women in favor of men with questionable qualifications. If hiring was purely done on a merit basis currently many companies should be doing better than they are. If diversity in hiring causes discomfort in employees then I would make the case that those employees are the problem. Companies can still invest in talent development while ensuring that leadership represents the diversity of society. Reverse discrimination simply does not exist and is often used as an excuse to keep leadership predominantly male.

text_initial: Government surveillance is absolutely necessary for national security. Prior to 2001, the U.S. had a significant national security structure to prevent terrorism and promote national security, but it as the events of September 11th, 2001 showed, it was not enough. Things like the Patriot Act enabled the U.S. government to create a massive digital filter, suctioning up great amounts of data to sift through and prevent any potential terrorist acts from occurring. The greater sharing promoted by the September 11th attacks also served to ensure the intelligence made it to the right places to stop any events before they occurred. Additionally, the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act served to promote further digital surveillance of personnel suspected of terrorism related crimes, further empowering the government to proactively prevent these acts before they occur. Government surveillance is critical to the success of our continued national security.
text_all: Government surveillance is absolutely necessary for national security. Prior to 2001, the U.S. had a significant national security structure to prevent terrorism and promote national security, but it as the events of September 11th, 2001 showed, it was not enough. Things like the Patriot Act enabled the U.S. government to create a massive digital filter, suctioning up great amounts of data to sift through and prevent any potential terrorist acts from occurring. The greater sharing promoted by the September 11th attacks also served to ensure the intelligence made it to the right places to stop any events before they occurred. Additionally, the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act served to promote further digital surveillance of personnel suspected of terrorism related crimes, further empowering the government to proactively prevent these acts before they occur. Government surveillance is critical to the success of our continued national security. It's not possible to strike a perfect balance, however emphasizing personal privacy promotes the risk of events like 9/11 happening again. Reducing personal privacy a small amount to reduce the chance of over 3,000 people dying over the course of several hours. Counter to your point, reduced privacy has paid off in that it increases security. We have not had a successful large terrorist attack in over twenty years, with the Patriot Act being implemented in October 2001. It's now December 2023, and outside of several small attacks like the Underwear bomber, no terrorist has been successful inside the United States. Privacy and prevention of criminal acts will always be a delicate balancing act. Prior to 2001, the U.S. leaned too far towards the civil liberties argument and prioritized data and privacy prevention. In the aftermath of 9/11, they promoted significant measures to place civil liberties at a more appropriate level while preventing terrorist attacks of that scale from happening again. Measures like the Patriot Act and the FISA Act have not significantly reduced our privacy and yet have worked remarkably well to prevent significant terrorist attacks from happening again. The small reduction in privacy after 9/11 has been well worth the cost to ensure the heinous acts of 9/11 never again occur on American soil.

text_initial: Since there is such a disparity between men and women in leadership positions, a quota system could be needed to effectively balance things quicker than simply allowing it to happen organically. It would send the signal that other women could become leaders in the future, through present day examples.
text_all: Since there is such a disparity between men and women in leadership positions, a quota system could be needed to effectively balance things quicker than simply allowing it to happen organically. It would send the signal that other women could become leaders in the future, through present day examples. Meritocracy and quotas could be mutually compatible, so this argument is a false dilemma. So long as the special treatment is temporary and addressing structural or historical problems, it doesn't imply that women are essentially inferior. Quotas can help provide career examples for future candidates, open up more possible candidates who may not have considered the roles but a highly qualified, and be combined with a meritocratic system. The stereotype that women can only succeed with special treatment is not as harmful as other problems, so even assuming this would be an issue it would be relatively unimportant.

text_initial: School uniforms should not be required for students. It adds a financial burden that not all families can overcome. Many families and students are financially constrained, and school uniforms will not be the priority purchase. Another argument against school uniforms is that they are wasteful. They are a clothing item that are not necessary. Additionally, if they are produced cheaply and made affordable, there is a greater chance that they may be produced by exploited, or basically slave, labor.
text_all: School uniforms should not be required for students. It adds a financial burden that not all families can overcome. Additionally, if they are produced cheaply and made affordable, there is a greater chance that they may be produced by exploited, or basically slave, labor.  As a former student of a uniform-requiring school, school uniforms did not lead to a sense of equality and belonging among myself and fellow classmates. Cliques still formed and there were still hierarchies. Even though we all wore the same uniform, it was evident which students had money and which did not. The quality and condition of our uniforms along with shoes and other accessories clearly spelt out class differences. Skilled teachers are more effective at reducing distraction in a classroom than a uniform code. Students may also be more comfortable in their own clothing and that will promote better learning. Eliminating the chance of exploitation in production is not that same as ensuring it. There are unnecessary added layers to a goal (wearing uniforms) that isn't even objectively important. Financial assistance programs still requires costs coming from somewhere. It is never free. text_initial: The concept of the minimum wage has always been on the publics mind. Many government officials have strong opinions on this matter, some overwhelmingly against raising the minimum wage. However, if government positions were paid the minimum wage it would not only attract more people who will fight to make change, but it will also make the topic one that can be discussed by people actually experiencing it. By having the officials experience the
text_all: The concept of the minimum wage has always been on the publics mind. Many government officials have strong opinions on this matter, some overwhelmingly against raising the minimum wage. However, if government positions were paid the minimum wage it would not only attract more people who will fight to make change, but it will also make the topic one that can be discussed by people actually experiencing it. For too long government officials have had the chance to make decisions on matters that do not directly impact them and that leads to an overall disconnect between the people and those chosen to serve. By having the officials experience the  My opponent brings up a good point - who will do the job for minimum wage. Yes there are less stressful jobs out there, however this will still be pursued by those passionate about social change. Many people take on lower paying jobs because it's what they are passionate about - not because they are an already wealthy group. This will once again allow people to make decisions that not only impact the masses, but themselves as well as they will be experiencing it too. For these reasons we need to pay our officials the minimum wage to lead to change. It would attract those who want real change because it's what they are passionate about. The kicker is that those in power will finally be able to experience the difficulties of living on minimum wage and the end effect will likely be an increase in the minimum wage. This change would be difficult at first, but once people experience the trials and tribulations of living on an unlivable wage, there is a real chance for change to happen. Not only will people continue to pursue these jobs, but with their passion they can drive real social change.

text_initial: It is difficult to promote paying government officials only minimum wage. One issue is the question of who would actually do the job for minimum wage, There are many other minimum wage jobs out there that are less stress and responsibility. Likely the position would attract people who are already wealthy through some means, and want the power of the position. It also wouldn't help corruption, as people desperate for money are likely going to be susceptible to bribes. The position would attract a very select group of people that don't represent most . The idealistic side of it that our officials should know what it's like to live on minimum wage is great, but the real problems that poses might be insurmountable.

It also wouldn't help corruption, as people desperate for money are likely going to be susceptible to bribes. The position would attract a very select group of people that don't represent most . The idealistic side of it that our officials should know what it's like to live on minimum wage is great, but the real problems that poses might be insurmountable.  How can paying minimum wage attract people who want to fight for change? Those people need to live, eat, support their families as well. The only way these people could pursue a minimum wage position with this level of responsibility is if they have someone with enough wealth supporting them. Those who are already wealthy are going to fight to preserve their wealth, not in the best interest of the masses. These positions would attract a select group of people who want power and don't need moneym. Passion cannot provide a roof over one's head or food on their table. A select few people without access to other wealth can manage this situation - those who are single with very low living expenses, no family to provide for. But the majority of people who have the passion and right ideas to implement changes, simply wouldn't be able to afford taking this position. If they had to work multiple jobs to make it work, I would say that would be a detriment to their ability to think clearly and lead. And if they fell upon any sort of hard time, it would leave them susceptible to bribes.

text_initial: Standardized testing cannot effectively account for all of a student's needs and performance metrics, and as such should not be implemented in schools. It is apparent that a catch-all method of testing millions of students could never address the personal circumstances of specific states, or regions, or neighborhoods of students; to say nothing of individual students themselves. Because of the rigidity of standardized testing and its inapplicability to individual students, it has no place in a school setting.
text_all: Standardized testing cannot effectively account for all of a student's needs and performance metrics, and as such should not be implemented in schools. It is apparent that a catch-all method of testing millions of students could never address the personal circumstances of specific states, or regions, or neighborhoods of students; to say nothing of individual students themselves. Because of the rigidity of standardized testing and its inapplicability to individual students, it has no place in a school setting. Proponents of standardized testing might argue that it's there to ensure fairness, but in reality it does anything but. Since students of different regions, socioeconomic backgrounds, and upbringings will necessarily perform differently, it's unfair to evaluate them all against the same measures to begin with. That is to say that the system by which they are evaluated is already flawed, regardless of whether or not it is applied the same way to all. You cannot simply ensure equality with equal treatment--it is a strange but true fact of life. In sum, standardized testing cannot account for unique circumstances of students and therefore cannot be used as a yardstick for student achievement. How could there exist an objective measurement of educational progress, when such incredible diversity exists in how students nationwide are taught, what they are taught, and how they learn, both in the classroom and out? Until standardized testing is able to reconcile with the inherent flaws in its measurements it should not be used in schools.

text_initial: Washington, DC and Puerto Rico should be granted US statehood. There are numerous benefits to them and very few drawbacks. Both places have citizens living and working there who are currently not being represented in Congress or in Presidential elections. This gives those citizens fewer rights than the citizens in the 50 states. Additionally, making DC and Puerto Rico states would allow funding, etc. to flow through the same channels as other states, making things simpler for everybody.
text_all: Washington, DC and Puerto Rico should be granted US statehood. There are numerous benefits to them and very few drawbacks. Both places have citizens living and working there who are currently not being represented in Congress or in Presidential elections. This gives those citizens fewer rights than the citizens in the 50 states. Additionally, making DC and Puerto Rico states would allow funding, etc. to flow through the same channels as other states, making things simpler for everybody. DC would retain its status as the nation's capital, even if granted statehood. This would be a technical, not practical, change that would benefit the ordinary people who call DC home. As for Puerto Rico, admitting it to the Union would not be any different than admitting Alaska or Hawaii. Those places have distinct cultural differences that they have retained while also getting representation in the government they live under. These changes will not fundamentally reshape America's political landscape in any appreciable way. DC already has non-voting representation in Congress. It's a voice, but it's not a vote, and votes are how decisions are ultimately made. There are millions of people living in these areas who currently do not have access to the same representation that the rest of us do. There is an argument to be made that granting statehood might not be the only solution that needs to be implemented, but it won't hurt. Like I said, this is more of a change in name, not practice--people are already living in these places, and they need a voice.

text_initial: In the US there is an expectation that every person has the freedom to chart their own path, to forge their future. However, in our current system that is not possible. College education has always been gated off to those who are unable to afford it, and more importantly not even a guarantee of a lucrative career path. For those who wish to even try, must incur thousands of dollars in debt, even more for "prestige" colleges. Amounts of money that only a few majors could even have the chance at paying a salary to make incurring that debt financially worth the strain.
text_all: In the US there is an expectation that every person has the freedom to chart their own path, to forge their future. However, in our current system that is not possible. College education has always been gated off to those who are unable to afford it, and more importantly not even a guarantee of a lucrative career path. For those who wish to even try, must incur thousands of dollars in debt, even more for "prestige" colleges. Amounts of money that only a few majors could even have the chance at paying a salary to make incurring that debt financially worth the strain.  As our country becomes further educated the value of a degree is not guaranteed to shift downward. In fact, individual degrees may become more lucrative as more jobs open, people go on to build business, and potentially gain contracts from other countries. The idea that we do not have the money is also false. We would be moving funds from those who would get them from government assistance programs to social classes where they would no longer need it. Thus shift funds away from those programs as they no longer required them. No one should have to take on crippling debt in order to seek a better life. There are countless individuals who love to open up their business but cannot take on said risk financially because school is too expensive or they have loans already and cannot secure new ones or would be far too risky. Those potential business starters could employ thousands of people who have degrees who seek employment. Ultimately we need these individuals who are willing to be entrepreneurs and create meaningful employment, increasing the job prospect.

text_initial: Those earning over $250,000 per year should not incur a tax raise. I say this because raising the taxes on those who earn more than $250,000 will raise prices for everyone. The rich set the prices, and raising expenses for the rich will increase expenses for everyone across the board.
text_all: Those earning over $250,000 per year should not incur a tax raise. I say this because raising the taxes on those who earn more than $250,000 will raise prices for everyone. The rich set the prices, and raising expenses for the rich will increase expenses for everyone across the board. Since the society we live in has operated as a capitalistic society, it would take a lot of undoing to reverse hundreds of years of tax cuts for the rich. Perhaps this will work with little tax raises towards those earning more than $250,000 a year, but as the system is, raising the taxes of the rich will only raise expenses for everyone else. Since those who earn more than $250,000 hold most of the wealth (including businesses), those individuals are directly in charge of adjusting prices, wages, and other transactions that directly affect those who earn less than $250,000. Until the system is adjusted to prevent this from happening, raising the taxes on the rich will only incur a net loss for those who earn less than $250,000 a year.

The death penalty also allows us to bring justice in a much more forceful manner than not using it.

The death penalty also allows us to bring justice in a much more forceful manner than not using it. Yes, the taxpayers are benefitting from the death penalty. Yes, we would be killing a living human being for a crime, but they knew going into the crime what the potential consequences would be. They had no remorse for the victims, so why should we have any for the criminals? If we were to look back at the history books and see that we allowed human monsters to get away with such horrible crimes by allowing them to live even after they have stolen the lives of others, then we would be vieewed  In closing, the death penalty should be legal. It brings real justice to the victims. It saves money for the taxpayers. It shows that we do not stand for such heinous crimes. The death penalty is not only a good thing, it is a necessary thing. We must show that we are against major crimes to deter any potential future criminals from acting out of line on a scale that is so large.

text_initial: The death penalty should not be legal. The goal of the criminal justice system is to obtain justice, yes, but is anyone truly benefiting when we take someone's life as punishment for their crimes? Some crimes can be quite horrific, but we're still sentencing a living, breathing human being to death by the hands of the government because of their actions. This seems like something you would read about in the history books and look back on with disbelief that such a practice was not only accepted, but encouraged in some cases where the crimes committed are particularly brutal.
text_all: The death penalty should not be legal. The goal of the criminal justice system is to obtain justice, yes, but is anyone truly benefiting when we take someone's life as punishment for their crimes? Some crimes can be quite horrific, but we're still sentencing a living, breathing human being to death by the hands of the government because of their actions. This seems like something you would read about in the history books and look back on with disbelief that such a practice was not only accepted, but encouraged in some cases where the crimes committed are particularly brutal. Is it really fair for the victims of a crime to demand the punishment be, in some cases, the same as the crime that was committed? To trade one life for another simply because the government is doing the killing this time instead of one independent person? Making more people suffer does not erase or mitigate the suffering the victims have and will endure, and simply knowing the perpetrator is in prison with no chance of ever leaving the prison as a free individual should be enough. There's no doubt that there are some truly horrible people out there that have committed atrocious crimes, but trading one life for another should not be something that we decide. The concept of karma or sowing and reaping is that all wrongdoing will eventually be punished one way or another. Some criminals are able to turn their lives around in prison as well, and while they may not be able to get jobs or become exceedingly profitable members of society, they still can do something with their lives. We only are guaranteed this one life, so we might as well try to enjoy it for as long as possible.

text_initial: The truth is that both science and math are essential tools for learning and understanding the world around us, and the arts play a significant role in helping students develop creative thinking and problem-solving skills.  Additionally, the arts can help students learn how to communicate and work in teams. Creative projects and performances can help students develop self-confidence and learn how to better express their thoughts and ideas.
text_all: The truth is that both science and math are essential tools for learning and understanding the world around us, and the arts play a significant role in helping students develop creative thinking and problem-solving skills.  Additionally, the arts can help students learn how to communicate and work in teams. Creative projects and performances can help students develop self-confidence and learn how to better express their thoughts and ideas.  Arts education is just as important as science and math in schools. Not only do the arts provide students with creative outlets, but they also help students develop higher order thinking skills. Research has shown that students who are exposed to the arts have higher test scores, better problem-solving skills, and improved critical thinking skills. Furthermore, the arts help students learn how to work together and foster collaboration, which is an important skill needed for success in the adult world. Additionally, the arts can help students gain a greater appreciation for the world around them and open their minds to diverse perspectives. Thus, arts education should not be seen as a diversion, but rather as an integral part of a well-rounded education. It is valid to argue that science and math are essential foundations for most careers, and should be given priority in education. However, the arts are just as important in providing a well-rounded education. The arts allow students to think more creatively and to develop their problem-solving skills, which can be just as important as knowledge in science and math. Moreover, the arts can provide a platform for students to express their individual identities, and can be incredibly fulfilling and enjoyable. As such, it is important to prioritize both science and math, as well as the arts, to give students the fullest education possible.

text_initial:
Artificial Intelligence is, on the whole, good for society. 

First, AI can do certain things far more efficiently than humans. It can interpret text and provide responses much faster than the average person. It can process information at amazing speeds. It has also been shown to have the potential to help people do better at their work. As a part-time programmer, I have used AI to troubleshoot programming mistakes and solve certain problems. 

Also, AI can help people write better arguments and essays as well. It can help with writing marketing proposals or with a job applicant sending a cover letter to a prospective employer. AI can also provide some useful basic advice to people learning about topics. 

And it is possible
text_all:
Artificial Intelligence is, on the whole, good for society. 

First, AI can do certain things far more efficiently than humans. It can interpret text and provide responses much faster than the average person. It can process information at amazing speeds. It has also been shown to have the potential to help people do better at their work. As a part-time programmer, I have used AI to troubleshoot programming mistakes and solve certain problems. 

Also, AI can help people write better arguments and essays as well. It can help with writing marketing proposals or with a job applicant sending a cover letter to a prospective employer. AI can also provide some useful basic advice to people learning about topics. 

And it is possible  It is debatable how much AI will cause job loss. For one, trying to limit AI to preserve jobs is likely to be a losing battle. Historically, trying to limit technologies to preserve jobs has always been a short-term effort that eventually becomes obsolete over time. We might as well try to replace power shovels with individual people shoveling so we can create more jobs. Maybe AI could expand the economy and help create a better social safety net by ta There are ethical and privacy issues, but government regulation can reign in some of the more dangerous aspects of AI. AI may temporarily create economic disparities. But AI can only do so much on its own. We still need people to oversee the AI. We could train the average worker to make use of AI and do some basic oversight for AI. For people whose jobs are obsoleted by AI, the economic benefits of AI could be used to tax software companies more so that the social safety net can be expanded for people who lose jobs.

Less skilled workers may have to be moved to other industries. We may have to have training programs that aggressively place people into different careers but are realistic.

text_initial: School uniforms should be required as they level the playing field and make it so that students can focus on studies.  Without needing to worry about one's outfit, there are no separations into class structures based on income levels or preferences for interests, colors, or design patterns.  Children can still express themselves creatively during their off hours, but with school uniforms, students can focus on studying.  This also allows a student to separate school from leisure, which will allow them to enter "work mode" while learning and more effectively relax in their off hours, leading to a better school-life balance.  Requiring school uniforms also eliminates the burden of dress codes and various violations including rude or crude images or text, torn or dirty clothing, or clothing of inappropriate length or coverage.  While some students might complain, school uniforms can be a great way to instill order when it is necessary.
text_all: School uniforms should be required as they level the playing field and make it so that students can focus on studies.  Without needing to worry about one's outfit, there are no separations into class structures based on income levels or preferences for interests, colors, or design patterns.  Children can still express themselves creatively during their off hours, but with school uniforms, students can focus on studying.  This also allows a student to separate school from leisure, which will allow them to enter "work mode" while learning and more effectively relax in their off hours, leading to a better school-life balance.  Requiring school uniforms also eliminates the burden of dress codes and various violations including rude or crude images or text, torn or dirty clothing, or clothing of inappropriate length or coverage.  While some students might complain, school uniforms can be a great way to instill order when it is necessary. While students may find it difficult to express themselves visually from a clothing standpoint, they are still humans, so they have unique personalities, behaviors, faces, and hair.  While it is important for a child to express themselves, school uniforms allow this to be kept to a minimum during a time when structure is beneficial to their learning process.  It is not about conformity, but rather unity and structure.  Students also get individual attention from teachers from their unique personalities and their unique learning needs for each subject, so this is not eradicated. Uniforms do not necessarily mean uniformity of personalities.  Children can still express themselves with their behaviors, attitudes, discussions, and even their friend choices.  Children's autonomy remains intact no matter what they are wearing, and diversity can be seen in each student's unique characteristics instead of the clothing they choose to wear.  Some students may even find this freeing as they do not have to worry about following trends, their parents being able to afford good clothing, or that their parents may force them to wear something they don't like.  School uniforms provide structure, but the rest of a person's cahract\

text_initial: Students should not have to wear uniforms because this stifles creativity and self expression of individuals. Schools should develop a positive culture on something other than conformity. Schools should focus on cultivating an educational environment that values and nurtures the unique qualities of each student.
text_all: Students should not have to wear uniforms because this stifles creativity and self expression of individuals. Schools should develop a positive culture on something other than conformity. Schools should focus on cultivating an educational environment that values and nurtures the unique qualities of each student. While there may be several merits to a school uniform mandate, it is essential to acknowledge that the emphasis should be on the development of a positive school culture through means other than uniformity. Encouraging respect, tolerance, and a sense of community can be achieved without sacrificing the autonomy and individuality of students. The school system's priority should be that of an environment that nurtures creativity, embraces diversity, and fosters a sense of belonging, without the unnecessary restriction of uniforms. School uniforms are too restrictive and tend to foster resentment and rebellion among students  Schools should strive to not only uphold the principles of individuality and personal growth but also contribute to the development of well-rounded, confident, and empowered young individuals ready to face the challenges of the world beyond the classroom.

This argument states that the US Vietnam draft is an argument against mandatory national service. I disagree with this statement on the foundation of the definition of the definition of "mandatory national service". This term differs from a "Draft", it could involve things like national construction projects, infrastructure upgrades, and many more community services. Thinking that mandatory national service only requires military service is a flawed thought.  In closing, my opponent made a flawed assumption by pigeon-holing the concept of Mandatory National Service into a military focused role. Furthermore, my opponent did not seem to read my rebuttal, and went on to provide three counter points to points that I never made or wrote. Overall, mandatory service to your country can take many forms, and can have many benefits to the society overall. Such as, personal investment in bettering our country, increasing unity, and directing our tax payer dollars into valuable and much needed community service.

text_initial: After the disaster of the Vietnam War, the United States should never again attempt to have mandatory service for anyone. During that time, young men were forced into service to fight an unnecessary and futile war that did not benefit our nation in any way. It would be foolish for citizens to trust the government not to misuse the draft in this way again. Prior to this conflict, citizens generally had faith that the draft would be used for critical missions, such as World War II. Vietnam saw young men killed, injured, and mentally destroyed for no good reason.
text_all:
After the disaster of the Vietnam War, the United States should never again attempt to have mandatory service for anyone. During that time, young men were forced into service to fight an unnecessary and futile war that did not benefit our nation in any way. It would be foolish for citizens to trust the government not to misuse the draft in this way again. Prior to this conflict, citizens generally had faith that the draft would be used for critical missions, such as World War II. Vietnam saw young men killed, injured, and mentally destroyed for no good reason. The draft days of Vietnam prove all three of these points utterly false.

1. Citizens rallied against our nation and government. There were protests and violence as a result. Our country has never recovered.

2. There was a turning away from service in the military, including young men looking for ways to defer their service or to escape the country and service altogether.

3. Even today, there is a divide between the men who were drafted and the men who were not or managed to defer the draft. They are still called draft dodgers by those who had to go. The only mandatory national service ever implemented in this country is military service. The military is used to carry out infrastructure projects and community service on a voluntary military service basis now. The military would be the established method by which to carry out any mandatory service going forward. It has the infrastructure and procedures in place. It would be naive to assume that mandatory national service would be conducted by any other means than through the military. Plus, it would provide the government with a ready, national military force without having to rely on volunteers anymore. Mandated service will end badly no matter how you attempt to paint it and will be used for force military service.

text_initial: In my personal opinion, the Supreme Court should be maximized so that it would be as efficient as possible. The more efficient the Supreme Court is, the faster issues can be resolved. The more minds that are working in helping the Supreme Court make decisions, the more views and arguments we can hear in order to find solutions and avoid potential problems.
text_all: In my personal opinion, the Supreme Court should be maximized so that it would be as efficient as possible. The more efficient the Supreme Court is, the faster issues can be resolved. The more minds that are working in helping the Supreme Court make decisions, the more views and arguments we can hear in order to find solutions and avoid potential problems. If it is legal, if it is allowed by law to happen, then it should be allowed no matter what. That's the point of laws in the first place. We cannot stop something that is legally allowed just because we don't want the potential result. If the "other side" can do it, then it means "our side" is also allowed to do it. We can all pack the court as much as we want if it is legally allowed and it's up to us to best use of the situation. You can disagree all you want, but if the court says it is allowed, then it is allowed. The law does not cater simply to our individual whims, it doesn't change just because one of us says it should. If packing the court is allowed, then so be it. As long as everybody is being treated equally under the same law, then there's no problem at all. You cannot make your political opponents stop doing something they are legally allowed to do just because you don't want them to do it.

text_initial: I don't think it's fair to pack the Supreme Court. It's obvious that the Democrats are upset that the conservatives on the Supreme Court have made choices that they don't agree with (over-turning Roe v Wade and ruling against affirmative action) and they want to gain the upper hand by expanding the Court. That's manipulative and unfair and sets a dangerous precedent.
text_all: I don't think it's fair to pack the Supreme Court. It's obvious that the Democrats are upset that the conservatives on the Supreme Court have made choices that they don't agree with (over-turning Roe v Wade and ruling against affirmative action) and they want to gain the upper hand by expanding the Court. That's manipulative and unfair and sets a dangerous precedent. I don't agree with this reasoning at all. I don't think there's been a lot of question about how efficient the Supreme Court is at handing down decisions. It's a good excuse for packing the Court, but it's not the main reason that many people would like to see the change made.  Just because something is "legal" doesn't mean that it is necessarily the best thing that can happen. If we do it once, every new administration will be trying to add members to make sure their side has the upper hand. It could get out of control quickly.

text_initial: Elected and appointed government officials should not be paid the minimum wage because they represent the people and if they are paid more they will do better work for us. They should be motivated to do the best job that they can for Americans and what better way to motivate someone than with more money? If they are paid minimum wage they will be too worried about their financial struggles to focus on what they need to get done with their work in my opinion.
text_all: Elected and appointed government officials should not be paid the minimum wage because they represent the people and if they are paid more they will do better work for us. Making them take minimum wage would likely make them more susceptible to taking bribes from rich people so I think this plan could backfire quickly. Minimum wage would not encourage them to have a whole other career and if it did then they would not be focused on making the best decisions for the citizens. This is why we need to focus on making sure that their votes absolutely cannot be bought and special interest money cannot be involved at all. Making elected or appointed government officials take minimum wage will not fix either of those issues and will only make them worse.

text_initial: I do not believe that social media is making people stupid. I believe that as a society we have disincentivized critically engaging with more than headlines and sound bites and unfortunately social media has become the scapegoat for that reality. Social media is the means in which that failure of society is put on display, but it is not the fault of social media that it is endemic within our society.  The failure lies with our society not being able to more quickly adapt, to raise and educate children in new ways that are conducive to contemporary society.
text_all: I do not believe that social media is making people stupid. I believe that as a society we have disincentivized critically engaging with more than headlines and sound bites and unfortunately social media has become the scapegoat for that reality. Social media is the means in which that failure of society is put on display, but it is not the fault of social media that it is endemic within our society.  The failure lies with our society not being able to more quickly adapt, to raise and educate children in new ways that are conducive to contemporary society. I believe in your overarching narrative, but not in the foundation on which it is structured. Social media is certainly more conducive to being intellectually lazy, as you put it, but that does not mean they are stupid. It is very possible to structure a society that takes social media into account while still raising kids to be just as critical and engaged as they ever have been. More information is readily available than there has ever been, we need to motivate children to engage with it critic I don't think I would disagree with some responsibility lying at the feet of the platform holders for as I've said, disincentivizing critical engagement. However, the existence of echo chambers and social media platforms does not in itself make people stupid, it is not inherent to the structure. As it is not a byproduct of that structure, we as a society can find ways to incentivize critical engagement and we will see that filter down through social media.

text_initial: I support making national service required in the United States. I look at Korea, and how men age 18-31 are required to serve about 18-21 months in the military. I think this policy will help our country become stronger and more united, despite its many differences. There are many more benefits of this kind of program than meets the eye. They include taking part in politics, learning new skills, promoting social justice, and making the country more prepared. It leads to a society that is better linked, skilled, and strong. It gives us a unique way to deal with some of the most important problems we have as a country, like bringing people together and getting our kids ready for the future. Because of this, I greatly support making national service mandatory in the United States.\n\n

text_initial: Government surveillance is not necessary for national security, and in fact proves counterproductive.  The greatest threat against America comes from the actions of lone actors, who can evade detection from necessarily broad-focused surveillance technologies.  Moreover, the act of surveilling its own citizens more intensely creates an atmosphere of distrust, itself exacerbating tensions in this country.  I'm also not convinced that America would be skillful at surveilling its citizens to detect threats.  We have not been able to detect complex plots in the past.  Moreover, some agencies, like the CIA, are not allowed to spy on domestic persons, by law.  Lastly, the utility of surveillance would come from its analysis.  Who would sift through mountains of data (much of which the government already has access to)?  How would they make threat determinations?  How could they act preemptively against perceived threats?  I don't see our security apparatus as able to carry out thees missions iwth available resources.
text_all: Government surveillance is not necessary for national security, and in fact proves counterproductive.  The greatest threat against America comes from the actions of lone actors, who can evade detection from necessarily broad-focused surveillance technologies.  Moreover, the act of surveilling its own citizens more intensely creates an atmosphere of distrust, itself exacerbating tensions in this country.  I'm also not convinced that America would be skillful at surveilling its citizens to detect threats.  We have not been able to detect complex plots in the past.  Moreover, some agencies, like the CIA, are not allowed to spy on domestic persons, by law.  Lastly, the utility of surveillance would come from its analysis.  Who would sift through mountains of data (much of which the government already has access to)?  How would they make threat determinations?  How could they act preemptively against perceived threats?  I don't see our security apparatus as able to carry out thees missions iwth available resources. The degree of bipartisan support has no bearing on the effectiveness of surveillance for national security.  Despite supposed differences, the two major political parties have a lot of overlap, and do not always prove wise in their groupthink.  Americans take a dim view of Congress in particular in polls, and bipartisan effort to amplify spying on its own citizens would not give much of an imprimatur.  The FISA courts are effective as a safeguard, but are time consuming and are not suitable for increased activity.  Its interbranch support does not speak to resources.  The American government already has access to massive amounts of data about its citizens.  The problem is twofold.  First, we need to find out how to better analyze the domestic intelligence we have, rather than add more information that will get lost in government agencies.  Second, the government needs to improve the political climate in this country to prevent the actions of lone actors in the first place.  The parties can do more for security by tamping down divisive political rhetoric versus increasing spying activities on its own citizens.  By creating political discourse that is inclusive rather than demonizing, potential domestic threats will decrease on their own.

text_initial: I believe that both Washington, DC. and Puerto Rico should be granted US statehood because giving them full statehood would ensure equal representation and rights for their residents which aligns with the principles of democracy and inclusion which is what the United States is all about. It could also economically benefit the country as a whole. It could boost their economies which would benefit everyone.

It could also economically benefit the country as a whole. It could boost their economies which would benefit everyone. It is important to consider the principle of equal representation and the rights of citizens. D.C has a population that is larger than several states and it's residents deserve the same voting rights and full representation in Congress. Puerto Rico also should have it's voice heard on a national level. The challenges of geographical location should not undermine the democratic rights and potential contributions of these regions to the country.  Granting statehood doesn't mean giving up those traits. D.C can still remain the capital while still giving residents voting rights. Statehood could even boost its economy by accessing more resources. Puerto Rico statehood won't erase its culture or attractions, its about giving residents equal rights. Economic worries can be addressed through smart planning, ensuring both Puerto Rico and the US benefit from this change.

Animals do not have to be subject to painful experiences when research is conducted, as several safety and ethical standards exist to protect them during clinical trials. Conducting research on animals will most accurately tell the scientific community what happens to organisms when X, Y and Z are introduced.
