text_initial: Standardized testing, when implemented correctly and following recommendations from educational and psychological sciences, is an invaluable tool for benchmarking, enriching, and personalizing education. Standardized testing provides a uniform metric by which to assess whether students are learning the content they need to and whether they are developing the soft skills like critical thinking and analytical problem solving to apply the content they've learned to problem solving. Because of this, standardized tests can help schools evaluate progress and identify areas for improvement at the state, district, school, class, classroom, and individual level. If standardized testing is used as this sort of benchmark to proactively adapt educational strategies so that students master the hard and soft skills they need to acquire at each grade level, then it is an invaluable tool for streamlining a student's educational experience and for promotion educational opportunity.
text_all: Standardized testing, when implemented correctly and following recommendations from educational and psychological sciences, is an invaluable tool for benchmarking, enriching, and personalizing education. Standardized testing provides a uniform metric by which to assess whether students are learning the content they need to and whether they are developing the soft skills like critical thinking and analytical problem solving to apply the content they've learned to problem solving. Because of this, standardized tests can help schools evaluate progress and identify areas for improvement at the state, district, school, class, classroom, and individual level. If standardized testing is used as this sort of benchmark to proactively adapt educational strategies so that students master the hard and soft skills they need to acquire at each grade level, then it is an invaluable tool for streamlining a student's educational experience and for promotion educational opportunity. I agree that students should be taught through experiential learning. That type of education allows students to engage in deeper, more effortful/slow cognition that results in better long term learning. This includes setting up students for success by establishing a culture of failing with grace and focusing on the effort to try to learn from our mistakes. Jobs do teach new experiences, but standardized testing can be used to allow students, teachers, and school admin to assess and address pedagogical gaps. This type of personalized experience can shed light on opportunities for growth, recognizing—for instance—where students might not have enough opportunity to learn by trying, failing, and trying again. Standardized tests enable schools to provide BETTER learning experiences. Standardized testing is often implemented with misguided intentions—teachers being evaluated solely based on how students perform, in turn motivating only "teaching to the test." But with a proper implementation, standardized testing COULD be a great tool for directing learning and actually focusing on the kids who NEED the extra attention to make sure they get the opportunity they deserve to be set up for success by being encouraged to keep trying and learning. Standardized testing is a potentially invaluable tool WHEN implemented with careful strategy and on-going reiteration of extant strategies.

text_initial:
The entire point of our school system is to teach things to our students. As a result, some form of testing is required. We need a way to test if these students have actually learned what is being taught in the classroom. We also need to test every student the same way, so that the student themselves is the only variable. For example, if student a has learned 90% of the information and student B has learned 60% of the information it is logical that student a will score higher on a standardized test. We can also use the average score of all the students to answer the question is "the current curriculum an effective way to teach this information?" If almost every student scores poorly on the standardized test, we can conclude that the curriculum is not effective.

So the standardized tests are not only for the students, but also in the classroom and the curriculum itself. If we have an ineffective teaching method or an ineffective lesson plan, we need a way to be aware of that, so corrections can be made.

 What you have said is true. These are valid flaws to a system that has standardized testing and absolutely nothing else. However, I need to point out that the question is not, should standardized testing ALONE in schools be the sole measure of performance. 

I believe that standardized testing is one valid way to measure the effectiveness of a curriculum, as well as the amount of information that the student has retained. But I believe that other methods should be implemented alongside standardized testing that can focus on the importance of critical thinking skills and account for different accessibility needs. I agree these are commonly known flaws about standardized testing. Teaching to the test is a well known flaw. For example, if the instructor knows that the test will only ask, what is 9 + 11 then as long as the student knows what is 9 + 11 it doesn't matter if they know addition at all. However, there are already methods in place known to curtail this issue. One commonly used method is dynamically generated tests, Where the instructor does not know exactly what will be on the test until it is pulled at random. You could simply have 200 questions, but only 50 of them chosen at random will actually be on the test.

This is a vast, oversimplification, but it shows the point that the flaws in standardized testing should be worked around and improved and not simply discarded.

text_initial: Arts Education is as important as science and math in schools because it helps provide students with a well-rounded education overall. Whether it's sculpting, painting, or drawing, art enables people to experiment with new ideas and materials. In addition to improving their artistic skills, this creative freedom also affects other areas of their lives, enabling them to approach problem-solving with an open mind. Students learn basic skills and become more dexterous. Additionally, students read texts and study works of art from all different cultures and perspectives throughout human time on the Earth. Giving students access to these imaginative activities at a young age will aid in their development. Imaginative activities for young learners can lead to better skills in social interactions and emotional regulation. Lessons in the arts introduce K-12 students to problem-solving techniques, which help them to see the world in new ways, and provide access to creative ways of knowing. Children will be more stimulated and exercise their brains better by coloring or making art by hand in comparison to watching a video on an iPad at the dinner table.

text_initial: Implementing tuition-free public college raises significant concerns about financial sustainability and the potential decline in educational quality. It risks imposing a heavy burden on government budgets and taxpayers, potentially leading to higher taxes or diverting funds from other crucial areas. Moreover, the influx of students in tuition-free public institutions could strain resources, overcrowding classrooms, and diminishing the overall quality of education.
text_all: Implementing tuition-free public college raises significant concerns about financial sustainability and the potential decline in educational quality. It risks imposing a heavy burden on government budgets and taxpayers, potentially leading to higher taxes or diverting funds from other crucial areas. Moreover, the influx of students in tuition-free public institutions could strain resources, overcrowding classrooms, and diminishing the overall quality of education. Tuition-free public college promotes social equity by ensuring access to education for all, fostering a skilled workforce that drives economic growth. Concerns about quality and economic strain can be addressed through efficient resource management and progressive funding models, maintaining the value of a college degree while benefiting society at large. While income-driven repayment plans offer a solution for managing student debt, they don't address the fundamental issue of accessibility to higher education. Tuition-free public college, financed through progressive models, not only opens doors for all students regardless of financial background but also represents an investment in a more equitable and prosperous society. This approach, rather than perpetuating the cycle of debt, lays the foundation for a more educated and economically resilient population.

text_all:
There should be standardize testing in schools.

Schools need to continously improve.
    1. Benchmark data is incredibly valuable for determining the effectiveness of schools.
        a. state-to-state comparisons
        b. district-to-district comparisons 
Trends can pinpoint 
     2. teacher training needs
     3. areas of curriculum reform
     4. areas where tartegeted support for students and families would be most valuable
Without comparative data, improvement is less In many successful schools teachers routinely teach beyond the test.

It is true that standardized test data can be and has been misused in this country.
Standardized test data is a tool.  Standards for proper use need to be developed and enforced.

The connection between rote or boring learning is not necessarily true.  Some poorly trained teachers would teach this way without standardized tests.  The relationship between the two is not causal.  Some teachers need better training in flexible engaging lessons. True, no one tool solves every problem.  

Large scale testing helps district, state, and national policy makes make informed decisions.

Report cards are the tool for showing individual student progress.  National testing does not provide daily in-class assessments, homework, chapter quizzes or any other assessment used by qualified teachers.  Standardized testing is not a replacement for qualified teachers and engaged students.

Training is needed to educate people who conflate the two.

text_initial: Abortion should be illegal. Many states have laws that say the fetus is legally a human, and therefore abortion would be murder. The fetus has no say in the operation, unlike live humans who can give their consent (or not) to any medical procedure. Except in cases of rape or incest, the woman had a choice in allowing herself to become pregnant, which is a life-altering decision that carries with it immense responsibility. She should not be able to simply undo that choice because she no longer wishes to carry her baby to term.
text_all: Abortion should be illegal. Many states have laws that say the fetus is legally a human, and therefore abortion would be murder. The fetus has no say in the operation, unlike live humans who can give their consent (or not) to any medical procedure. Except in cases of rape or incest, the woman had a choice in allowing herself to become pregnant, which is a life-altering decision that carries with it immense responsibility. She should not be able to simply undo that choice because she no longer wishes to carry her baby to term. Abortion is not just a female issue, it involves all of us, including the fathers and unborn children who don't have a say in the decision. Individual autonomy is already established - women have the right to the level of medical care they wish to receive, but that right should not be extended to ending the life of an unborn child. The rights of all humans should be respected, live and otherwise, and in the case of an abortion, only one person's rights and wishes are being respected. This is critically unfair to the unborn child. It is well understood that having a child is an expensive, emotional process that demands a lot of a woman during pregnancy. None of these issues are hidden; women know the risks and challenges involved from the start.

text_initial: Hello.  I take the position that there should be mandatory quotas for women in leadership. These statistics over the last 40 years back up my opening statement.  There is a vast need for more diversity and equity when it comes to women women in leadership positions, whether private industry or public sectors.  Mandatory quotas will solve this problem.

text_initial: It's important to have standardized testing for two reasons. The first reason is that it shows schools how students are doing and can use that information to see where their students are overall. The second reason is that it can be used to figure out where more funding needs to go in order to make sure that students who need more help will receive it.
text_all: It's important to have standardized testing for two reasons. The first reason is that it shows schools how students are doing and can use that information to see where their students are overall. The second reason is that it can be used to figure out where more funding needs to go in order to make sure that students who need more help will receive it. Students do not just memorize information and then copy it down on a standardized test. The tests are designed to see what people know in general at that specific point. It's also not meant to test creativity. I think that the issue with this statement is a lack of understanding the purpose of standardized testing itself. It's there to just show schools what a student has learned so that information can be used to make important decisions, from placement to how to allocate funds.  Standardized testing is important because it tells schools how well students are doing and lets them know what areas need work and where further funding is needed. This information is important because without it, schools are blindly teaching without knowing if anything is successful or a failure.

text_initial: I think it's on all of us to really consider what death means, and what the implications are on such a penalty. It's no simple thing to enforce a means to judgment on killing another under some moral or non-moral pretense. If we want such a penalty, we'd have to really think on what it means to kill another, or at what point it ought to be justified in killing another, solely down to the legal system in some way. We can't have any major "what ifs" or etc. in place when deciding this, as this would render the whole policy of the default death judgment to be still on shaky or unconvincing ground. If we want an assured death penalty we'd have to really think about who gets it, why, and what it really means to have such a system controlled, enforced, or otherwise in the hands of some federal body.

text_initial: The United States has governing parties that control on the town level, the state level, and on the national level. Laws are in some ways based on geographic location--laws are created to fits the needs of people based on where they are located. The internet is obviously not a physical location. The laws that pertain to US citizens are not applicable in the online space--it is an entirely different realm.\n\nWhen a person or entity posts online, creates a website, or engages with content, their actions do not fall under the dominion of US law. Stating your opinion online is a personal act that is outside the realm of the US ju
text_all: The United States has governing parties that control on the town level, the state level, and on the national level. Laws are in some ways based on geographic location--laws are created to fits the needs of people based on where they are located. The internet is obviously not a physical location. The laws that pertain to US citizens are not applicable in the online space--it is an entirely different realm.\n\nWhen a person or entity posts online, creates a website, or engages with content, their actions do not fall under the dominion of US law. Stating your opinion online is a personal act that is outside the realm of the US ju The US government has no ownership over online domains. Posting information to the World of Tanks forum is akin to having a conversation in the middle of the ocean--there is no jurisdiction that should control the conversation. There are many complications that arise with ascribing the words of an avatar to an actual person. Producing evidence that a certain person did indeed share government secrets could be impossible. An online persona can't be tied to any one person with certainty.

text_initial: Space exploration is a worthwhile investment for mankind. For one thing, the investment doesn't cost nearly as much as people think it does. Only .3% of the spending budget was allocated to NASA in 2020 and with that miniscule amount, progress has been made. In addition, investing in space exploration has a positive effect on the economy, providing many jobs for many Americans. Furthermore space exploration may end up having a positive effect on humanity should problems ever escalate on Earth to the point of inhabitation.
text_all: Space exploration is a worthwhile investment for mankind. For one thing, the investment doesn't cost nearly as much as people think it does. Only .3% of the spending budget was allocated to NASA in 2020 and with that miniscule amount, progress has been made. In addition, investing in space exploration has a positive effect on the economy, providing many jobs for many Americans. Furthermore space exploration may end up having a positive effect on humanity should problems ever escalate on Earth to the point of inhabitation.  It's possible to have an interest in space exploration as well as talk about issues on Earth. You could contend that space exploration is a last resort if things on Earth ever make it uninhabitable.  My opponent here has the idea that defunding the space program will somehow cause other problems on Earth to be solved, when in actuality with such a small amount of funds allocated towards space travel, would it make a difference? As I mentioned before, you can have an interest in space exploration while still diverting resources to Earth on more pressing matters. This is not an all or nothing argument, so I agree with my opponent on that argument.

text_initial: Some contend that the emphasis on space exploration reflects a skewed perspective, diverting attention away from the planet's urgent issues. The argument is that humanity should prioritize understanding and resolving problems on Earth before venturing into space so as not to regret.
text_all: Some contend that the emphasis on space exploration reflects a skewed perspective, diverting attention away from the planet's urgent issues. The argument is that humanity should prioritize understanding and resolving problems on Earth before venturing into space so as not to regret. While my opponent contends that space exploration represents only a small fraction of the spending budget, critics argue that even a small percentage amounts to significant absolute figures. The resources allocated to space exploration could be redirected to address more immediate and widespread issues on Earth, such as poverty, healthcare, and education. It's crucial to weigh the benefits of space exploration against the potential gains from addressing terrestrial challenges directly. From my perspective The opportunity costs associated with space exploration are significant. Critics contend that the resources, talent, and technology invested in space missions could be directed toward solving pressing issues on Earth, potentially achieving more immediate and tangible results that benefit a larger portion of the global population. I believe that While it's true that individuals can have interests in both space exploration and Earthly issues, the question remains about resource allocation. Critics argue that finite resources, including financial, technological, and human capital, should be judiciously distributed to maximize their impact. Prioritizing space exploration as a last resort might divert resources that could address pressing issues on Earth more effectively.

text_initial: Climate change is real. Up to 97 percent of the scientists agreed on this phenomenon. Fossil fuels contribute a major chunk of global carbon emissions. It is thus logical that we phase them out quickly. This can only be done via a ban. Since this fall at the national level, we can only enact a ban on fossil fuels in the U.S., which, ironically, is still the biggest per-capita polluter.  Gasoline, coal and other fuels need to be replaced by clean energy, including nuclear. The ban won't come immediately though as we need to give a grace period of five years or so. Other countries can follow the American example and this will have a net positive impact on the global climate.
text_all: Climate change is real. Up to 97 percent of the scientists agreed on this phenomenon. Fossil fuels contribute a major chunk of global carbon emissions. It is thus logical that we phase them out quickly. This can only be done via a ban. Since this fall at the national level, we can only enact a ban on fossil fuels in the U.S., which, ironically, is still the biggest per-capita polluter.  Gasoline, coal and other fuels need to be replaced by clean energy, including nuclear. The ban won't come immediately though as we need to give a grace period of five years or so. Other countries can follow the American example and this will have a net positive impact on the global climate. The gradual transition argument is cogent but lacks cognition of the pressing issue. The transition can continue for decades without any tangible improvement in climate metrics. While an immediate ban is not feasible either, we should ban fossil fuels starting 2030. Any further delay can bring climate disasters, which we are already seeing today. But not doing anything, or using gradual measures, also has a major downside. We are already witnessing climate disasters like unusual flooding, droughts and fires. We need to do this urgently and a set deadline will make us work extra hard to achieve the targets.

text_initial: I think being an elected official is a very difficult job to have. As such, I think that they should be rewarded for their efforts. I also think minimum wage is not enough to survive with today's costs and everyone should be paid above minimum wage, even the jobs that require lesser skill to accomplish.
text_all: I think being an elected official is a very difficult job to have. As such, I think that they should be rewarded for their efforts. I also think minimum wage is not enough to survive with today's costs and everyone should be paid above minimum wage, even the jobs that require lesser skill to accomplish.  This is a selfless focused job, but they still have to be able to provide for their families and themselves. This isn't an easy job, so they should make more than minimum wage for their required efforts. If we do not offer enough pay for the position, we're likely to get less than qualified people to get the job done. Anyone who makes minimum wage from a job with a family knows how hard it is to make ends meet with low wages. I do not think an elected official should barely make enough money to survive when the job requires such a high skill set from all individuals.

text_initial: Hi, I think the Electoral College works because it worked in the past. I also think it is a good way to ensure democracy has been implemented relatively for the country. It offers a way to select a president fairly so that no one can interfere with the process. It makes both parties agree on the result without ever fighting the consequences. So far, it is a fair process that works for the political system. That is why I think the US should keep using the Electoral College in Presidential Elections and continue using it.
text_all: Hi, I think the Electoral College works because it worked in the past. I also think it is a good way to ensure democracy has been implemented relatively for the country. It offers a way to select a president fairly so that no one can interfere with the process. It makes both parties agree on the result without ever fighting the consequences. So far, it is a fair process that works for the political system. That is why I think the US should keep using the Electoral College in Presidential Elections and continue using it. The US should not use the Electoral College in presidential elections because it offers no democratic but secret way to select a president without consensus. It provides no transparency but hidden information from the public. It is unfair to choose a president away from the public. It offers the opportunity to manipulate the process without notice from the public. It is a dangerous way to elect a president. It is unfair and unequal to the general public.  I think you might be wrong about the fair process. It is fair because of the process itself. It does not mean the system is outdated, but it works because everyone agrees with the result. It is fair because both parties agreed in the end, which is the most important thing about the election result.

text_initial: i believe the elctoral college needs to be abolished. it is dumb that we dont decide elections by popular vote instead of giving disproportiate numbers of votes to certain states. the system is rigged. it is not a true democracy if the majority of the peoples voice is not heard.
text_all: i believe the elctoral college needs to be abolished. it is dumb that we dont decide elections by popular vote instead of giving disproportiate numbers of votes to certain states. the system is rigged. it is not a true democracy if the majority of the peoples voice is not heard. you have not given any good reasons why you think its a fair process. the majority of people disagree with you in this country as evidenced by the difference in the popular vote and the electoral vote in the past 3 elections. the system is outdated and the district lines make no sense given the growth in infrastucture since they were created. you are wrong, and furthermore being dishonest or critically misinformed about the matter. youre wrong and probably a coservative. you let racist views cloud your judgement. the system is rigged so that red states can remain relevant dispite the huge discrepany in the number of democrats and republicans in this country. the only reason you support this system is because it benefits your party. i am not going to continue this back and forth with you if you insist on arguing in bad faith.

text_initial: I agree with the idea of student uniforms, especially from grades 6-12. I believe this is a preparatory item regarding when someone will eventually enter the workforce in the future, and what will be required of them when they work for an employer. It also provides for insight on how someone should behave when under a particular authority at particular times. It should also define how the work versus personal life boundaries are also defined.
text_all: I agree with the idea of student uniforms, especially from grades 6-12. I believe this is a preparatory item regarding when someone will eventually enter the workforce in the future, and what will be required of them when they work for an employer. It also provides for insight on how someone should behave when under a particular authority at particular times. It should also define how the work versus personal life boundaries are also defined.  There will always be controls in place in life. That is not necessarily a suppression of individuality, it is simply that individuality cannot always be expressed at all times. Freedom still is very much not suppressed, opinions and civil opinion is always available to be shared withing certain contexts.  How is when having controls undermining diversity or authenticity? They always exist, but that doesn't mean one can do whatever one wants at any given moment. If everyone can do whatever they want at any moment, then how will anything get actually done in life and work? The end always surmounts to chaos in any society.

text_initial: AI is taking away work from a wide variety of jobs. It's stealing original work from artists and writers. It's not good for society for art and other creative work to be taken away from humans. AI is also being used to cheat at assignments in school which is a major issue. We need to support human intelligence and creativity. AI is pushing us away from that and it's going to lead to unhappiness. There are also major privacy issues with AI as it currently stands.
text_all: AI is taking away work from a wide variety of jobs. It's stealing original work from artists and writers. It's not good for society for art and other creative work to be taken away from humans. AI is also being used to cheat at assignments in school which is a major issue. We need to support human intelligence and creativity. AI is pushing us away from that and it's going to lead to unhappiness. There are also major privacy issues with AI as it currently stands. Quality of life will plummet when people are put out of work thanks to AI and when AI takes over art and other creative endeavors. Supporting human creativity and intelligence will always lead to better and more innovative ideas. We can't count on AI to solve pressing issues like climate change. We're currently seeing AI taking over creative fields with those jobs being cut. This is the opposite of what should be happening and is lowering quality of life and appreciation of human intelligence and creativity. In education, we should be focusing on supporting teachers and on much needed reform instead of pawning our kids off on AI. It's unlikely that privacy issues will be properly addressed as that is simply not how politics and regulation realistically works. Because of that, it isn't reasonable to plan for an ideal scenario that won't happen.

text_initial: Women have been historically, systemically discriminated against in nearly all industries and especially in leadership positions. In many major corporations there are no women at all in any of the top leadership positions. This is morally wrong and it is also a bad way of doing business. A more diverse leadership team allows for different perspectives that can lead to innovation and new ideas. Although many companies claim to have anti-sexism policies, in practice their leadership is still predominantly male, especially in certain industries like technology, government, and medicine. It's not enough to pay lip service to the idea. There need to be mandatory quotes to ensure that sexism ends for good.
text_all: Women have been historically, systemically discriminated against in nearly all industries and especially in leadership positions. In many major corporations there are no women at all in any of the top leadership positions. This is morally wrong and it is also a bad way of doing business. A more diverse leadership team allows for different perspectives that can lead to innovation and new ideas. Although many companies claim to have anti-sexism policies, in practice their leadership is still predominantly male, especially in certain industries like technology, government, and medicine. It's not enough to pay lip service to the idea. There need to be mandatory quotes to ensure that sexism ends for good. Given the dearth of women in leadership positions, it's clear that special measures ARE needed for women to get a fair shot. Many women are qualified for positions but don't get that chance to succeed because of systemic sexism. I absolutely agree that individuals must be qualified to get leadership positions, but I think we have to take a broader view of the context of someone's credentials, experience, and talent. If only men are promoted, then only men will ever be qualified. It's a systemic problem that we must address at all levels. Anti-sexism education and policies are important. But diversity training and lip service is not enough. Many companies do these things and still have no women in leadership (see the tech industry for one good example). Recommendations and gentle nudges are not enough. We have to hold companies to their ideals and make it a mandatory requirement for women to be represented.

text_initial: This is a delicate time in U.S. foreign policy history. In this moment, we must stand up and support Ukraine in any way possible. Not only has Russia encroached upon Ukranian territory previously without any backlash, allowing them to conquer Ukraine would be a major US foreign policy disaster. Appeasement is a strategy that has consistently failed and it is only through helping Ukraine defeat Russia that we can establish order in the international stage.
text_all: This is a delicate time in U.S. foreign policy history. In this moment, we must stand up and support Ukraine in any way possible. Not only has Russia encroached upon Ukranian territory previously without any backlash, allowing them to conquer Ukraine would be a major US foreign policy disaster. Appeasement is a strategy that has consistently failed and it is only through helping Ukraine defeat Russia that we can establish order in the international stage. You assume that these measures are not being taken and they do not have to be mutually exclusive. You can still engage in diplomatic methods even though you are supporting someone with financial and military might backing. Russia would not hesitate to do the same so why should the United States play by Russia's rules? Although a diplomatic option would be ideal, there is little chance of that occurring. To prevent Russia from further taking land away from a sovereign nation, the United States should continue in its support of Ukraine through monetary and military methods. Only by punishing the actions of others will we be able to keep order on an international level.

text_initial: I think the US could benefit in the future from helping Ukraine with military aid. Only if Ukraine is an ally to the US. They should.
text_all: I think the US could benefit in the future from helping Ukraine with military aid. Only if Ukraine is an ally to the US. They should. It would cost must for the US to lend a helping hand to Ukraine. It would be looked upon as positive deed in the future. In case the US needed assistance in the future they will possibly have someone to turn too. The consequences of providing military aid would be very minimal if any at all. There is a significant amount of aid available for both the US as well as other countries in need. Considering the US isn't independent on itself alone.

text_initial: It is simply wrong to purposefully inflict pain and suffering on other living creatures. Science has advanced to the point that experiments on living subjects are not as necessary as they were just a few decades ago. We don't need to hurt animals to see if a treatment or product is effective or safe.  It is not worth the moral cost to get a little gain in science, when it isn't entirely necessary.
text_all: It is simply wrong to purposefully inflict pain and suffering on other living creatures. Science has advanced to the point that experiments on living subjects are not as necessary as they were just a few decades ago. We don't need to hurt animals to see if a treatment or product is effective or safe.  It is not worth the moral cost to get a little gain in science, when it isn't entirely necessary. Science has advanced to the point that animal trials are not necessary any more. New advances, like the MRNA vaccines you mention, do not need animal trials to determine either efficacy or safety. For every lab that maintains the "strict ethical guidelines" that you mention, there are probably 10 more that don't, and cause horrible suffering. Science has indeed advanced to the point that animal trials are not necessary, and the only regulation we should have is the regulation banning the practice. Cruelty might have been advantageous in the past, but time, and science, have evolved past that point.

text_initial: Social Media has made people more stupid than in the past.  People get involved in echo chambers instead of seeing out differing views and opinions.  In fact, social media has given a voice to idiots and fools that is as loud as those of experts, and for some people, it is difficult to discern who is right, especially when they choose who they want to be right, rather than who is actually right.  For example, there are some people now who believe that the earth is flat.  A generation ago, virtually no one believed this, but social media spread this thought and people want to be "in the know" and want to show that they are smarter than the rest of the people, and instead wind up showing they are dumber.  \n\nTo close, social media gives equal time to both morons and experts, and users often don't take the time to differentiate between the two, which leads to more people being led down false pathways by idiots than ever before.
text_all: Social Media has made people more stupid than in the past.  People get involved in echo chambers instead of seeing out differing views and opinions.  In fact, social media has given a voice to idiots and fools that is as loud as those of experts, and for some people, it is difficult to discern who is right, especially when they choose who they want to be right, rather than who is actually right.  For example, there are some people now who believe that the earth is flat.  A generation ago, virtually no one believed this, but social media spread this thought and people want to be "in the know" and want to show that they are smarter than the rest of the people, and instead wind up showing they are dumber.  \n\nTo close, social media gives equal time to both morons and experts, and users often don't take the time to differentiate between the two, which leads to more people being led down false pathways by idiots than ever before. Evidence shows this not to be the case.  In fact, using my example of people believing in all sorts of crazy conspiracy theories these days shows that people hear what they want to hear and don't listen to everything they should listen to.  Social media also reinforces belief systems by using algorithms that make you more likely to hear viewpoints that coincide with your own instead of being challenged by opposing viewpoints.  So it narrows your worldview as opposed to informing you. The fact is that people are not taught how to discern good information from bad.  So they turn to social media not to be informed, but to have their worldview reinforced.  The very fact that we are dealing with issues that have long been settled which now are seemingly open to debate (like flat earth) shows that people are willingly becoming more stupid by their usage of social media.

text_initial: There are certain instances where felons should morally be given a right to vote. These instances are few and far between, but they definitely are there. Some felons have a change of heart and even convert to faith during their imprisonment. These individuals are then not the same that they were before, and are able to contribute in a meaningful way to community again. There are programs that provide training and counseling in prisons that rehabilitate prisoners to begin a new life that is vastly different than the one that they led before. Finally, there are a very few felons that may have committed a felony for a good reason, (i.e. to protect a family member or to prevent a catastrophe). In those cases, the individual's ability to vote in a beneficial manner has never been violated.
text_all: There are certain instances where felons should morally be given a right to vote. These instances are few and far between, but they definitely are there. Some felons have a change of heart and even convert to faith during their imprisonment. These individuals are then not the same that they were before, and are able to contribute in a meaningful way to community again. There are programs that provide training and counseling in prisons that rehabilitate prisoners to begin a new life that is vastly different than the one that they led before. Finally, there are a very few felons that may have committed a felony for a good reason, (i.e. to protect a family member or to prevent a catastrophe). In those cases, the individual's ability to vote in a beneficial manner has never been violated.  I honestly agree wholeheartedly with you for most felons. It is for the few that do change in prison (i.e. convert to faith and/or learn skills and get counseling to improve) that I argue in favor of. These few deserve a second chance in life, just as we all would hope for. There are also a very few that may have committed a crime for a good reason, for instance to protect a mother or to prevent a crime. These may not have ever become violated in their conscience to begin with and therefore are still able to contribute to society in a meaningful way. All humans mess up from time to time. Some much worse than others. Some are evil to the core, but not all. There are definitely those who have gotten tied up with the wrong crowd, grown up in extenuating circumstances, and never were given a chance in life. I would hope that there would be forgiveness and a second chance for those who have a change of heart and are trying to live a new, good life.

text_initial: Said person committed a crime and even if and when they are released they have lost that right to vote. They did something that sent them to prison and it was something bad enough they lost the right to help decide what should happen in the community and country they live in.
text_all: Said person committed a crime and even if and when they are released they have lost that right to vote. They did something that sent them to prison and it was something bad enough they lost the right to help decide what should happen in the community and country they live in.  I do not buy a change of heart and convert of faith to me is a massive copout. I do agree that some might have committed a crime to protect family, if that was true they would have been found not guilty. That point does not hold up to me.  I still do not agree. I believe that once you have does something that bad and are sent to prison for whatever the reason you have lost that right to vote.  Yes, some might change. That is the price that needs to be paid so that we have a just society (if you even want to say we have one now).

text_initial: Uniforms are simple for every student to wear, are all consistent and no student has to worry about whether or not they have the expensive clothes.  Students also don't have to worry about picking out their clothes for the next day so they are more likely to be on time to school for learning and not rushed out the door.
text_all: Uniforms are simple for every student to wear, are all consistent and no student has to worry about whether or not they have the expensive clothes.  Students also don't have to worry about picking out their clothes for the next day so they are more likely to be on time to school for learning and not rushed out the door. The schools should have an allotment for uniforms that are provided.  Also, the colors of the uniform don't have to be lighter colored or they can have multiple options for school colors so stains/dirt don't show up as often.  Also, even without uniforms, the parents need to do laundry and take care of that, dirty clothes are not just a uniform issue.  There will be plenty of options for shirts/pants to accommodate different body types, and the school districts can work with local retailers and or fashion companies to make sure these options are available to everyone.  Clothes, whether they are uniforms or not need upkeep.  Just like with free lunch programs, if some family can't afford the uniforms, there should be financial help for that.  Students get teased if they don't have Nike, Adidas or other name brand clothes.  Students also take forever picking out their clothes to impress their friends causing them to lose valuable hours a week to focus on home work, study and sleep.

text_initial: Students should not have to wear school uniforms. Although this is a well-intentioned gesture it can also backfire. First of all, there is the expense of the uniforms. There is also the day-to-day upkeep of the uniforms. Parents who are of limited means may find it burdensome to not only have to buy the uniforms but also to have to launder them and otherwise keep them clean and spotless. Also, these days, there is pervasive childhood overweight and obesity, and uniforms may not be flattering for all body types. This is an unfortunate reality. Some students may feel very awkward and uncomfortable in uniforms for this, and for other reasons. This may have a deleterious effect on their concentration and focus on schoolwork. School uniforms may have been beneficial in bygone days, but they may cause more problems than they attempt to solve.
text_all: Students should not have to wear school uniforms. Although this is a well-intentioned gesture it can also backfire. First of all, there is the expense of the uniforms. There is also the day-to-day upkeep of the uniforms. Parents who are of limited means may find it burdensome to not only have to buy the uniforms but also to have to launder them and otherwise keep them clean and spotless. Also, these days, there is pervasive childhood overweight and obesity, and uniforms may not be flattering for all body types. This is an unfortunate reality. Some students may feel very awkward and uncomfortable in uniforms for this, and for other reasons. This may have a deleterious effect on their concentration and focus on schoolwork. School uniforms may have been beneficial in bygone days, but they may cause more problems than they attempt to solve.  Uniforms may not be "simple for every student to wear" for the simple reason that not all students' parents will be able to afford the uniforms and the upkeep. These uniforms may not be flattering or suitable for all body types, and students who find themselves in this plight may suffer because they may be teased or bullied. They may also feel self-conscious and not able to focus on school work. Unfortunately, these days, we are not in a one-size-fits-all situation and uniforms are not a solution. Schools might now have an "allotment," as budging for education is often quite restrictive. Students' parents will already be encumbered with many tasks, not least of which is getting their children to focus on the schoolwork and homework. Trying to cope with buying and upkeep of uniforms adds an additional burden. While it is a well-meaning gesture to have unforms, the problems will outweigh the benefits. And again, I reiterate, the focus needs to be on learning.

Unless this work can be done humanely or with human volunteers, it is morally wrong to continue.  There is an awakening concerning this topic.  The brutality must end if we are to be
text_all: Animals are sentient beings and are fully emotionally and physically impacted by scientific testing and experimental usage for human benefit.  It is abusive and immoral to use them in this way.  We are lesser beings for exploiting them for our gains.  Unless this work can be done humanely or with human volunteers, it is morally wrong to continue.  There is an awakening concerning this topic.  The brutality must end if we are to be  If an experiment can result in death to a human, it can to an animal as well.  Why does an animal owe its life to a human experiment?  It does not.  Human moral code dictates better behavior.  Genetic similarity isn’t a given and AI models can now be developed to sub in for animal testing.  These would be superior and end this abuse. Being a human comes with a set of moral obligations.  Preserving life — both human and animal — is part of an instinct we should know as humans.  In this age of technology and innovation, better ways exist to learn and advance scientifically than cruelly experimentation on animals.  Time to move forward another way.

text_initial: Colleges should consider race as a factor in admission because we need to level the playing field.  Students who have not done well in high school still deserve a chance to go to college and make something of themselves.
text_all: Colleges should consider race as a factor in admission because we need to level the playing field.  Students who have not done well in high school still deserve a chance to go to college and make something of themselves. text_initial: The US should definitely ban fossil fuels to combat climate change.  The longer we make excuses not to do so, the more irreversible damage is being done to the planet.  Fossil fuels and their respective industries are making massive profits at the expense of our planet and its current and future generations.  This transformation would bring valuable jobs to the economy and put us on a path to a cleaner energy future.  It's time to stop making excuses and do what needs to be done.
text_all: The US should definitely ban fossil fuels to combat climate change.  The longer we make excuses not to do so, the more irreversible damage is being done to the planet.  Fossil fuels and their respective industries are making massive profits at the expense of our planet and its current and future generations.  This transformation would bring valuable jobs to the economy and put us on a path to a cleaner energy future.  It's time to stop making excuses and do what needs to be done.   While climate change is caused by many factors, fossil fuels have been proven to be the primary cause.  The transformation as well as the jobs created make this a no brainer.  Manufacturing and transportation are ready for the change and should not put it off any longer The logistics of change are never easy.  There are costs involved in making big changes for sure.  However, we can create jobs and make the changes advantageous as we move towards a cleaner and better future.  Our innovations in recent decades with technology don't only prove our capability but can also be instrumental in helping us on a path to a better future.

text_initial: Fossil fuels have been blamed for climate change but I think they share the blame with other factors. I think there are other ways to combat climate change.
text_all: Fossil fuels have been blamed for climate change but I think they share the blame with other factors. I think there are other ways to combat climate change. text_initial: The government must have broad surveillance powers in order to defend the country from threats.  Without proper intelligence, it will be unable to deter or prevent large scale tragedy.  This does not imply that large-scale domestic surveillance is required.  There are myriad external threats that justify the use of surveillance powers.  Other countries have sophisticated systems in place that allow them to do with the same.  Without similar powers, our government would be left behind or be left dependent on the shared resources that other countries allow us to access?  This could leave the country vulnerable to any number of political and military threats.

However, and as you have acknowledged, national security goals can not be met or established without the tools to do so.  Surveillance, targeted to the proper threats, is imperative in manifesting a  Again, you and I seem to agree.  Surveillance is necessary for national security, although it must be supervised and targeted to avoid injustices against those that are properly exercising their rights.  Without any surveillance, national security will be compromised.  Without supervised and cautious surveillance, individual freedom will be compromised.  Thus, even individuals require a patient and empathetic surveillance to remain safe within

text_initial: College should be completely based on merit, right? Well let's consider what this statement means. Is merit being born into a family that can afford to move to an area with good education? Does merit include being tracked into classes that lead to college admission? Do you consider it merit that one can apply for prestigious colleges only if they have the money to pay for the application fee? There is much more to this train of reasoning than first appears. Deep seeded racial biases, historical and structural racism, and barriers to education lead to the inability to merely use "merit" as a accurate measure for college admissions. The ground is not level from the beginning and if diversity is to be a value in the U.S. and in any college, there must be a consideration of race in the admissions process of higher education.
text_all: College should be completely based on merit, right? Well let's consider what this statement means. Is merit being born into a family that can afford to move to an area with good education? Does merit include being tracked into classes that lead to college admission? Do you consider it merit that one can apply for prestigious colleges only if they have the money to pay for the application fee? There is much more to this train of reasoning than first appears. Deep seeded racial biases, historical and structural racism, and barriers to education lead to the inability to merely use "merit" as a accurate measure for college admissions. The ground is not level from the beginning and if diversity is to be a value in the U.S. and in any college, there must be a consideration of race in the admissions process of higher education.  Diversity of thought is certainly a noble cause, but how much diversity of thought comes from a college system based on this so called "merit"? Consider the factors needed to get into college- a high school degree, technology to apply, extracurricular activities, money to pay for the college. These factors immediately eliminate an entire class and status of people from the college campuses across America. Should this be happening? Of course not. And in a perfect world, complete merit might be an option. But the diversity of thought you talk of is flawed from the beginning. While there are very valid thoughts and concerns with focusing on community programs, financial aid, and scholarships, the reality is that these are not enough to fix the stratification that is already happening. Students attending college should be able to learn from those who are vastly different from them. Right now, the college admissions process produces a cookie cutter student. Diversity in higher education is not just a vague goal but should be an integral part. Every college student learns that we learn the most from those who are different than us, but each student sitting in the class is the same, how is that supposed to play out in the classroom?

text_initial: I believe students should wear school uniforms because it promotes inclusivity and unity in schools.  School uniforms typically go on sale at stores and are fairly affordable.  Most school districts allow parents to donate uniforms so that families who cannot afford them can provide uniforms to their children.  Having students wear school uniforms can also help bridge the socioeconomic gap to where no one is judging one another by how they dress or if someone is wearing a brand name versus a lower priced brand.
text_all: I believe students should wear school uniforms because it promotes inclusivity and unity in schools.  School uniforms typically go on sale at stores and are fairly affordable.  Most school districts allow parents to donate uniforms so that families who cannot afford them can provide uniforms to their children.  Having students wear school uniforms can also help bridge the socioeconomic gap to where no one is judging one another by how they dress or if someone is wearing a brand name versus a lower priced brand. I disagree because school uniforms tend to be very affordable and most stores put these items at sale prices for back to school.  On the other hand, regular clothes have become very expensive.  Most students will typically prefer name brand clothing.  Low income families have the option to call their school district and inquire if there are school uniforms that have been donated by other families.  Families also save on time and students don't have to worry about what to wear for school every morning. As I mentioned before, school polos tend to sell for under ten dollars and are available at most stores.  The reality is that there will always be families who need extra help and whether they need to purchase "regular" clothes or school uniforms, something will need to be purchased.  Regular clothes tend to be pricier versus school uniforms.  I don't think there is anything wrong with accepting donations.  Help is always available from what I have seen at several school districts.

text_initial: I think abortion should be legal because a woman should always have bodily autonomy. No one else is obliged to use her body without her permission. Oftentimes, pro life arguments will be in accordance to someone's religious beliefs, however in The Bible it says life begins at first breath. That means life does not begin until the baby is actually born. However, late term abortions should only be performed if there is risk to the mother or the baby is not viable. Otherwise abortions in the first trimester should be legal.
text_all: I think abortion should be legal because a woman should always have bodily autonomy. No one else is obliged to use her body without her permission. Oftentimes, pro life arguments will be in accordance to someone's religious beliefs, however in The Bible it says life begins at first breath. That means life does not begin until the baby is actually born. However, late term abortions should only be performed if there is risk to the mother or the baby is not viable. Otherwise abortions in the first trimester should be legal. A fetus is not a child. In the first few weeks of development, it is a clump of cells smaller than that of a housefly. The "heartbeat" is a pulsating vein which is not a fully formed heart. A woman has the right to end a pregnancy if it interferes with her bodily autonomy and life does not begin until first breath as a fetus it not a child. A person is not obliged to give their body to someone else. Just as we have to have permission beforehand from a deceased person to donate their organs, a woman's body should be required to have permission to use it. Nor is a woman required to use her body to breastfeed after a baby has been born as it is her body.

text_initial:
The wording is odd. As I understand it I'm arguing that abortion should NOT be legal.

I have a strong moral belief - it is integral to my well-being to maintain it - that abortion is the murder of an innocent child. Regardless of the reason, a life has been created. It is against all we hold good and right that nobody has the right to end someone else's life. Once sperm meets egg, someone else has been created and that person has a life. Nobody has the right to take that away.

This life may not be aware of its surroundings, perhaps cannot perceive anything or feel emotion, but that doesn't matter. It's alive, and that life belongs to it, not you.

Since I believe it's murder, the killing of an unborn person, I don't think you can persuade me by describing the wants and needs of other people, parents or not. You can't kill innocent people, and you can't convince me this isn't an alive person.
text_all:
The wording is odd. As I understand it I'm arguing that abortion should NOT be legal.

I have a strong moral belief - it is integral to my well-being to maintain it - that abortion is the murder of an innocent child. Regardless of the reason, a life has been created. It is against all we hold good and right that nobody has the right to end someone else's life. Once sperm meets egg, someone else has been created and that person has a life. Nobody has the right to take that away.

This life may not be aware of its surroundings, perhaps cannot perceive anything or feel emotion, but that doesn't matter. It's alive, and that life belongs to it, not you.

Since I believe it's murder, the killing of an unborn person, I don't think you can persuade me by describing the wants and needs of other people, parents or not. You can't kill innocent people, and you can't convince me this isn't an alive person. The unborn child has a body, even if that body is just a couple of cells, and that unborn child should have bodily autonomy as well, not just the pregnant person. No one else is obliged to use their body without their permission, either. Stalemate.

The bible says life begins at first breath, the bible describes how to do an abortion, the bible says not to wear clothing of mixed fibers, that drowning everyone on earth was a good idea. The bible says a lot of things and none should be at play here.

You're killing an unborn child whether it's the first, second, or third trimester, whether or not there are complications in the pregnancy, regardless of any of that. N You can declare a fetus not to be a child and I can declare a fetus to be a life. Where's that get us? How does the size of a clump of cells matter? I contend that this is the life of an innocent we're talking about here, regardless of the development of a body.

The "heartbeat" is a *different* thing that indicates life, not this pulsing vein that also indicates life, those are different? 

A woman, or anyone else, has the rights that we decide they have. The alive person inside that woman has the rights we decide they have.

"Life does not begin until first breath" is just a statement, not any kind of scientific fact. You don't get to choose when life begins.

Making a US 'territory' a state gives the citizens the respect and rights they deserve and would strengthen our country in a variety of positive ways. Equal rights equals statehood

text_initial: Making public college tuition-free may sound great, but it comes with some downsides. First off, it's expensive, and that bill usually lands on taxpayers, which isn't fair to those who didn't attend college or already paid off their loans. Plus, it might lead to overcrowded classrooms and a dip in the quality of education. Critics say it doesn't fix the real issues causing high education costs and might not help those who need financial aid the most. So, while the idea seems generous, it's not as simple as it sounds and could have some unintended consequences.\n
text_all: Making public college tuition-free may sound great, but it comes with some downsides. First off, it's expensive, and that bill usually lands on taxpayers, which isn't fair to those who didn't attend college or already paid off their loans. Plus, it might lead to overcrowded classrooms and a dip in the quality of education. Critics say it doesn't fix the real issues causing high education costs and might not help those who need financial aid the most. So, while the idea seems generous, it's not as simple as it sounds and could have some unintended consequences.\n While it sounds great to make public college free, it's like saying, "Let's have a massive party, and someone else will pick up the tab!" The truth is, making college free is super expensive, and that usually means taxpayers foot the bill. It might also lead to too many people wanting to join the party, making classes crowded and possibly lowering the quality of education. Instead of a one-size-fits-all free tuition, it makes more sense to figure out why college costs so much and help out those who really need the financial help, rather than making everyone pay for a free-for-all.\n While the idea of tuition-free public college may seem like an investment in our nation's future, the reality is that such a move poses significant financial challenges. Funding education on a massive scale requires a substantial amount of resources, and expecting taxpayers to foot the bill raises questions of fairness, particularly for those who may not have attended college or have already paid off their student loans. Moreover, the potential surge in demand for higher education could strain resources, compromising the quality of education that students currently enjoy. Instead of a blanket approach to free tuition, we should focus on addressing the root causes of rising education costs and ensuring that resources are targeted efficiently to support those who truly need financial assistance, rather than burdening everyone with the costs of universal free education.

text_initial:
There should be international agreements to enforce climate change. 
As we all know, climate change is a global issue affecting all countries.
Therefore, there have to be some agreements about changes that have to be met to reduce climate change. 
There should be a shared responsibility by all countries and regions of the world, as one or two regions can't do everything.
If someone doesn't follow the agreements, then they are benefiting from the work and money that other countries have put in. 
Countries should be held accountable for their actions.
This will all lead to collective action and change.
There are always going to be countries that may not be able to meet the targets and unfortunately, it may mean that bigger countries may have to lift more.
text_all:
There should be international agreements to enforce climate change. 
As we all know, climate change is a global issue affecting all countries.
Therefore, there have to be some agreements about changes that have to be met to reduce climate change. 
There should be a shared responsibility by all countries and regions of the world, as one or two regions can't do everything.
If someone doesn't follow the agreements, then they are benefiting from the work and money that other countries have put in. 
Countries should be held accountable for their actions.
This will all lead to collective action and change.
There are always going to be countries that may not be able to meet the targets and unfortunately, it may mean that bigger countries may have to lift more. It's true that different nations have different economic capabilities.
Some of these smaller countries are actually part of bigger countries that have more money to deal with climate change. 
There should be some leeway given to smaller countries that have far more pressing priorities.
However, if it's a bigger country, and they do have the capability and decide not to do their fair share, then they should face repercussions, as happens with various other issues.
We shouldn't let the smaller country argument stop an agreement from happening as if these smaller countries aren't part of bigger countries or allied with a bigger country in some way. The opponent keeps on sticking with the smaller country red herring.
Again, not all countries have the capability, but they often part of or allied with a bigger country.
For example: the US and UK are big countries and have numerous countries and territories that they are affiliated with.
The US and the UK can take that burden for example. 
This is for countries like the US and UK that can clearly handle the economic burden, but parts of it may not decide to.

text_initial: It's been made clear that most entities, be it companies or nations, will not voluntarily do things that aren't in their best financial interest. text_initial:
1. The penny helps keep pricing more precise and helps to avoid inaccurate pricing and overpricing of goods.
2. The use of the penny helps improve overall contribution to charities. Many people may not want to donate more than a penny, so this will help keep contributions up.
3. The penny has historical significance, as it has been around for the majority of the time the country of the USA has been established.
4. It is more cost-efficient to produce than the next highest denomination, the nickel.
text_all:
1. The penny helps keep pricing more precise and helps to avoid inaccurate pricing and overpricing of goods.
2. The use of the penny helps improve overall contribution to charities. Many people may not want to donate more than a penny, so this will help keep contributions up.
3. The penny has historical significance, as it has been around for the majority of the time the country of the USA has been established.
4. It is more cost-efficient to produce than the next highest denomination, the nickel. Percentage-wise, the cost to produce a penny is nearly equivalent to digital transactions. Per-transaction fees typically cost 1.5% to 4% of the payment amount, depending on the type of electronic transaction and provider. Also, your claim that the penny is not made of good materials Is not based in fact. People also do still use pennies. This argument does not take into account the cost it takes to make digital transactions. Per-transaction fees typically cost 1.5% to 4% of the payment amount, depending on the type of electronic transaction and provider. The 1 cent coin is the basis upon which the US currency system was founded, and to do away with it would degrade the overall US currency system.

text_initial: I don't believe that the arts are as important as math and science in schools. The way that the future is going, math and science are very important to the future of our society as a whole. Technology such as ChatGPT and other artificial intelligence tools have rendered the arts nearly obsolete, as these tools can generate documents, artworks, and other artistic mediums. They can also correct grammar. However, technology is consistently evolving, and students need to be prepared for what the future holds.
text_all: I don't believe that the arts are as important as math and science in schools. The way that the future is going, math and science are very important to the future of our society as a whole. Technology such as ChatGPT and other artificial intelligence tools have rendered the arts nearly obsolete, as these tools can generate documents, artworks, and other artistic mediums. They can also correct grammar. However, technology is consistently evolving, and students need to be prepared for what the future holds. It's important to understand that creativity and innovative thinking, while they could be fostered, are not universally understood. You could even make the argument that creativity either exists within a person or it doesn't. I think that it's important to teach objective science and math over these subjective abstract-based subjects. You could also teach about diversity without the need for unnecessary material. Human creativity is not something that is easily understood and is not exclusive to the arts. This means that it could easily be encouraged in a more pragmatic environment. Also, it's well-understood that you can't teach creativity, it either exists within a person or it doesn't. Furthermore, a balanced education is not necessarily a good thing. Schools should be focusing on students' strengths based on what is in demand, not making them casually good at a variety of subjects irrespective of the job market.

text_initial:
There should be more regulations on elections to ensure fairness. The reasoning for that relates to election interference via poll workers, potential issues with hacking election computers, and a lack of voting opportunities for some communities. 

Not all poll workers can be trusted, and regulations on who can work the polls would ensure a more fair election. Opposing countries to the United States could also meddle with polling via computer hacking. There needs to be stronger regulation on how ballots are counted to hopefully avoid that issue entirely.

Lastly, certain places don't have access to polling like other places do. This can lead to unfair election outcomes simply by access means.
text_all:
There should be more regulations on elections to ensure fairness. The reasoning for that relates to election interference via poll workers, potential issues with hacking election computers, and a lack of voting opportunities for some communities. 

Not all poll workers can be trusted, and regulations on who can work the polls would ensure a more fair election. Opposing countries to the United States could also meddle with polling via computer hacking. There needs to be stronger regulation on how ballots are counted to hopefully avoid that issue entirely.

Lastly, certain places don't have access to polling like other places do. This can lead to unfair election outcomes simply by access means. You simply cannot trust that elections are fair as they are. Nothing would be taken away from the election process if more regulations were enacted. More regulations would only ensure every eligible vote gets counted and anything ineligible would not get counted. The history of recent elections shows that some regulation of elections is needed.  Not all states have mail in ballots. Some locations even have polling places which are inaccessible to people without access to reliable transportation. Polling places are not accessible to everyone.

On top of that, poll workers in the past have been untrustworthy in their conduct. There needs to be more regulations on who can become a poll worker because of that.

There is no reason to enforce any more regulations that could take away from the election process and skew election results. As long as you are registered to vote, you are part of the democratic process that is elections.

As long as you are registered to vote, you are part of the democratic process that is elections.  Poll workers are a part of helping elections run smoothly in the country and should not be demonized. There are already balances in check to ensure voting fairness. \n\nThere are multiple ways to cast a ballot, including by mail. Therefore, voting is already accessible to all places and people in the US. If more regulations were enacted, there would be even more opportunity for the election to be skewed by the fact that there would be more people meddling in the election results. Evidence of election results being tampered with is slim.

text_initial: I believe that every US citizen has a right to privacy.  An acquaintance recently was charged with selling  prescription drugs and there was a year long investigation.  I have known this person for approximately one year.  We text and were previously friends on facebook.  Because he is being investigated does not mean anyone should have access to my texts, phone calls, or conversations on facebook.  \nIf the government picks up certain "buzzwords" in conversation, it should not give them the right to listen to entire private conversations.  We should not be living under Big Brother's watchful eye.  We are still Americans with rights.
text_all: I believe that every US citizen has a right to privacy.  An acquaintance recently was charged with selling  prescription drugs and there was a year long investigation.  I have known this person for approximately one year.  We text and were previously friends on facebook.  Because he is being investigated does not mean anyone should have access to my texts, phone calls, or conversations on facebook.  \nIf the government picks up certain "buzzwords" in conversation, it should not give them the right to listen to entire private conversations.  We should not be living under Big Brother's watchful eye.  We are still Americans with rights. National security is extremely important.  However, for citizens like myself who have a lifetime history of no terrorist involvement, history of a criminal record, we should be given our right to privacy.  It's not a matter of worrying that the government is checking in with us, it's a matter of personal privacy.  And who is paying these government employees to listen in?...we are, as taxpayers.  I'm not completely sure if there are MANY attacks that could have been prevented by listening in, where is the proof of that? As a parent, I would not listen in on my child's private conversations.  The government is certainly not my parent. If it were a matter of life and death, that is a completely different scenario.  However, I do not believe even then the government would have the right to listen in on every American citizens private conversations.  What the government could and should be doing instead is tracking all of the illegal immigrants who were recently let into our country under the current administration.  My friend violated my trust, is that what you are saying? That doesn't give the government the right to listen in on my private conversations.  That is between my friend and myself.
