Do you have individualized instruction for each student then? This seems like from a logical standpoint hard to replicate. During tests there are different learning styles that are offered. Those with hard of hearing are read the tests. Those with slow reading are read the tests. There are accommodations for all kinds of students. Standerized tests serve as a starting point. It allows teachers to know what they know and what they need to teach. Students should be taught about identifying their own strengths and weaknesses. We should teach about self esteem. Having a nation wide class that is mandatory that talks about mental health. Having tests like this set them up for the real world. Its a dog eat dog world and we have to be used to competition. Having the same questions across the board allows for test scores to looked at evenly. If you give different kinds of tests then you cannot look at them with a balanced approach.

text_initial: Standardized testing can do much more harm than good, and here are some ways: it can set an unfair standard for students who learn slower or are a bit more behind than the other students. This can cause unnecessary anxiety and depression. The tests have no natural way to measure progress; those with better memories can do better than those who learn at a slower pace. They also need to consider different learning styles that students may have and, in this way, DO NOT measure academic proficiency. Finally, many of the questions on the test have no relevance to the material taught by the teacher and are random questions the student may have never seen before.
text_all: Standardized testing can do much more harm than good, and here are some ways: it can set an unfair standard for students who learn slower or are a bit more behind than the other students. This can cause unnecessary anxiety and depression. The tests have no natural way to measure progress; those with better memories can do better than those who learn at a slower pace. They also need to consider different learning styles that students may have and, in this way, DO NOT measure academic proficiency. Finally, many of the questions on the test have no relevance to the material taught by the teacher and are random questions the student may have never seen before.  You need to take into account the different ways students learn, the subjects they like the most, and the further processing abilities of each student. Having all the same questions doesn't measure a student's ability to the utmost respect; different students with diverse learning abilities may be better at other sections than others. They should not be shamed for this. These tests cause unnecessary stress and anxiety to an already vulnerable youth and create space for depression when the student believes they cannot live up to the other scores.  Okay, but many of the questions on these tests will NEVER be taught by the teacher or put into the curriculum. So much of the information clog up the memory or will never be addressed again, and failing a student for not knowing something they DON'T need to know is entirely unfair. Different learning styles don't mean 'hard of hearing'; we're talking about kids with varying learning abilities or disorders. What about kids who have autism or something along those spectrums? Should they be included in the same testing as other kids? Tests don't always measure a kids ability, stress can produce poor test results while the kid excels in homework and every other part of school.

text_initial: With a shorter life cycle than humans, animal models can be studied throughout their whole life span and across several generations, a critical element in understanding how a disease processes and how it interacts with a whole, living biological system. However animals can be used for scientific research.
text_all: With a shorter life cycle than humans, animal models can be studied throughout their whole life span and across several generations, a critical element in understanding how a disease processes and how it interacts with a whole, living biological system. However animals can be used for scientific research.  Virtually everyone alive today has benefited from the medical advances made possible through animal research. Polio, smallpox, diphtheria, cholera and measles are no longer major threats to public health in the United States. Sophisticated diagnostic tests mean early treatment of cancer and heart disease. If using animals for research is unethical, kindly give a specific alternative to this opposition you're making. Numerous vaccines and medications throughout human history became what they are today from animal testing. Everything from cholera to polio had animals leading the charge to save millions of human lives. It is not just vaccines and medications — they also advanced surgical procedures, like organ transplants and other body part replacements. Biomedical research would not be the same without it.

text_initial: While expanding the Supreme Court may seem like a good idea to some, there are a multitude of reasons as to why it should never happen. The U.S. government is meant to exist on a system of checks and balances, where the supreme court is to function as a largely impartial entity. Packing the supreme court sets a dangerous precedent in which outside forces would be allowed to interfere with this impartiality.
text_all: While expanding the Supreme Court may seem like a good idea to some, there are a multitude of reasons as to why it should never happen. The U.S. government is meant to exist on a system of checks and balances, where the supreme court is to function as a largely impartial entity. Packing the supreme court sets a dangerous precedent in which outside forces would be allowed to interfere with this impartiality.  While it is true that court packing may ensure a balanced justice system, it seems unnecessary given the fact that, historically, the court is able to balance itself. Moreover, it makes more sense that a court free from outside interference would be more impartial than one influenced by other government forces.  The Court has had a long history of having nine judges. Changing long-held traditions now may lead to more strife between political parties, shaking the boat for a change that is not necessary to upholding democracy.

text_initial: Arguing in favor of the proposition, I assert that social media's design and content structure contribute to a decline in critical thinking skills and intellectual depth.
text_all: Arguing in favor of the proposition, I assert that social media's design and content structure contribute to a decline in critical thinking skills and intellectual depth. Disputing the CON side's view, I emphasize the detrimental effects of social media's addictive design, superficial connectivity, and its impact on critical thinking skills and depth. The inherent challenges posed by algorithmic biases, the prevalence of echo chambers, and the risk of misinformation outweigh these potential benefits. The addictive nature of social media, coupled with the platform's design aimed at maximizing engagement, often leads to passive content consumption rather than active curation.

text_initial: There's enough bickering and interference between countries as it is. We don't need to add to the list. Do you want your neighbor telling you how to run your household? Probably not, and it's the same with countries. Let countries have the right to decide their own climate policies. And how difficult is it going to be to establish policy that many countries agree on? Nigh impossible, I contend. All the energy and money and time spent on the endeavor will prove futile and a waste.
text_all: There's enough bickering and interference between countries as it is. We don't need to add to the list. Do you want your neighbor telling you how to run your household? Probably not, and it's the same with countries. Let countries have the right to decide their own climate policies. And how difficult is it going to be to establish policy that many countries agree on? Nigh impossible, I contend. All the energy and money and time spent on the endeavor will prove futile and a waste. If you and your country feel that particular issue is more important than the wellbeing of the populace, crime, defense, and so many others, then I say you and your country are entitled to act upon that belief. But for you to impose that belief onto another country and its citizens, for you to tell them what should be most important to them, is immoral. It may be sad, but the times we're living in... climate change just isn't as important to a country and its citizens as other issues. And on so many of those other issues, global disagreement is more common than not. Collaboration on the scale required to effect environmental results just isn't realistic today, this year, this decade.

text_initial:
As an advocate for considering race in college admissions to ensure diversity, I firmly believe in the value of a diverse educational environment. From my perspective, when colleges include race as one of the factors in their admissions process, it's not just about numbers or meeting quotas; it’s about enriching the educational experience for everyone.

I've seen firsthand how diverse classrooms lead to more dynamic and comprehensive discussions. In an increasingly globalized world, being exposed to a variety of perspectives and backgrounds is essential. It prepares us not just academically, but also socially, for the real world where we will interact with people from all walks of life.

Moreover, it's important to acknowledge the historical context. For decades, certain racial and ethnic groups have been underrepresented in higher education due to systemic barriers and inequalities. By considering race in admissions, colleges can play a role in leveling the playing field and offering opportunities to those who have been historically marginalized.

This isn't just about benefiting minority students; it's about benefiting all students. When we interact with people who are different from us, we learn tolerance, empathy, and gain a broader understanding of the world. It prepares us to be better leaders, innovators, and citizens.

Finally, the legal framework in many places, including the U.S. Supreme Court, has recognized the importance of diversity in education and upheld the limited use of race as a factor in admissions. This is a testament to the value our society places on diversity as a crucial component of education.
text_all:
As an advocate for considering race in college admissions to ensure diversity, I firmly believe in the value of a diverse educational environment. From my perspective, when colleges include race as one of the factors in their admissions process, it's not just about numbers or meeting quotas; it’s about enriching the educational experience for everyone.

I've seen firsthand how diverse classrooms lead to more dynamic and comprehensive discussions. In an increasingly globalized world, being exposed to a variety of perspectives and backgrounds is essential. It prepares us not just academically, but also socially, for the real world where we will interact with people from all walks of life.

Moreover, it's important to acknowledge the historical context. For decades, certain racial and ethnic groups have been underrepresented in higher education due to systemic barriers and inequalities. By considering race in admissions, colleges can play a role in leveling the playing field and offering opportunities to those who have been historically marginalized.

This isn't just about benefiting minority students; it's about benefiting all students. When we interact with people who are different from us, we learn tolerance, empathy, and gain a broader understanding of the world. It prepares us to be better leaders, innovators, and citizens.

Finally, the legal framework in many places, including the U.S. Supreme Court, has recognized the importance of diversity in education and upheld the limited use of race as a factor in admissions. This is a testament to the value our society places on diversity as a crucial component of education. Comprehensive View of Merit: Firstly, it's important to clarify that considering race in college admissions doesn't mean neglecting merit or individual achievements. It's about broadening our understanding of what 'merit' encompasses. Merit should not be viewed solely through the lens of test scores or grades; it should also include diverse experiences and backgrounds that contribute to an enriching academic environment. By considering race alongside other factors, we are not diminishing merit but enriching its definition.

Race and Experience: While it is true that an individual cannot control their race, it is equally true that race can significantly influence one's life experiences. These experiences, shaped by racial backgrounds, bring unique perspectives to the educational environment. To ignore race is to overlook the rich, varied insights that racial diversity contributes to the learning experience of all students.

Socioeconomic Factors and Race: Addressing socioeconomic disparities is indeed crucial, and I agree that it should be a factor in admissions. However, socioeconomic status and race are not mutually exclusive factors. Racial minorities often face unique challenges that are not solely defined by socioeconomic status. These challenges, informed by both historical and present-day contexts, can impact educational opportunities and outcomes. Therefore, a holistic approach that considers both race and socioeconomic factors is more effective in promoting true diversity and equity. Intersectionality of Race and Socioeconomic Status: While socioeconomic status is undeniably important, it cannot fully replace the consideration of race. Race and socioeconomic status are interrelated but distinct factors. Each contributes uniquely to an individual's identity and experiences. Ignoring race in favor of solely focusing on socioeconomic status risks overlooking the specific challenges and perspectives that come with racial identity.

Comprehensive Diversity Beyond Socioeconomic Status: Racial diversity adds significant value to the educational experience that cannot be fully captured by considering socioeconomic status alone. It brings varied cultural perspectives, historical contexts, and social experiences that enrich classroom discussions and campus life, promoting a deeper understanding and appreciation of different cultures and backgrounds.

text_initial: It may perpetuate discrimination by treating individuals differently based on their race. A truly diverse and equitable system should prioritize socioeconomic factors, ensuring opportunities for underprivileged individuals, rather than solely relying on race as a diversity indicator

text_initial: Some people argue that social media is making people more stupid, but in actuality social media has been improving peoples' intelligence in many areas. Being exposed to a wider variety of cultures, etc gives people a broader understanding of life, and thus makes them wiser. Many people also share life skills or hacks that many aren't taught in skills, and people are instead able to learn from people teaching on social media. So in a wide variety of ways, social media is making people smarter.

There are some comments on posts where people have gone and done research, and then come back to the post to add intelligent comments or new perspectives to the post. Same with posts about life skills, etc. People are brought awareness to these skills, and then it's a jumping board for them to do more research on their own and learn more.

text_initial:
The government should not have the right to censor the internet.  The public should have free and open access to the internet, with no censorship.  

For example, communist countries often censor what their public can and can't see online, and it not only encourages, but makes it a reality that the public will continue to be a giant bubble.
text_all:
The government should not have the right to censor the internet.  The public should have free and open access to the internet, with no censorship.  

For example, communist countries often censor what their public can and can't see online, and it not only encourages, but makes it a reality that the public will continue to be a giant bubble. 

 If the government is the one making the rules, and enforcing censorship, how does that imply that only unlawful content would be censored?  The problem with censorship is not that some content online is maybe best not spread, but that by censoring the content at all, you are imparting bias.  The public has the right to all information, and content and to make their own decisions and choices based on that information.  If the government censors the internet, we are encouraging a dumber population, who has not had access to multiple sides of an argument and/or issue. If the government is censoring the internet, who is stop them from generating their own 'fake news'?  No human is truly unbiased and able to be completely neutral.  Therefore, it is important that the public can determine for themselves what aligns with them and what doesn't.

text_initial: The question posed asks whether or not citizens should receive universal basic income (UBI) from the government. UBI is a process wherein the government provides citizens with a monthly baseline stipend as default due to their citizenship. This is in addition to any monthly income that they may be currently accruing. Universal basic income is often quoted as a necessity as the economy of the worlds countries continues to increase often without consideration for the everyday citizen. However, proponents of UBI often do not consider the impacts of implementing such a policy. Largely, the introduction of UBI would lead to an increase in taxes across the population. A severe increases in taxes due to the introduction of this policy would heavily burden and likely harm the lowest income classes in a population, placing undue stress upon them that may not be offset by the supplem
text_all: The question posed asks whether or not citizens should receive universal basic income (UBI) from the government. UBI is a process wherein the government provides citizens with a monthly baseline stipend as default due to their citizenship. This is in addition to any monthly income that they may be currently accruing. Universal basic income is often quoted as a necessity as the economy of the worlds countries continues to increase often without consideration for the everyday citizen. However, proponents of UBI often do not consider the impacts of implementing such a policy. Largely, the introduction of UBI would lead to an increase in taxes across the population. A severe increases in taxes due to the introduction of this policy would heavily burden and likely harm the lowest income classes in a population, placing undue stress upon them that may not be offset by the supplem While there is an assumption that a guaranteed basic income would ensure economic security, there is no consideration for the economic impact that an increase in taxes, as would be necessary to implement this program would have on the working class. As guaranteed basic income necessities this increase, there is no clear evidence that the net profit gain from the worker would be offset by the loss in the increase of taxes. Similarly, the impact on welfare while a reasonable assumption is not guaranteed. The government would need to enact sweeping policy touching on all sectors for  The crux of the argument lies in whether or not the costs of increased taxes would be offset by the safety net provided via the implementation of a universal basic income. This implementation would need to handled broadly - that is not merely by a single policy but various ones that would increase welfare across the board, that is sweeping changes to any non socialized systems (such as healthcare in the US, for example). A simple UBI policy without evidence of any offset costs after the initial tax increases is likely due to fail, as it my end up merely producing a net zero increase in income from the general population. Similarly, the pricing of goods and services may ultimately fluctuate as the general income of the population on average increases slightly, once again offsetting the purpose of the policy.

text_initial: When we think of the term 'worthwhile', we should consider worthwhile to whom, and in what context? Space exploration is worthwhile to humanity because of the individual desires of people who wish to understand the greater universe and all its mysteries. For decades, people have been interested in exploring the unknown, which is why becoming an astronaut is one of the hardest and most competitive career paths. It was a big hot button topic during the Cold War, culminating in one of the most famous events in American history.
text_all: When we think of the term 'worthwhile', we should consider worthwhile to whom, and in what context? Space exploration is worthwhile to humanity because of the individual desires of people who wish to understand the greater universe and all its mysteries. For decades, people have been interested in exploring the unknown, which is why becoming an astronaut is one of the hardest and most competitive career paths. It was a big hot button topic during the Cold War, culminating in one of the most famous events in American history.  This argument posits that it is a mutually exclusive situation, where one can do one of the two but not both. Yes, while it is true that it would be a costly endeavor, previous federal budgets continue to balloon. There is money to go around, and there should be plenty of room in the budget for both initiatives. In terms of the environmental cost, it would be a much more fruitful argument if every human and company's impact on the environment were already perfect. It would be much more impactful to discuss the negative effects of our actions here on Earth, which we can do plenty about regardless of space exploration. Once again, the opposing argument is based on the premise that there can only be one focus at a time. Governments and states should be well equipped to devote resources to multiple things at once. Environmental preservation and education is important, perhaps equally as important as space exploration. There is no reason to pit these two against each other. Space exploration is deserving of its own resources as the pursuit of knowledge of the universe is worthy in its own right.

text_initial: The electoral college was  created as a way to counter what the founding fathers thought of as an unknowing electorate.  It was designed in a time where there were states with little population.  This is no longer necessary.  We've had decades where presidents have been installed who did not receive the majority of votes.  The Electoral college has made our system less democratic as it ignores the will of millions.
text_all: The electoral college was  created as a way to counter what the founding fathers thought of as an unknowing electorate.  It was designed in a time where there were states with little population.  This is no longer necessary.  We've had decades where presidents have been installed who did not receive the majority of votes.  The Electoral college has made our system less democratic as it ignores the will of millions. Traditions are wonderful; they bring emotions and a sense of nostalgia.  Traditions are unbending and we live in a world that is changing.  We need to build the structure that will support the way we live and how we work.  The electoral college takes away the voices of many.  This isn't supposed to be minority rules.   The college needs to be eliminated so that we can grow. Traditions are great, but we are a nation of laws.  We can make a new tradition of representing all our voters and not rely on an outdated system that was established by a lack of trust in the people.  Installing candidates that represent the minority is not a symbol of democracy and it makes us weaker in the view of other democracies.

text_initial:
Hello there!
I feel that the United States should provide aid and assistance to the Ukraine.
I feel that they are fairly innocent people being invaded and that we should always defend the under-dog, especially when it is Russia who is leading the attacks against them.
When we had Trump as a president, we were almost allied with Russia, which has always been an enemy of the United States... now under different leadership, we're no longer required to take sides with Russia, I suppose.
I feel that the Ukraine is more of an ally to the United States than Russia is, and we should go out of our way to ensure they are safe and protected.
Although it certainly costs tax dollars and troops, I feel that it is an important thing to do for these people, and to ensure that we maintain ourselves as a moral nation.
A lot of innocent lives can be lost through this conflict, and I feel that we should support a nation that may not have the means to defend itself from this attack.
Thank you for listening to my stand on this issue!
text_all:
Hello there!
I feel that the United States should provide aid and assistance to the Ukraine.
I feel that they are fairly innocent people being invaded and that we should always defend the under-dog, especially when it is Russia who is leading the attacks against them.
When we had Trump as a president, we were almost allied with Russia, which has always been an enemy of the United States... now under different leadership, we're no longer required to take sides with Russia, I suppose.
I feel that the Ukraine is more of an ally to the United States than Russia is, and we should go out of our way to ensure they are safe and protected.
Although it certainly costs tax dollars and troops, I feel that it is an important thing to do for these people, and to ensure that we maintain ourselves as a moral nation.
A lot of innocent lives can be lost through this conflict, and I feel that we should support a nation that may not have the means to defend itself from this attack.
Thank you for listening to my stand on this issue! How would this affect self employed and small business owners?
I'm genuinely curious, being a self employed small business owner myself.

As for the issue with Russia, I don't feel that it's right to not interfere and protect a nation because we're afraid of making Russia angry.
That feels like giving in and looking away from a wrong-doing because we're afraid of their reaction.
The United States is a strong nation and should not be taking orders from Russia, nor should it be afraid of enraging their leader.
If war is to happen, it will likely happen, whether or not we "kiss up" and refuse to help an innocent nation.
Plus, if we establish ourselves as a nation of people who can be intimidated, what else will Russia try to get away with? I feel that we, as a nation, need to be strong against powers which may be intimidating.
I feel that if we give in and deny supplies and aid to the Ukraine because we're afraid of what Russia may do to us, this gives way to a slippery slope.
What else will they do?
Who else will they invade while we're too afraid to step in and help?
What happens when we're the ones who need the other nations to step up and defend us, but they're too involved in their own economics to help us?
I feel that we need to take a stand against "wrong and evil" actions now, before it's too late.

text_initial: Public college should be tuition-free in a functioning society. It is the duty of a healthy government and society to provide free education to children and, in our modern era, it should also be considered a duty to provide education to young adults. A college degree is necessary to perform many well-paying jobs in today's climate, and it is beneficial if the most people possible can be trained with the necessary skills. In addition, a well-educated population will make better choices when it comes to voting and for their own life decisions (i.e., finance, relationships, health). All in all, it would benefit everyone to have tuition-free college.
text_all: Public college should be tuition-free in a functioning society. It is the duty of a healthy government and society to provide free education to children and, in our modern era, it should also be considered a duty to provide education to young adults. A college degree is necessary to perform many well-paying jobs in today's climate, and it is beneficial if the most people possible can be trained with the necessary skills. In addition, a well-educated population will make better choices when it comes to voting and for their own life decisions (i.e., finance, relationships, health). All in all, it would benefit everyone to have tuition-free college. Resources can be reallocated, so it is not simply that resources are limited - for example, a fraction of military funding could be redirected to paying for college tuition, or taxes could be raised by a marginal amount. Offering tuition-free college does not mean that the quality of the education would decrease. In addition, offering scholarships drastically limits the opportunities to only a select handful of students, when all young people deserve to receive an advanced education if they so wish. Why should degrees be devalued by more people receiving them? More knowledge in the population is better for every person and there should be no gatekeeping to artificially increase the value of knowledge. It is a false argument to state that success hinges on quality when providing tuition-free college does not have to result in a decrease in quality at all (in fact, its quality might increase as it would be held to more universal standards). In addition, vocational training and apprenticeships are also not cost-free, and providing free public college does not necessarily limit those routes being overlooked (particularly if they are also made to be cost-free).

text_initial: The most equitable solution for ensuring that college admissions candidates receive admission to the college of their choice should be based on skills, GPA, test scores, extracurricular activities, and aptitudes. Unfortunately, in our society, we sometimes overlook the most qualified candidates to create a more diverse demographic of students. Higher education institutions must pick the most qualified range of students for admissions, despite students' racial backgrounds. What we should instead, to ensure that colleges do have a diverse student population, is to make sure that public and private K-12 education is funded equitably throughout the nation so that all students have access to the same opportunities for college educations, if desired.

Unfortunately, in our society, we sometimes overlook the most qualified candidates to create a more diverse demographic of students. Higher education institutions must pick the most qualified range of students for admissions, despite students' racial backgrounds. What we should instead, to ensure that colleges do have a diverse student population, is to make sure that public and private K-12 education is funded equitably throughout the nation so that all students have access to the same opportunities for college educations, if desired.  Colleges do not necessarily benefit from having a more racially diverse student body. Education is based on the quality of the institution, resources, professors, and curriculum that is provided - not racial diversity amongst students. Furthermore, anyone of any race can build an environment of inclusivity and increased cultural perspectives. While representation amongst a diverse group of individuals is important, it is not necessary to ensure a high-quality educational experience for college students. Colleges should instead focus on admitting the most qualified candidates amongst many other factors to ensure the success of thei As a public school educator, I can understand that merit-based admissions are a component of institutional and structural racism that exists. What I think we should focus on, rather than enabling colleges to pick a diverse population of students regardless of their academic merit, is to ensure that all learners in America's K-12 education system have equitable opportunities throughout their schooling. This way, there would inevitably be a more diverse population of students at colleges because a diverse range of students would be highly qualified. Let's fund public schools and public education nationally so that we can alleviate the racial and economic disparities that exist nationwide. High quality public education will ENSURE that colleges do not need to look at race, because students across demographics will ALL be highly qualified candidates.

This will also set up students for success in working individually and increasing their concentration. There is no difference in sitting in a classroom in a full room than there is at home. text_initial: Artificial Intelligence is good for society. Once properly trained, AI can be more reliable than a human taking away the risk of "human error." AI can be used to automate certain activities, which frees up time and resources which we can then devote to other endeavors. AI is the future and will assist us in advanced even further. AI can assist us with little tasks like helping us to write a text message to large tasks like driving us to the airport. In that little task, they are helping us to do something better. In the large task they are saving us time and making the task less risky.
text_all: Artificial Intelligence is good for society. Once properly trained, AI can be more reliable than a human taking away the risk of "human error." AI can be used to automate certain activities, which frees up time and resources which we can then devote to other endeavors. AI is the future and will assist us in advanced even further. AI can assist us with little tasks like helping us to write a text message to large tasks like driving us to the airport. In that little task, they are helping us to do something better. In the large task they are saving us time and making the task less risky. Artificial Intelligence is bad for society. Everything is becoming automated with AI and we are losing the human touch. If you look at resumes today, compared to ten years ago, you'll find a large majority of them are written (or improved) with AI. That takes away the personality and invividuality of the person whose resume it is. AI is getting involved in all sorts of things, like driving and customer service jobs, that are causing humans to lose out on those positions. We are becoming less engaged in what we are doing, and with others, because of AI. AI is asisting us. It is not for me to judge what others do with the extra time provided by AI completing tasks. AI is a great and useful tool and to not use it seems foolish. It is the future and it needs to fully utilized and to assist us in making life and the world better.

text_initial: I believe that artificial intelligence is bad for society. I find that the most consistent effect of artificial intelligence on human society is that it robs people of the ability to think for and be efficient in themselves. Artificial intelligence continues to provide for people much more quickly then the human body and mind can but as a consequence people begin to take the easy route and rely on artificial intelligence more. This means that the natural human proclivity towards challenging and improving oneself becomes less common as people just use this technology as a shortcut. Employers no longer train people to do what artificial intelligence can do which takes away peoples jobs and does not improve their station. In the end people are both ostracized by technology and increasingly reliant upon it to function which is truly terrifying. Society thus becomes more atomized and vulnerable with each passing year.
text_all: I believe that artificial intelligence is bad for society. I find that the most consistent effect of artificial intelligence on human society is that it robs people of the ability to think for and be efficient in themselves. Artificial intelligence continues to provide for people much more quickly then the human body and mind can but as a consequence people begin to take the easy route and rely on artificial intelligence more. This means that the natural human proclivity towards challenging and improving oneself becomes less common as people just use this technology as a shortcut. Employers no longer train people to do what artificial intelligence can do which takes away peoples jobs and does not improve their station. In the end people are both ostracized by technology and increasingly reliant upon it to function which is truly terrifying. Society thus becomes more atomized and vulnerable with each passing year. AI can take away the "risk" of human error but this also takes away the opportunity for self-improvement within the human being. AI automating activities does not guarantee that time and resources that are freed up will be used in such a way as to improve the average person's life nor that they will be the one to benefit resource wise in our capitalist society. AI being the future is something that should trouble everyone. The fact that AI invades human activity on both a small and large scale such as text messages versus driving us to the airport suggest that it is invasive in all aspects of life and we cannot escape it even if we wish to. If AI takes away our jobs, our art and our science than what is there left for us to do better at exactly? They are not saving us, artificial intelligence is rendering human life as irrelevant. Even if we are losing the human touch we have the opportunity to develop new technologies and new techniques through our integration with AI. AI writing resumes means that the resumes will be more concise and uniform which will make it easier for people who aren't skilled at writing resumes. The personality and individuality of resumes can be a bad thing for many individuals. Humans who lose out on these positions will acquire new ones as a matter of course or they will advocate for government reforms so that they are supported in any case. We will have more opportunities to engage with one another because we will work less and expend less energy due to the aid of artificial intelligence.

text_initial: I don't think it's worth investing time and money to explore space because we have other more important things to focus on in our planet. We are muddled up in a bunch of worldly issues like war, poverty, overpopulation, global warming and so much more that space exploration is the last thing we should talk about. Take the Russian and Ukraine war, as well as the Israel and Palestine war, for example. And even then, sometimes the government doesn't pay too much attention to these things which they should do.
text_all: I don't think it's worth investing time and money to explore space because we have other more important things to focus on in our planet. We are muddled up in a bunch of worldly issues like war, poverty, overpopulation, global warming and so much more that space exploration is the last thing we should talk about. Take the Russian and Ukraine war, as well as the Israel and Palestine war, for example. And even then, sometimes the government doesn't pay too much attention to these things which they should do.  I understand that it might progress humanity and help further advance technology, which we are in now. But I think it is something we should talk about in the future and not in the present, as we have way worse issues to focus on. I think it should be done in a timely manner, and now is not the right time unless we resolve our worldly crisis. Yes, but it is not the only problem that we are facing right now. While it could help our environment a lot, we have been thinking of ways to eliminate negative environmental factors without having to spend a ton of money for space exploration. My point is, focusing on space exploration is possible and may even be beneficial for humanity, but now is not the right time to do it.

text_initial:
-If we enact more regulations then it will discourage voters from registering and voting. 
-It will be costly to enact more regulations and we need to use our money, time, and resources for more pressing issues such as healthcare and food security.
-Making more regulations will just complicate the voting franchise and make it more difficult for citizens to vote.
text_all:
-If we enact more regulations then it will discourage voters from registering and voting. 
-It will be costly to enact more regulations and we need to use our money, time, and resources for more pressing issues such as healthcare and food security.
-Making more regulations will just complicate the voting franchise and make it more difficult for citizens to vote.

 Creating more regulations just complicates an already complicated process. It will use money, energy, and resources that are needed for healthcare and food security, which are much more important issues. More regulations doesn't necessarily equal increasing the public trust, since many regulations created still often favor specific political parties.   If you perceive spending money on the voting franchise as making necessary investments, then doing voter registration drives would be more important than increasing regulations. That would actually encourage people to register and educate them on the right to vote. My husband is actually a volunteer that does voter registration, and in all actuality, the process of registering more people could actually be free since my husband is a volunteer.

text_initial: Standardized testing disrupts that natural course load of the teacher, taking away from the natural flow of learning. Students are exposed to unnecessary stress trying to fit a "standard", which only serves to measure the mean of intelligence of students. Creative students who may not do well on these tests may be brilliant, but suffer under unrealistic expectations. They are forced to fit into a box into which they are judged unfairly, and develop lack of confidence. Many students do not do well with pressure and excel in a more creative model of learning.  Highly intelligent students may do well, but find themselves bored. Each student should find his or her individual style of learning, and these tests only measure logical thinking.
text_all: Standardized testing disrupts that natural course load of the teacher, taking away from the natural flow of learning. Students are exposed to unnecessary stress trying to fit a "standard", which only serves to measure the mean of intelligence of students. Creative students who may not do well on these tests may be brilliant, but suffer under unrealistic expectations. They are forced to fit into a box into which they are judged unfairly, and develop lack of confidence. Many students do not do well with pressure and excel in a more creative model of learning.  Highly intelligent students may do well, but find themselves bored. Each student should find his or her individual style of learning, and these tests only measure logical thinking.   I counter your belief that equality is a blanket term to repress creativity and these tests do not identify areas of improvement. Each teacher should have the choice to formulate their own teaching style aside from the socialized standard.  Teaching efforts are measured by the growth and enhancement of individual students, and these tests are designed for the school board and not the students.  There must be a better way to assess students rather than placing them into a box. Being left behind due to disengagement is exactly why these tests serve no purpose; many students think that intelligence alone, or the ability to perform, are the cornerstones of a happy life. This is not the case. By placing a standard upon them, they are taught that education is simply the remembering of facts and figures, and does not teach them about their true capacity.  I know many teachers who have experienced severe disruption.

text_initial: Animals should be used for scientific research. Human subjects are not always suitable for scientific research. Animals should be used to test medications and procedures before they are tried out on humans. While some things, such as cosmetics, often boast  “no animal testing,” most cosmetic ingredients are either known irritants or not known irritants. In medicine however new and untried procedures and medications should be tested on animals first with the minimum amount of cruelty necessary. For example, animals should not be subjected to pain and if they are injured by a medication or procedure they should be quickly and humanely Euthanized.
text_all: Animals should be used for scientific research. Human subjects are not always suitable for scientific research. Animals should be used to test medications and procedures before they are tried out on humans. While some things, such as cosmetics, often boast  “no animal testing,” most cosmetic ingredients are either known irritants or not known irritants. In medicine however new and untried procedures and medications should be tested on animals first with the minimum amount of cruelty necessary. For example, animals should not be subjected to pain and if they are injured by a medication or procedure they should be quickly and humanely Euthanized.  Animals are genetically very similar to humans, and make ideal subjects for testing. Computer simulations cannot begin to do the same job that animal testing would accomplish. Any animal testing should be done as humanely as possible. Not using animals for testing could result in disaster when medications were given to humans. It's not ideal to use animal subjects, but it's the best we have. Animal testing has a long history in the scientific community. It is true that sometimes testing practices have been cruel, but there are ways to circumvent that. Any lab that tests on animals should be overseen, and any unnecessary cruelty prevented. To make advances in medical science, it is necessary to use creatures biologically similar to humans. Many medical advances would never have happened without the use of animals as subjects. No computer simulation can possibly equal the results of trying out medications or procedures on animals.

text_initial: Yes, Online Learning is a Suitable Replacement for Traditional In-Person Education as it provides many more opportunities for students due to geography limitations, in-person fees and access restraints, timing availability of coursework, and also a social boundary for those too shy to engage with an in-person lecture. The knowledge is the same, only the the application of that knowledge will change in an online environment. For instance, a lazy student taking a course in-person will likely do worse than a committed student taking the same course online. I personally have received my graduate degree from an online program strictly due to the added convenience and would recommend that approach to others hoping to achieve the same.
text_all: Yes, Online Learning is a Suitable Replacement for Traditional In-Person Education as it provides many more opportunities for students due to geography limitations, in-person fees and access restraints, timing availability of coursework, and also a social boundary for those too shy to engage with an in-person lecture. The knowledge is the same, only the the application of that knowledge will change in an online environment. For instance, a lazy student taking a course in-person will likely do worse than a committed student taking the same course online. I personally have received my graduate degree from an online program strictly due to the added convenience and would recommend that approach to others hoping to achieve the same. While you have a valid argument that online learning does not exactly replace in-person courses, they certainly are a suitable replacement as the prompt suggests. While I agree they are not a Premium replacement, online courses certainly are a suitable replacement for the same piece of paper at the end-goal. Additionally, technology and learning management systems are better equipped today to work with students in breakout groups, engage in interpersonal conversations via chat and discussion threads, as well as provide a safe space for students to absorb information at their own pace. The richness of the education is exactly the experience you suggested, strictly up to the individual not the environment. As a suitable substitute for in-person lectures, the quality of education is dictated by the student wherever they may be taking courses. Additional input from similarly uninformed peers does not add to my course enrichment, only the professor's expertise which I fully get as an online student just the same as an in person student.

text_initial: I think enacting more regulations should be done so to help prevent voter suppression/discrimination, guard against cyber threats, increase transparency and strengthen our voting infrastructure so that results are trusted to be accurate.
text_all: I think enacting more regulations should be done so to help prevent voter suppression/discrimination, guard against cyber threats, increase transparency and strengthen our voting infrastructure so that results are trusted to be accurate. First, it's important to ensure that voting is accessible to all citizens. Rules should be implemented to prevent unfair practices like fraud.  Secondly, reasonable regulations can enhance trust in the system.  Fair elections can encourage more people to vote.  Lastly, regulations aim to protect integrity so that it's not influenced by outside forces.  Without these, trust can be lost. Technology can be vulnerable to hacking but it can also improve accessibility.    Electronic machines can help people with disabilities. Also, AI can be seen as a threat, however, we need to create regulations to safeguard us against this sort of manipulation.  Lastly, encouraging grassroots movements are important and these regulations can be created to help with supporting these engagements.

text_initial: The US is already over-regulated with rules and regulations making it harder for citizens to vote and express their decisions. \nAdditional regulations will cause far fewer people to engage in the democratic process, which is not healthy for our country. \nFurthermore, when the voice of the people is diminished by a process that overcomplicated their will with incentives the country becomes less and less a democracy as a result.
text_all: The US is already over-regulated with rules and regulations making it harder for citizens to vote and express their decisions. \nAdditional regulations will cause far fewer people to engage in the democratic process, which is not healthy for our country. \nFurthermore, when the voice of the people is diminished by a process that overcomplicated their will with incentives the country becomes less and less a democracy as a result.  There must be ways to bring voting into the grassroots and public domain without involving more and more computer systems that can be hacked or even manipulated in the future by AI. AI will soon be a singularity and a greater threat to the process than either party.  But where do we draw the line? At some point, the process becomes so over-regulated that most people are too confused and dissuaded from voting altogether.  This means that far fewer people can engage in the democratic process, which is in itself undemocratic.

text_all: I believe women should have mandatory quotas if they're in a leadership positions because without these quotas women may be taken advantage of by male colleagues or even men in leadership quotas.  These quotas are a must have for women if they do not want to be taken advantage of. While you do raise good objections, I still believe there should be quotas.  If there are not quotas, women may not get into the leadership roles and they will definitely be taken advantage of. I just believe there needs to be quotas.  Men will always have the leading edge in any leadership positions.  Women will always be the under dog and unless there are quotas that will never change.

text_initial: There shouldn't be a mandatory quote for women in leadership positions because it doesn't take into account individual competence. Focusing on an individual's qualifications should be more important than their gender. Also, it might lead to tokenism, which could basically end up awarding women these positions JUST because they're women and not because they earned them or are qualified for them. As a result, it could potentially undermine the credibility of women in leadership and encourage stereotypes.
text_all: There shouldn't be a mandatory quote for women in leadership positions because it doesn't take into account individual competence. Focusing on an individual's qualifications should be more important than their gender. Also, it might lead to tokenism, which could basically end up awarding women these positions JUST because they're women and not because they earned them or are qualified for them. As a result, it could potentially undermine the credibility of women in leadership and encourage stereotypes. I disagree. It could actually lead to MORE stereotypes and discrimination if men feel threatened by them due to the possibility of tokenism and corporations feeling forced to strictly hire women based solely on their gender and not their qualifications. I feel like they would be MORE prone to being taken advantage of if they're suddenly thrust into a position where they feel like they didn't earn their position and are treated with resentment by their male peers. I understand your point as well. However, I will still have to disagree because with proper qualifications, recommendations, and abilities, the chance of a woman getting into a leadership position isn't really DECREASING without a quota. WITH a quota, it would definitely increase, but it feels forced and for the wrong reasons. I would argue that it could probably INCREASE their chance of being taken advantage of if they're placed in a position where others feel like it was gifted or forced and not earned based on their abilities.

text_initial: How many movies have you seen about the end of the world? While it may seem like cinematic fiction, we are living in our own dystopian horror created by our own hands. We must ban the consumption of fossil fuels and prioritize saving the planet. The waste and damage we have caused is already irreconcilable, and we are hitting a point of deep impact where a solution will be too little too late. With all of our technological resources available, we must allocate the funds towards saving the earth and saving our race as we know it.
text_all: How many movies have you seen about the end of the world? While it may seem like cinematic fiction, we are living in our own dystopian horror created by our own hands. We must ban the consumption of fossil fuels and prioritize saving the planet. The waste and damage we have caused is already irreconcilable, and we are hitting a point of deep impact where a solution will be too little too late. With all of our technological resources available, we must allocate the funds towards saving the earth and saving our race as we know it.  Not only does the use of fossil fuels damage our earth, it is damaging the health of the workers in the field of those occupations. It is causing respiratory disorders, stroke, and asthma, not to mention severe air pollution much of which is still being measured as far as how extreme it goes. Creating a greener solution will open new jobs and new opportunities for employment. For example, a wind farm will aide in energy production and will need a skilled staff to assist with production. Jobs will Although ideally, the shift away from fossil fuels would be a gradual one, we are short on one major thing—time. The rate of destruction fuel consumption has caused is terrifying, and pretty soon, people will not be able to turn a blind eye to what is happening. We need actionable results now, not years from now if we are expected to keep this planet and everything we've built within our economy alive.

text_initial: In an era of evolving threats, government surveillance is indispensable for national security. It enables early detection and prevention of terrorist activities, cyber threats, and other risks that jeopardize the safety of citizens. Balancing privacy concerns, robust surveillance safeguards our nation, ensuring a secure and resilient society.
text_all: In an era of evolving threats, government surveillance is indispensable for national security. It enables early detection and prevention of terrorist activities, cyber threats, and other risks that jeopardize the safety of citizens. Balancing privacy concerns, robust surveillance safeguards our nation, ensuring a secure and resilient society. \nWhile privacy concerns are valid, responsible government surveillance is crucial for national security. Strict oversight, transparent policies, and legal safeguards can mitigate privacy infringements. History teaches us the importance of learning from past mistakes, allowing us to refine surveillance practices for better accountability. Effectively managed surveillance enhances security without compromising fundamental rights. In conclusion, acknowledging the concerns raised by opponents, it's imperative to strike a delicate balance between security and privacy. A well-regulated and accountable surveillance framework, coupled with transparency measures, ensures that the benefits of threat detection are reaped without compromising personal freedom. The evolution of surveillance practices should be guided by lessons from the past, emphasizing the importance of continuous refinement and safeguards to protect citizens' rights. Ultimately, a nation can achieve security resilience by fostering trust through responsible and ethical surveillance practices.

text_initial: College should  be free because pressuring kids to take out loans to into going into school right away without knowing what they want or what their plan is is predatory.  If they choose a field they don't like then they're still stuck with debt so College at this point is a business investment that kids who are heavily pressured to choose, which is completely unfair. It's conducive to making 18 year olds which are basically kids to taking out predatory loans because that's what's expected of them.  College used to be a place of learning first and foremost yes, but now it's also seen as job training for the majority of white collar work.  That is fine but if it's going to be job training then it shouldn't exort the youth.

text_initial: Climate change is an unprecedented global challenge that transcends national boundaries, affecting the entire planet. The interconnectedness of our environment demands a unified, cooperative response. By establishing international agreements, we can foster collective responsibility, ensure equitable contributions, and create a standardized framework for addressing this critical issue. Our shared future hinges on a coordinated global effort to mitigate climate change and protect the well-being of current and future generations.\n
text_all: Climate change is an unprecedented global challenge that transcends national boundaries, affecting the entire planet. The interconnectedness of our environment demands a unified, cooperative response. By establishing international agreements, we can foster collective responsibility, ensure equitable contributions, and create a standardized framework for addressing this critical issue. Our shared future hinges on a coordinated global effort to mitigate climate change and protect the well-being of current and future generations.\n international agreements may indeed raise concerns about economic imbalances, so it's essential to recognize the long-term benefits they bring. By fostering a global commitment to climate change policies, we create a level playing field where every nation, irrespective of its economic standing, contributes to the common goal of environmental preservation. The argument for localized approaches overlooks the interconnected nature of environmental challenges. Climate change knows no borders, and a collective, standardized effort is crucial for addressing its root causes and impacts. International cooperation ensures that resources, technologies, and knowledge are shared, benefiting both developed and developing nations in their pursuit of sustainable development. In essence, the collaborative framework of international agreements provides a more robust foundation for achieving meaningful and widespread environmental change. In conclusion, while respecting the importance of national autonomy, we must recognize that the urgency and scope of climate change demand a unified global response. International agreements provide a platform for collaboration without compromising a nation's ability to address unique challenges. By combining global efforts with localized strategies, we strike a balance between collective action and respect for individual contexts. This approach ensures that no nation is left behind, fostering a shared responsibility to protect our planet for future generations. The challenge is immense, but through international cooperation, we can create a sustainable and equitable path forward.

text_initial: It seems like our federal deficit just keeps getting larger and larger, and the social programs and entitlement programs offered by our government keep getting more expensive. How are we going to pay for all of this? Everyone wants things like healthcare subsidies and people in need benefit from welfare and lower housing costs and medicaid and CHIP, etc. I think that in order to make sure the government receives enough in funding to pay for all its programs, we need to increase the taxes that the richest citizens pay. These people who make so much, say, in excess of 400k per year, are the ones that have all the access to investments in which they can avoid paying taxes. Such as tax-free municipal bonds, sheltering money in tax-deferred investments such as annuities, etc, and in addition to that, can afford CPAs to help them come up with creative deductions. People who don't make much money can barely afford their basic living expenses, and so it certainly can't be on them to pay more.
text_all: It seems like our federal deficit just keeps getting larger and larger, and the social programs and entitlement programs offered by our government keep getting more expensive. Such as tax-free municipal bonds, sheltering money in tax-deferred investments such as annuities, etc, and in addition to that, can afford CPAs to help them come up with creative deductions. People who don't make much money can barely afford their basic living expenses, and so it certainly can't be on them to pay more.  This may be true for some people, who may be saddled with a couple hundred thousand in debt when they first complete their residency or graduate law school. But over the course of their lifetime they will make that money back and then many times over. We need people, too, to do the kinds of jobs that keep our country running, like teachers and city workers and police officers, etc. These people don't make as much money because they are in more noble (and less lucrative) professions. What would we do with a country full of no one but rich doctors and lawyers? Taxes are a percentage of income, and it makes sense that it is positively correlated and not a flat tax like sales tax. That is a popular argument but by and large I don't think people who are on welfare actually want to stay there. I don't think they're "incentivized to work less or not seek higher paying jobs" when it's more likely that they're already working 2 or 3 jobs but because they have to pay for car repairs on their cheap, junky card that was what they could afford, or are paying for daycare that is the same amount of money as they make at one of their 2 jobs, that there is just no more time in the day to get a better education or seek out something higher paying. Your con argument drips of privilege. People can't just "better" themselves when they're trapped in a cycle of poverty. When? How? With what money and what time? these are big problems that won't be fixed overnight but the programs these people depend on are funded with tax dollars.

text_initial: There is no reason why there needs to be a quota for women in leadership positions. A woman should not be able to have a postion simply because she is a woman when there are factors that are more important, such as experience, knowledge, and skillset. Lets assume there are two candidates that are running for the same position. They have the same qualifiactions, except that the woman gets chosen simply because she meets the quota for women. This is not fair.
text_all: There is no reason why there needs to be a quota for women in leadership positions. A woman should not be able to have a postion simply because she is a woman when there are factors that are more important, such as experience, knowledge, and skillset. Lets assume there are two candidates that are running for the same position. They have the same qualifiactions, except that the woman gets chosen simply because she meets the quota for women. This is not fair.  Reaching a mandatory quota for women in leadership does not ensure equality in the field. You cannot argue that systemic racism is an issue if men are getting the exact work done, and perhaps even better. For certain positons, men are more qualified than women. Unconcious bias is not an issue when a person is hired based on skillset and qualifications rather than their gender. Representation should reflect qualification.  Mandatory quotas are discriminatory toward the opposite sex, in this case, towards men. The quota system favors women simply for the fact that they are women. Women who are qualified for the position will not be overlooked if they have what it takes to fulfil the desired position. Merit is more important when looking for job candidates than chosing the sex of a person to fill quotas.

text_initial: Elected officials should be paid minimum wage. They are working on behalf of many individuals earning minimum wage, which will give them first-hand experience of how it feels to live and function in this way of living. They are also afforded other conveniences at taxpayer expense, so they are likely better off than the average person they represent. Having these representatives work for minimum wage will create compassion for the workers and make the government more likely to increase the minimum wage to something close to livable. The minimum wage usually leaves people living at or below the poverty level.
text_all: Elected officials should be paid minimum wage. They are working on behalf of many individuals earning minimum wage, which will give them first-hand experience of how it feels to live and function in this way of living. They are also afforded other conveniences at taxpayer expense, so they are likely better off than the average person they represent. Having these representatives work for minimum wage will create compassion for the workers and make the government more likely to increase the minimum wage to something close to livable. The minimum wage usually leaves people living at or below the poverty level.  We shouldn't be paying people more just to "prevent corruption" we should seek to elect people who genuinely want to work to make a difference. If they are in the job for the money, they aren't there for the right reasons. Their skills and responsibilities will help them do a good job and that in itself is the best reward. Again, they will learn how others who make minimum wage have to live doing what is likely much more demanding work.  With the way government politics has been "working" in recent years, even the highest paid officials are often engaged in illicit activities. I don't believe salary determines how likely you are to be corrupt. We could use some frugal minded people in office who genuinely care about making their community better, and are pleased and honored to serve while being paid minimum wage. It would clear out a lot of these older politicians who only care about getting re-elected and are still fighting battles that aren't even relevant now.

text_initial: The US should not keep using the electoral college in Presidential Elections. The electoral college is extremely outdated and its usage does not reflect the wants of everyday American voters. It is often the case that the electoral college is able to completely alter the results of an election after they vote, which completely negates the popular vote. This happened when Trump became president, he did not win enough votes from actual voters to win, he did not even have enough key swing states, what swung the election in his favor was the Electoral College. The electoral college was first initiated because the founders wanted a certain kind of checks and balances over a potential uneducated voting class. This does not sound very constitutional because that means every vote is not equal.
text_all: The US should not keep using the electoral college in Presidential Elections. The electoral college is extremely outdated and its usage does not reflect the wants of everyday American voters. It is often the case that the electoral college is able to completely alter the results of an election after they vote, which completely negates the popular vote. This happened when Trump became president, he did not win enough votes from actual voters to win, he did not even have enough key swing states, what swung the election in his favor was the Electoral College. The electoral college was first initiated because the founders wanted a certain kind of checks and balances over a potential uneducated voting class. This does not sound very constitutional because that means every vote is not equal. The electoral college is not balanced anymore due to gerrymandering. This point would be more accurate if the voting districts were the same as when the Electoral College was created, but they are not. Politicians from both parties have drawn lines to favor their constituent's, with republicans for example forcing black and brown voting districts to vote as one block even though they are spread out across red states, and then they divvy up their white voting block to increase the value of their votes. It is justly unfair and unconstiutional.   It is impossible for the Electoral College today to represent people equally in every state, not with gerrymandering allowed. Therefore the Electoral College in modern times is unconstitutional and archaic function that is no longer necessary. The popular vote is the people's vote, if the Electoral College can override the people, how can you claim the Electoral College as a key function of a democracy?

text_initial: Not all children learn the same way. We all have our unique talents. Arts education should be considered just as important as math and science. One student could excel in art versus math or science. You can be just as successful in life with art as you can be with math or science. We could have the next Picasso if Arts Education was taken as seriously and important.
text_all: Not all children learn the same way. We all have our unique talents. Arts education should be considered just as important as math and science. One student could excel in art versus math or science. You can be just as successful in life with art as you can be with math or science. We could have the next Picasso if Arts Education was taken as seriously and important.  While it is true that the core function of education includes equipping students with essential career and life skills, it's important to recognize that arts education plays a crucial role in developing skills that are not only complementary but also essential in various professional fields. Creativity, critical thinking, and problem-solving, which are fostered through arts education, are increasingly recognized as vital components of success in diverse careers. Arts education provides students with a unique platform to explore their creativity, encouraging them to approach problems from different perspectives and fostering the ability to find innovative solutions. While science and math are undoubtedly essential components of education, arts education complements these subjects by fostering creativity, critical thinking, and communication skills, which are increasingly recognized as essential for success in a wide range of careers and life endeavors. While emphasizing a strong foundation in science and math is undoubtedly valuable, it is essential to challenge the notion that these skills are universally applicable to the detriment of fostering individual talents. The world is not solely shaped by equations and scientific principles; it is equally influenced by the creative and artistic contributions of individuals. By disproportionately prioritizing mathematical and scientific literacy, we risk stifling the potential of the next generation of innovators and visionaries who may excel in fields outside the traditional STEM disciplines. A more holistic approach to education should recognize and celebrate diverse talents, understanding that a comprehensive skill set, including the arts, is equally vital for addressing the multifaceted challenges of our society. Restricting education to a narrow focus on science and math may inadvertently limit the potential for groundbreaking advancements in other equally important spheres of human endeavor.

text_initial: Women have historically been underrepresented in leadership positions and positions of power, and this needs to change. 50% of the world is made up of women, and they represent a large demographic people who deserve to have their voices and experiences represented in leadership roles. Government, for example, has historically been very male-dominant, resulting in a number of policy decisions over the years that fail our system of checks and balances, and are detrimental to women, such as the rescindance of the Roe V. Wade decision. The senate today is made up of 75% men and only 25% women, for example. Increasing the number of women in leadership roles for not only government, but also private companies and local groups promises to benefit a large group of people who historically have not had the power or voice to make their needs heard.
text_all: Women have historically been underrepresented in leadership positions and positions of power, and this needs to change. 50% of the world is made up of women, and they represent a large demographic people who deserve to have their voices and experiences represented in leadership roles. Government, for example, has historically been very male-dominant, resulting in a number of policy decisions over the years that fail our system of checks and balances, and are detrimental to women, such as the rescindance of the Roe V. Wade decision. The senate today is made up of 75% men and only 25% women, for example. Increasing the number of women in leadership roles for not only government, but also private companies and local groups promises to benefit a large group of people who historically have not had the power or voice to make their needs heard. While mandatory gender quotas can create divisions and negative attitudes in the workplace, this is a preferable outcome to having a group of workers unequally represented. It is by far more important to make employees feel welcome, respected, and represented in their place of work and across government than to spare the feelings of those who historically have had no issues with representation in the workplace or being hired into leadership roles. Negative attitudes are the result of systemic exclusion of women, not poor hiring practices. In 2023, your argument is simply false. Women have historically been prevented from pursuing roles in leadership and government due to the culture of those places. Politics has always been referred to as a boys club, and since the creation of the US constitution, women have had to fight for their rights in ways that men are simply granted. Until the 19th amendment, women were not even allowed to vote! Much less hold positions of power. Until we exist in a society in which women are consistently paid equally and are represented equally in positions of power traditionally held by men, mandatory quotas for women in leadership positions would be a net positive.

text_initial: Mandatory gender quotas do more harm than good and therefore should not be implemented. Quotas can lead to negative attitudes among other employees, specifically the gender that does not have a quota. Mandatory gender quotas can also lead to poor hiring practices.
text_all: Mandatory gender quotas do more harm than good and therefore should not be implemented. Quotas can lead to negative attitudes among other employees, specifically the gender that does not have a quota. Mandatory gender quotas can also lead to poor hiring practices.  Women do makeup 50% of the world's population, but that is not an accurate representation of the percentage of women who are interested in pursuing a political career or leadership position in large organizations. Women are underrepresented in these areas because they choose different paths than their male counterparts.  Mandatory gender quotas prevent an organization from optimizing its human resource power. It prevents them from hiring the most qualified candidate to do the job. If creates negative attitudes in the workforce and leads to organizations underperforming.

text_initial: The Electoral College should be kept in place. This is due to the varying population density within the United States. It would be unfair to give more populous states a greater advantage, and vice versa. Votes should aim to be kept as equal as possible. The Electoral College ensures that each state gets their due representation without any biases.
text_all: The Electoral College should be kept in place. This is due to the varying population density within the United States. It would be unfair to give more populous states a greater advantage, and vice versa. Votes should aim to be kept as equal as possible. The Electoral College ensures that each state gets their due representation without any biases.  A direct popular will not make votes more equal. In fact, it would have the opposite effect. States with higher populations will take precedence, and who knows if that state is majorly Democratic or Republican? This would make votes disproportional amongst party lines.  The fact lies that not all states respect party lines. Many of the most populous states swing Democratic, for example, and implementing only a popular vote would unfairly favor the Democratic party. The Electoral College was introduced to mitigate exactly this dilemma.
