Think about how you learn in school: you read, you study, you listen, you share, you discuss. Very little of that happens with social media. It's just knee-jerk statements and responses. It does not invite the pursuit of thought.  This argument follows my original question - what do we mean by "stupid?" First, stupidity and ignorance are not the same thing, so to use them interchangeably moots your argument. What is stupidity? Where does it come from? It comes from not bothering to learn, not bothering to educate yourself. Social media fosters this - social media does not encourage learning, does not encourage educating yourself. So in that manner, i We must again begin with what we mean by "stupid" and what do we mean by making people stupid. The question is not well-written to begin with, because one can argue that nothing outside of a person makes that person stupid. But what the question really asks is does social media contribute to people's ignorance, their lack of thoughtfulness when thinking through a situation or problem. That is the obvious meaning of the question, and the answer to that question is equally obvious: social media greatly contributes to people's ignorance and to their lack of thoughtful reflection to issues and questions.

text_initial: Government surveillance is often necessary to maintain national security. Though it is not without risk and drawbacks, it's intent is to enhance the overall safety of a nation from it's enemies, both internal and foreign. \nAn example of government surveillance being necessary for upholding national security can be found with Islamists, radical terrorists, and their relation to the U.K. Some of these groups are known to inhabit the U.K., and actively support violence or propaganda against the U.K.
text_all: Government surveillance is often necessary to maintain national security. Though it is not without risk and drawbacks, it's intent is to enhance the overall safety of a nation from it's enemies, both internal and foreign. \nAn example of government surveillance being necessary for upholding national security can be found with Islamists, radical terrorists, and their relation to the U.K. Some of these groups are known to inhabit the U.K., and actively support violence or propaganda against the U.K.  Government surveillance needn't be heavy-handed, and laws can be enforced to ensure that the surveillance does not impede upon civil liberties such as the right to a fair and speedy trial, and so on. Many nations have needed to keep an eye on bad actors for a long time, and some have done so with a general respect towards personal freedoms. It is imperfect, but no surveillance at all would be a worse . It is not a matter of labeling, it is being focused on people who are part of specific violent groups, e.g. gangs, religious fanatics, supremacists, etc. who have shown in the past and and currently that they pose a real and data-backed threat to national security. Surveillance can be used in conjunction with more surgical methods.

text_initial: Every human being has the right to exist and to feel safe in the world. Part of feeling safe is that you are not subject to hateful speech, which can threaten your existence by leading to violence, cause emotional strife and prolonged stress, and leads to the general public feeling a sense of disunity. Because of these net negatives, free speech should be limited to prevent people from saying things that are untrue or things that are calls to violence against a specific group of people. Some people may be concerned with who gets to delineate the bounds of what is "good" speech, but these can be well defined in an un-biased manner. Any speech that is an explicit message promoting violence should be disallowed. Similarly, any speech that is untrue and said by a person who knows it to be untrue should not be considered as protected speech. These limits enable people to have genuine and robust conversations.
text_all: Every human being has the right to exist and to feel safe in the world. Part of feeling safe is that you are not subject to hateful speech, which can threaten your existence by leading to violence, cause emotional strife and prolonged stress, and leads to the general public feeling a sense of disunity. Because of these net negatives, free speech should be limited to prevent people from saying things that are untrue or things that are calls to violence against a specific group of people. Some people may be concerned with who gets to delineate the bounds of what is "good" speech, but these can be well defined in an un-biased manner. Any speech that is an explicit message promoting violence should be disallowed. Similarly, any speech that is untrue and said by a person who knows it to be untrue should not be considered as protected speech. These limits enable people to have genuine and robust conversations.  First, I believe you mean that free speech is protected under the first amendment - the second amendment deals with firearms. And hate speech, and the stereotypes it promotes, have been shown to have measurable effects on human health and wellbeing. People who have a negative self-image or who think that their world is unfriendly are going to be more stressed, which increases cortisol and leads to numerous health issues. By saying "as long as hate doesn't affect people," you have ceded the argument. The fact is that hate does affect people in real and quantifiable ways, especially when the speech is intended to cause harm.  Speech is action. Words do not exist on their own, but lead people to think and behave differently. Of course people can have hateful thoughts, and they can even say hateful things, but there are some things that ought not be allowed. You are allowed to say bigoted things, or make off-color jokes, but if you give speeches calling for an entire ethnic group to be eliminated, or say that someone who is qualified for a job should not be hired because of their sexual orientation, these types of speech break other laws that are in place to promote an equitable society. Having limits on free speech is not the same as instituting an Orwellian "thought police," and it is already in place with many of the laws we currently practice.

text_initial: I think freedom of speech should be protected by the Second Amendment and can't be removed or revoked in any case as long as hate doesn't affect people to the point where it's very personal and health-affecting whether it's mental or physical.
text_all: I think freedom of speech should be protected by the Second Amendment and can't be removed or revoked in any case as long as hate doesn't affect people to the point where it's very personal and health-affecting whether it's mental or physical. freedom of speech is different than having freedom of action, while a person can say whatever they like they still cant do whatever they like. we cant remove hate as its gonna be always there but instead we can shift our focus to what comes after hate which is violence and thats what we can control the problem is not hate the problem is what comes after hate, we all hate certain types of people because of certain things they've done to upset us. but the problem is when we act upon our hate and try to affect their peace, health or wealth. thats what should be stopped

text_initial: There is a lack of women in leadership positions, and to solve that issue, there should be a mandatory quota for women to be given those positions. Misogyny is major problem in our society, and women deserve to have a place in the higher echelons where they can help solve the issues we face. Because leadership roles are commonly filled by men, and misogyny is all too frequent amongst men in power, we should require companies and governments to fulfill a quota lest women be kept away from the levers of power.
text_all: There is a lack of women in leadership positions, and to solve that issue, there should be a mandatory quota for women to be given those positions. Misogyny is major problem in our society, and women deserve to have a place in the higher echelons where they can help solve the issues we face. Because leadership roles are commonly filled by men, and misogyny is all too frequent amongst men in power, we should require companies and governments to fulfill a quota lest women be kept away from the levers of power.  A quota does not preclude valuing ability; there are already many women qualified to participate in leadership today. There is already an attitude that women need special treatment to succeed, and it can only be solved by education and representation. Mandating a quota would serve to negate discriminatory hiring practices and unequal pay as more women would be in the position to dictate those processes. There is nothing that prevents us from instituting gender-neutral family leave or unconscious bias training in addition to mandating quotas for women in leadership. While it is understandable that one may not want to send the message that women need an artificial boost, keep in mind that the quota need not be permanent; after a generation or two, people will be so accustomed to women in leadership that the quota may not be necessary, and we may have implemented the other policies that also address inequality.

text_initial: I am in favor of enacting more regulations to ensure fair elections. This includes that all folks will have easy access to their poll locations. The poll locations will be well-staffed to move the line quickly so that people can vote in a reasonable amount of time. There will be a way to ensure the voter is at the right polling location with a form of identification such as but not limited to a driver's license. It is important to ensure that those folks who may not have an ID can still vote. Someone who doesn't drive due to a disability should still have a fair time voting.
text_all: I am in favor of enacting more regulations to ensure fair elections. This includes that all folks will have easy access to their poll locations. The poll locations will be well-staffed to move the line quickly so that people can vote in a reasonable amount of time. There will be a way to ensure the voter is at the right polling location with a form of identification such as but not limited to a driver's license. It is important to ensure that those folks who may not have an ID can still vote. Someone who doesn't drive due to a disability should still have a fair time voting.  More regulations would increase transparency as you know exactly who is voting. text_initial: The US should not enact more regulations to ensure fair elections because having more government intervention would lower transparency and the chances of a fair election. Additionally, it would create a higher barrier to entry, which would unfairly harm certain groups. Rather than introducing more regulations, a better approach would be to enforce the current election laws.
text_all: The US should not enact more regulations to ensure fair elections because having more government intervention would lower transparency and the chances of a fair election. Additionally, it would create a higher barrier to entry, which would unfairly harm certain groups. Rather than introducing more regulations, a better approach would be to enforce the current election laws. While the goal of placing an emphasis on making elections more accessible and efficient, it is important to take into account that additional staffing and comprehensive resource allocation will have to be considered which will lead to increased costs, thus harming certain communities. Enacting more regulations will undoubtedly provide a higher barrier to entry. Individuals with specific demographics such as lower-income individuals or minority groups will be disproportionately affected and potentially impede their ability to vote. There needs to be a balance to prevent potential discrimination who might not have traditional forms of identification. Instead, we should focus on enforcing current law.

text_initial: The United State should not keep using the Electoral College in Presidential Elections because it can negate the election choice of the citizens of the United States. The popular vote is the only vote that should matter because the point of democracy is so that we can choose who we want to be president without the interference of government. Overall, citizens should have more power than government in electing their president and all government officials. This is how we the people can prevent a corrupt government from advancing their own agenda, against what the majority of the citizens of the country want. It is our constitutional right to choose our president without governemtn interference.

text_initial: When money is involved to the extent of oil and fossil fuel companies, then the government must become involved in order for there to be a change. A company will ignore everything but profits. It is up to the government to step in when these things get out of control. A complete ban might not be necessary, but steps must be taken to ensure that we do not continue to destroy our atmosphere. It is not cars which are the problem, but fossil fuels as a whole, which are used in many different environments. Thus, the average person/consumer cannot make much of a change. This is something that must be left up to the government to take action towards.
text_all: When money is involved to the extent of oil and fossil fuel companies, then the government must become involved in order for there to be a change. A company will ignore everything but profits. It is up to the government to step in when these things get out of control. A complete ban might not be necessary, but steps must be taken to ensure that we do not continue to destroy our atmosphere. It is not cars which are the problem, but fossil fuels as a whole, which are used in many different environments. Thus, the average person/consumer cannot make much of a change. This is something that must be left up to the government to take action towards. Strangely enough, this is where the pro and con meet in the middle. It is understood by both sides of this issue that we cannot simply ban fossil fuels tomorrow. A gradual change is definitely necessary, but the change must be impactful enough to have some sort of economic and social destabilization. Without this, it wouldn't be much of a change. Those times during change will be rough, but enduring these challenges will be what helps to combat this crucial problem.  Without a ban, companies will always find a way around these measures and choices. There are always loopholes, and unless something is clear cut, there will always be grey area for a business or company to exploit. Keep in mind, that it is not consumers who are causing the problem, but businesses. They will only react when the consequences are harsh. It is simply risk versus reward in their eyes, so the risk has to be incredibly high.

text_initial: It would be an absolutely massive expense to provide all public college for free. Where would those funds come from and what would they be taken away from? College isn't a guaranteed return-on-investment for the government--who is to say all the graduates will go on to participate in careers where they will be paying it back in taxes? Additionally, fewer people will likely be able to attend college, because there will be fewer services offered (due to the cost), and that will mean less of the public is educated. Students who aren't the best of the best in high school may not be able to attend college, meaning they won't be able to broaden their horizons or perhaps move past the things that held them back in middle or high school.
text_all: It would be an absolutely massive expense to provide all public college for free. Where would those funds come from and what would they be taken away from? College isn't a guaranteed return-on-investment for the government--who is to say all the graduates will go on to participate in careers where they will be paying it back in taxes? Additionally, fewer people will likely be able to attend college, because there will be fewer services offered (due to the cost), and that will mean less of the public is educated. Students who aren't the best of the best in high school may not be able to attend college, meaning they won't be able to broaden their horizons or perhaps move past the things that held them back in middle or high school.  This is an absolutely great ideal, but it doesn't answer the question of where the money will come from. What will have money taken away from it in order to provide free college? Some of those answers, such as health care, or social services, may end up shooting the "economic" argument in the foot. There are ways to provide equal opportunities (making public college free for all those whose families earn under a certain benchmark, for example) that aren't going to have such intense ramifications on costs.  I'm not sure how free tuition will induce reforms like those expansions, as public colleges will be able to employ fewer professors and other professionals on a reduced budget. Ultimately, this would be a huge and over-reaching investment, that there isn't the current money available for, without taking away from something else. A more middle-of-the-road approach to start would be a better idea.

text_initial: It would be discriminatory to have mandatory quotes for women because it would force hiring managers to hire women, even if they were not as qualified. Hiring should be done based on who brings the best skill set, knowledge, and experience to the team. Sometimes that can be a man and other times that can be a female. At the essence, the best candidate should be selected and not based on gender. It is essential to always keep the organization's mission, vision, and values in mind when hiring new people or promoting current employees. Competency and alignment are what is most important in the makeup of an organization's employees and teams, not gender.
text_all: It would be discriminatory to have mandatory quotes for women because it would force hiring managers to hire women, even if they were not as qualified. Hiring should be done based on who brings the best skill set, knowledge, and experience to the team. Sometimes that can be a man and other times that can be a female. At the essence, the best candidate should be selected and not based on gender. It is essential to always keep the organization's mission, vision, and values in mind when hiring new people or promoting current employees. Competency and alignment are what is most important in the makeup of an organization's employees and teams, not gender.  Yes, mandatory quotes foster diversity and inclusivity, but what if there is a male candidate who is far more qualified and would serve as a stronger asset to the organization? There are already more women at the executive level now than there ever were before, so I believe we are past breaking the glass ceiling. An organization's performance and innovative capacity are based on talent, not on women or gender.  An organization can not accomplish its mission, vision, or values if it is solely focused on elevating women. The best hiring practice is to always hire the person who is the most competent, regardless of gender. If both are equal, then choosing the women would be fair as this would align with increasing diversity and inclusion.

We should be respectful towards their human rights. Restricting access due to gender violates their fundamental rights. It may have an impact on their mental health. People misusing the freedom is rare in real life, so they should not be banned.  Research and real-world examples indicate that cases of individuals falsely claiming a different gender identity for malicious purposes are extremely rare. 
Many inclusive policies are based on the facts of trust and respect for people and their self-identified gender. Fostering an place of mutual respect can contribute to a more inclusive and supportive community.
Implementing educational programs can help address concerns and promote understanding. Striking the right balance between ensuring the safety and comfort of every gender while respecting the rights and dignity of transgender individuals is a challenge worth addressing.
It is essential to approach the given issue with empathy, recognizing the shared humanity that unites us all where everyone can live authentically and without fear of discrimination.

We don’t want to risk investing a significant amount of money into renewable energy without all of the facts. We also don’t want to risk anyone losing their job for an uncertain solution.

text_initial:
There is a simple reason why college tuition should not be free, and that reason has to do with who would then be paying for it. People already have to pay high taxes, on many things that they themselves do not use. This will only add to that list. 

Less than 50% of people go to college and complete it. That means that there are tens of millions of people that would be subsidizing college for those that do attend. People that will never attend college paying for their entire fee out of their pockets. 

Many people who attend college never even complete their degree. So that money would be in essence, wasted. 

This does not sound like a fair deal for those people. The system already works as it is, without more tax dollars going towards college.
text_all:
There is a simple reason why college tuition should not be free, and that reason has to do with who would then be paying for it. People already have to pay high taxes, on many things that they themselves do not use. This will only add to that list. 

Less than 50% of people go to college and complete it. That means that there are tens of millions of people that would be subsidizing college for those that do attend. People that will never attend college paying for their entire fee out of their pockets. 

Many people who attend college never even complete their degree. So that money would be in essence, wasted. 

This does not sound like a fair deal for those people. The system already works as it is, without more tax dollars going towards college.  It may reduce debt for the few, but for everyone else it creates unnecessary strain on their already difficult living situation. There are many people who live around or near the poverty line. Additional taxes will put them further over that line. The money does not come from thin-air. Colleges themselves have no interest in making it free, because they have fees to pay. 

There are too many unknowns. While it sounds nice to have free college for students, we don't know if it actually will strengthen the economy. We could be putting tax dollars into a fund that gains the general populace absolutely nothing.  People already have the ability to attend college right now. Nobody is stopping them but themselves. If they apply themselves and put the effort in, they can either work to pay for college, or take out loans. There are also already many ways to get most or all of your tuition paid for free. 

If college is "free" for everyone, it is likely that many more people will try to attend just to do it. Not because they have interest or passion, but because they don't know what else to do with their lives. That much money going towards college tuition that will never be utilized is a massive cost to tax-payers. For what could amount to be very little gain.

text_initial:
The US should enact more regulations to ensure fair elections, specifically in these areas:

-Ending gerrymandering
-Allowing formerly incarcerated people to vote (because Black men are disproportionately incarcerated)
-Ensuring that registration is automatic and early voting is offered. 

The Republican party is increasingly unpopular and the only way they can win elections is if they reduce turnout by people of color and people in big cities. They also engage in gerrymandering to make some districts unwinnable by Democrats. And, intimidation at polling places. None of this should be allowed.
text_all:
The US should enact more regulations to ensure fair elections, specifically in these areas:

-Ending gerrymandering
-Allowing formerly incarcerated people to vote (because Black men are disproportionately incarcerated)
-Ensuring that registration is automatic and early voting is offered. 

The Republican party is increasingly unpopular and the only way they can win elections is if they reduce turnout by people of color and people in big cities. They also engage in gerrymandering to make some districts unwinnable by Democrats. And, intimidation at polling places. None of this should be allowed.  Currently, Republicans are actively trying to disenfranchise voters, so active regulation is needed to put an end to this. Apparently the existing laws give too much power to the state legislatures. The fairness of elections shouldn't depend on which party is in power in a state. It should be regulated on a federal level. Sadly, the state of our country is such that the two parties really have diverged in terms of their basic commitment to democracy. Republicans (or at least the current leaders of the party) increasingly only believe elections are fair if they win -- and say the election was rigged if they don't win. Meanwhile, they want to shrink the electorate and keep people away from the polls as much as possible. Early voting has been done for years in certain states without a problem. Automatic registration has been implemented in other countries without a problem as well.

text_initial: The question of whether college should be tuition-free is a complex and debated issue with various perspectives. Advocates argue that a tuition-free college system could enhance accessibility, alleviate economic burdens, and promote social equality. They contend that such a model could lead to a more educated and skilled workforce, contributing to economic growth and innovation.Opponents, on the other hand, express concerns about the potential increase in taxes and the impact on the quality of education. The debate extends to the role of federal investments, state contributions, and the overall cost of public colleges and universities.
text_all:
The question of whether college should be tuition-free is a complex and debated issue with various perspectives. Advocates argue that a tuition-free college system could enhance accessibility, alleviate economic burdens, and promote social equality. They contend that such a model could lead to a more educated and skilled workforce, contributing to economic growth and innovation.Opponents, on the other hand, express concerns about the potential increase in taxes and the impact on the quality of education. The debate extends to the role of federal investments, state contributions, and the overall cost of public colleges and universities. The three issues raised by you are effect on teacher salaries, waitlisting, and accessibility. First, we can subsidize teacher salaries and offer debt relief. While concerns exist, the benefits of tuition-free college outweigh potential drawbacks.

Accessible Education: Tuition-free college ensures equitable access to education, breaking down financial barriers and providing opportunities for students from diverse backgrounds [1].

Economic Empowerment: It promotes economic growth by fostering a more educated workforce, contributing to innovation and increased productivity [2].

Debt Relief: Tuition-free models alleviate the burden of student debt, allowing graduates to enter the workforce without financial constraints ].

Higher Completion Rates: Eliminating tuition barriers results in higher college completion rates, ensuring a more educated society.

Social Equality: Tuition-free education addresses disparities, creating a more inclusive society and bridging gaps in educational opportunities. Universities are locations of free thought. To have the most diverse thought in the classroom, we should have free tuition so anyone can access it. While concerns for faculty are valid, implementing free college education can be balanced to address both student accessibility and faculty needs. Advocates propose allocating additional funds to ensure universities can hire more faculty, maintaining a healthy student-to-teacher ratio. Investing in programs that attract and develop teaching talent can mitigate potential shortages, ensuring a quality education experience. Research suggests that tuition-free college can contribute significantly to the economy, potentially providing more resources for educational institutions. Institutions can engage in strategic planning to accommodate an influx of students, including expansion and adapting educational models to efficiently handle increased enrollment.

text_initial: While it is a nice idea for tuition to be free, this would cause a number of problems. This would cause countless new students to not even reach entry-level pre requisite courses. This would cause students to never reach college, as life may progress and prevent them from being able to attend.

Additionally, free tuition can cause a substantial level of waitlisting for nearly all colleges, putting new high school graduates at the bottom of a list. This would cause countless new students to not even reach entry-level pre requisite courses. Universities and community colleges alike will not have enough professors to keep up with the overflow of brand new students, and they will have less to pay them as well. text_initial: Social media is a good channel of information for many who lack the skills to properly research.  Therefore, it is not making people stupid.  In fact, it provides a medium for like minded people to be able to exchange ideas and information.  It provides people of the same intelligence level to provide research, ideas an information in ways they can understand.  It does not expect everyone to elevate to the same level of intelligence.  Therefore, to say it is making people stupid is a generalization that holds no merit.
text_all: Social media is a good channel of information for many who lack the skills to properly research.  Therefore, it is not making people stupid.  In fact, it provides a medium for like minded people to be able to exchange ideas and information.  It provides people of the same intelligence level to provide research, ideas an information in ways they can understand.  It does not expect everyone to elevate to the same level of intelligence.  Therefore, to say it is making people stupid is a generalization that holds no merit. This is only based on the assumption that people will only take the information on social media and run with it.  On the contrary, most people take these ideas as a base foundation for information and elaborate through their own research.  What this does is it provides a starting point for every individual to be able to diversify their arguments in their own way.  Without this starting point, it would be difficult for many to even begin the task of starting a research project, collecting information or simply keeping up with current information. Although it is true that many sources have not been vetted, it is a starting point.  A starting point can be a statement that is true, somewhat true, or absolutely false.  It can also be very broad or very specific.  But the fact remains it provides a starting point for them to research the thesis- think of it as an encouragement of sorts to delve deeper into a topic they want to learn more about.  As with anything, there is misinformation out there- regardless if it is through verbal conversation, classroom or printed media.

text_initial: In general, some form of social programs that ensure safety, shelter, food, and basic needs can be available for all is beneficial.  The problem with these types of programs is that some people feel they can be taken advantage of, and with free benefits, there is not incentive to work.  The good news for the basic income approach is that all citizens receive it, so the typical opponents of such programs will also receive this benefit.  This means they won't feel like they are being taken advantage of, and instead they are also receiving this basic needs level of support.  So you only have to work if you would like a higher standard of living.  The good news is, people will then feel like they are working for the luxuries in life and acquiring more, and not feel like their pay for others.

I think some of this was already addressed in my initial argument, but again the fact that this income is given to all citizens helps with equality and the realization that no one is getting more than anyone else.  In addition, there is already a fair amount of tax dollars going towards social programs that this benefit would utilize funding to be implemented.  Overall, this would eliminate some unnecessary costs associated with managing, monitoring and complying with Social Security, Medicaid, Food Stamps, welfare, unemployment, and others.  This would be a clean sheet approach that fairly, eliminates some of the bureaucracy.   People will be able to choose what they do for work to obtain more in life beyond basic needs.  The ability to pursue work you are passionate about, as opposed to the one that pays the bills will lead to more dedication and innovation.  Overall, some people may choose not to work, but some people choose not to work now and find ways to receive government assistance.  So I do not anticipate this will change that.  In addition, this will lead to a number of opportunities for higher standards of living.  The reality is a basic level of income is not much to those who are extremely wealthy, so it will benefit those who are working and living in poverty and struggling to make ends meet quite a bit.  It will not cause those lower wage earners to want to quit.  It will encourage them as their standard of living will improve drastically.

text_initial: Online learning is NOT a suitable replacement for traditional in-person education. First of all, it is nearly impossible to get children to behave if they are not all in front of you. They can turn off their cameras, not show up to class, and not participate. Plus, I believe that the person to person interactions are important and necessary for proper development and growth. Not having peers to talk to makes people introverted and more antisocial.
text_all: Online learning is NOT a suitable replacement for traditional in-person education. First of all, it is nearly impossible to get children to behave if they are not all in front of you. They can turn off their cameras, not show up to class, and not participate. Plus, I believe that the person to person interactions are important and necessary for proper development and growth. Not having peers to talk to makes people introverted and more antisocial. Flexibility is great, however people, children specifically, need structure. Time management is important, which can be learned via both online education and in person education. However, in person requires much more effort and instills a sense of importance for both time management and education. In person education can also be personalized, depending what area you are studying. Also, there is the ability for in person tutors which individualize these learning objectives even more. Proper management is difficult to instill when you cannot see every student, which is more likely than not the case with online learning. Virtual interaction is not the same as in person interaction. While virtual one might improve on their writing skills, the importance of in person also focuses on verbalization and communication skills, but verbal and nonverbal for this last facet. While online learning is a option for those who cannot be in person, in person education is the best option for growth and developmental skills.

text_initial: Women have historically been discriminated against in the work place and especially in leadership positions. Women are more likely to be overlooked for leadership positions as they are seen to lack the traits that we typically associate with leaders such as being commanding, fearless, always certain, confidence, etc. We typically associated these as masculine traits, which biases these positions towards men, unfairly I might argue as they overlook the other qualities of leadership that anyone can have. To compensate for this unfair gap, it might be useful in the short term to mandate that qualified women be entered into these roles to satisfy a quota. In time, once women in these roles have shown they can handle the job, the unfair biases should become less prevalent.
text_all: Women have historically been discriminated against in the work place and especially in leadership positions. Women are more likely to be overlooked for leadership positions as they are seen to lack the traits that we typically associate with leaders such as being commanding, fearless, always certain, confidence, etc. We typically associated these as masculine traits, which biases these positions towards men, unfairly I might argue as they overlook the other qualities of leadership that anyone can have. To compensate for this unfair gap, it might be useful in the short term to mandate that qualified women be entered into these roles to satisfy a quota. In time, once women in these roles have shown they can handle the job, the unfair biases should become less prevalent. The reality is that we don't exist in a meritocracy, and while we should strive towards equal opportunities, we shouldn't leave those decisions in the hands of those that have typically benefited from denying those opportunities to others in the first place. Discrimination against women isn't just the result of unfair biases, it is also a deliberate exclusion that is justified by said biases and perpetuated by often powerful men who benefit from the status quo. If you want change, you sometimes have to force it and ignore the criticisms of other people whose minds won't be changed until the change actually occurs. Advocacy has only taken us so far, and if we're being frank, you are advocating for essentially the status quo. "Don't change things too much, don't push for too much." If advocacy were enough, women would already be in more considerable levels of power in these institution. The reason they aren't is due to institutional obstacles that are embedded due to centuries of prejudice and bias. Civil Rights only became popular and accepted AFTER The Civil Rights Bill was passed. Sometimes change needs to be forced before it is accepted.

text_initial: Space exploration is a necessary and completely worthwhile investment for humanity. Carbon emissions and human destruction of the environment and the planet have been on the rise since the industrial revolution and are now far past the limit that our planet can handle for much longer. Climate change will soon make our planet unlivable, and space exploration offers a possible way to change that future. Science shows us that research fuels innovation and problem solving, and by exploring space, we may find solutions to the problems we've created on Earth. The possibility of life outside of Earth is inevitable, given the vastness of the universe, and investing in discovery of that life would forever change the course of humanity.
text_all: Space exploration is a necessary and completely worthwhile investment for humanity. Carbon emissions and human destruction of the environment and the planet have been on the rise since the industrial revolution and are now far past the limit that our planet can handle for much longer. Climate change will soon make our planet unlivable, and space exploration offers a possible way to change that future. Science shows us that research fuels innovation and problem solving, and by exploring space, we may find solutions to the problems we've created on Earth. The possibility of life outside of Earth is inevitable, given the vastness of the universe, and investing in discovery of that life would forever change the course of humanity.  While we haven't explored all of Earth's oceans, we know that human life is not a possibility at the depths of the ocean. Besides, an argument for the exploration of the ocean isn't an argument against the exploration of space, and there is no reason why both can't be a worthwhile investment. The possibility that there is another planet that humans could inhabit is in itself a reason to explore the unknown.  I agree that more effort should be put into making our planet more livable and mending some of the damages done by humanity. We should in no way think of space exploration as a way out or a reason to continue to trash our planet, however, the possibility that space can hold complex life forms is a completely worthwhile and necessary investment for humanity. The discovery of life beyond Earth is inevitable, and these discoveries could teach us, not only about other planets and our solar system, but possibly how to heal our own Earth.

text_initial: An outright ban of fossil fuels by the U.S. would have extremely detrimental and far reaching effects. Too much of the global economy, including the developing world, is currently dependent on fossil fuels for basic infrastructure and economic stability. The U.S. itself would find itself in the globally irresponsible position of racing to mine even more precious medals at the expense of the environment and in some cases, human rights all over the world. And this just barely touches the surface of what would happen to the U.s. internally.
text_all: An outright ban of fossil fuels by the U.S. would have extremely detrimental and far reaching effects. Too much of the global economy, including the developing world, is currently dependent on fossil fuels for basic infrastructure and economic stability. The U.S. itself would find itself in the globally irresponsible position of racing to mine even more precious medals at the expense of the environment and in some cases, human rights all over the world. And this just barely touches the surface of what would happen to the U.s. internally. Indeed, transitioning to renewable energy sources, as you suggest, at a very manageable and responsible pace is a viable option. However, banning fossil fuels in the U.S. is an entirely separate argument and is not addressed by your statements. No-one would disagree that there is value in alternative energy, but banning the lynchpin of the current global economy is not the first step. All things in moderation, after all, is a wise and tested path to follow. You have demonstrated my point very well and perhaps, in closing, my smartest position would be to quote your latest statements, "Fossil fuels currently form the backbone of our energy infrastructure, and a sudden cessation would likely lead to severe economic repercussions. Industries and jobs tied to the fossil fuel sector contribute significantly to the national economy, and a hasty ban could result in mass unemployment and economic instability. Additionally, the global nature of the issue requires a coordinated international effort, and a unilateral ban by the U.S. may not significantly impact global carbon emissions, especially considering the substantial reliance of other nations on fossil fuels." Again, an outright ban is not feasible nor the best path to combat climate change.

text_initial: Artificial intelligence is not good for society. The inner workings of AIs are black boxes controlled by a small number of highly influential tech companies. They lack transparency, and the level of technical understanding required to even evaluate transparency is so high that few people in society could do it. This funnels power and influence even further into the hands of a small minority. In addition, AI cannot be guaranteed to act in society's best interests. Even if no nefarious purpose is built into the AI intentionally, this technology can independently present a threat to existing institutions, systems and human life.
text_all: Artificial intelligence is not good for society. The inner workings of AIs are black boxes controlled by a small number of highly influential tech companies. They lack transparency, and the level of technical understanding required to even evaluate transparency is so high that few people in society could do it. This funnels power and influence even further into the hands of a small minority. In addition, AI cannot be guaranteed to act in society's best interests. Even if no nefarious purpose is built into the AI intentionally, this technology can independently present a threat to existing institutions, systems and human life. There are other tools that can achieve the same purposes that are both transparent and incapable of acting independently in a way that is damaging to society. The internet pre-AI was already very effective at collating information via a myriad of human driven tools (such as wikipedia, etc.) that required human oversight and transparency. These tools are also not capable of independently "hallucinating" truths that are total fabrications, which AI does do. In conclusion, AI is not good for society. It operates without transparency, making decisions and functioning in ways even its creators may not understand. It consolidates power into the hands of a technocratic minority and leaves the rest of us subject to the consequences of their decisions. AI has the potential to upend societal norms and systems, as well as to act independently in a way no other technology has been able to.

text_initial: Limiting freedom of speech to prevent hate speech poses significant challenges to democratic principles. Free expression fosters intellectual diversity and societal progress. Combatting hate speech can be better achieved through education and dialogue, preserving the fundamental right to free expression.
text_all: Limiting freedom of speech to prevent hate speech poses significant challenges to democratic principles. Free expression fosters intellectual diversity and societal progress. Defining hate speech is subjective and risks censorship of legitimate opinions. Allowing open dialogue encourages change through constructive engagement. Combatting hate speech can be better achieved through education and dialogue, preserving the fundamental right to free expression. I appreciate the concern expressed by the PRO side about the potential harm caused by unrestrained free speech, particularly in the context of online interactions. However, the CON side contends that limiting freedom of speech, especially to prevent hate speech, raises fundamental concerns. Free expression is a vital element of democracy, fostering intellectual diversity and societal progress. Defining hate speech is subjective and poses risks of censoring legitimate opinions. Open dialogue, rather than restriction, encourages positive change through constructive engagement. It is argued that addressing hate speech can be more effectively achieved through education and dialogue, preserving the essential right to free expression. I appreciate your concern for fostering a positive online environment by limiting hate speech. However, from the CON perspective, the importance of free expression as a cornerstone of democracy cannot be understated. While acknowledging the harms caused by hate speech, defining it is subjective and risks stifling legitimate opinions. The CON side contends that addressing hate speech is better achieved through alternative means, such as education and constructive dialogue, preserving the fundamental right to free expression.

text_initial: I think that standardized testing can have a negative effect on students because they are studying for a test through all these websites and different books instead of gaining knowledge organically. You can have people who do really well on tests just because their memory is strong but actually aren't very good critical thinkers while you can have poor test takers/memorizers that are actually very thoughtful students.
text_all: I think that standardized testing can have a negative effect on students because they are studying for a test through all these websites and different books instead of gaining knowledge organically. You can have people who do really well on tests just because their memory is strong but actually aren't very good critical thinkers while you can have poor test takers/memorizers that are actually very thoughtful students. It's a good measure to see if students have a basis understanding of certain subjects. It's not a perfect system but it's the most sensible option. Like I said before even though you have this system in place that is the best we currently have it doesn't make it a perfect system that shouldn't be updated. We need to figure out a way to actually test students critical thinking skills and not simply if they are good test takers.

text_initial: The USA should keep using the electoral college in president elections because it functions in a way that allows fair representation for both states of all sizes. In this way, it truly captures the meaning of having a democratic government. To get rid of this system would lead to a neglect of less populous places in the country. The electoral college is in place to protect the interests of ALL states in this country.
text_all: The USA should keep using the electoral college in president elections because it functions in a way that allows fair representation for both states of all sizes. In this way, it truly captures the meaning of having a democratic government. To get rid of this system would lead to a neglect of less populous places in the country. The electoral college is in place to protect the interests of ALL states in this country.  While the disconnect from the popular vote is a valid concern, this method is a much more cumbersome and controversial way of deciding a winner. The electoral college significantly reduces the chance of recounts and allows for a much clearer race. I would also argue that the electoral college actually discourages extreme viewpoints  As I mentioned previously, the popular vote is too cumbersome and unreliable to decide the presidency. The electoral college protects the interest of both the state and the people by allowing the majority of people in each state to decide their vote. In theory, I understand that the popular vote seems like the most democratic way to determine the presidency, but in practice, it is a lot less feasible.

text_initial: Although there are good reasons to tax the rich, there are also cons that we must consider. Firstly, increasing taxes can lead to more social divisiveness and a class war situation. Higher taxes also does not signify an efficient increase in government aid and programs. For business and corporations, there is a higher potential in tax evasion and avoidance. As wev'e heard in the news, there already administrative burdens on taxing authorities. Would we even have enough to cover this new feat? If we keep taxing the rich, there is also a possibility of them moving to other countries just to avoid the tax. All in all, there are many things to consider and we would need a great plan before we decide that the rich should pay more taxes.
text_all: Although there are good reasons to tax the rich, there are also cons that we must consider. Firstly, increasing taxes can lead to more social divisiveness and a class war situation. Higher taxes also does not signify an efficient increase in government aid and programs. For business and corporations, there is a higher potential in tax evasion and avoidance. As wev'e heard in the news, there already administrative burdens on taxing authorities. Would we even have enough to cover this new feat? If we keep taxing the rich, there is also a possibility of them moving to other countries just to avoid the tax. All in all, there are many things to consider and we would need a great plan before we decide that the rich should pay more taxes. Although it is true that people who make more should pay more in taxes to help wealth distribution, there are currently no efficient systems in place that can be able to help make that possible. The government is already overburdened that even when there is funding for projects, it does not mean it will get done. We need to revise these systems and ensure the money will be well spent. In that way, the rich can see that there money is not going to waste and is actually in good use. Giving them more of an incentive to want to pay taxes. I think spending more efforts in one place can be a great idea in theory but realistically it's not there. We are amidst the worst economic state in the country due to inflation, gas prices, job market, housing market etc. We need to focus on putting legal systems in place that can help with all this and therefore distribute the wealth. Adding more money will not just fix everything. I agree that the rich get richer and that they must be taxed appropriately however, before we do so we must look at specifics. Right now, the rich should not pay more taxes until we've handled everything appropriately and ensure that the funding will land in the right place.

text_initial: I feel as though if we continue to aid Ukraine, we may trigger a war with Russia. We only got out of the Cold War in the 90s, and our relationship with Russia is tenuous, at best. Worse, we have had a new issue with Russia since we found out they were using social media to influence our elections, via Cambridge Analytica. \n\nWe simply can't afford to have more problems with Russia. We need to keep peace with them. We need to stay out of Ukraine. If we don't, we may literally end the world.
text_all:
I feel as though if we continue to aid Ukraine, we may trigger a war with Russia. We only got out of the Cold War in the 90s, and our relationship with Russia is tenuous, at best. Worse, we have had a new issue with Russia since we found out they were using social media to influence our elections, via Cambridge Analytica. 

We simply can't afford to have more problems with Russia. We need to keep peace with them. We need to stay out of Ukraine. If we don't, we may literally end the world. While it's true that they need to be put in their place, Ukraine is not the place to do it. We have managed to get Ukraine to the place they are now, and they've even crafted ways to defend themselves heartily, even without large scale weapons. It's even more non-sensical, when you consider we are also helping Israel, who is on any side of Russia. 

We should focus on extracting ourselves from all of these wars. The thing is, we've intervened on ALL the people currently involved in war. We've sent our weapons to almost every continent on the planet, and for what? We're still being asked to send more. It never ends. 

Russia may be coming for us, but it makes more sense to hold onto our own weapons, on the off chance that they attack us directly. Sending them to the Ukraine front isn't smart.

text_initial: If we enact more regulations around voting, what we are doing is basically making it harder for many people to vote.   The more complex and convoluted the rules for voting are, the more people who will decide to just not bother.  We need to make voting easier - we need things like automatic voter registration when people get their driver's license, and we need to keep the vote-by-mail process open  the way it is - we don't need to shut people out.
text_all: If we enact more regulations around voting, what we are doing is basically making it harder for many people to vote.   The more complex and convoluted the rules for voting are, the more people who will decide to just not bother.  We need to make voting easier - we need things like automatic voter registration when people get their driver's license, and we need to keep the vote-by-mail process open  the way it is - we don't need to shut people out.      Election integrity is important, it's true, but we already have the protection in place to make sure that only the people who are supposed to vote do so.    Voting by mail is very secure - in fact, those people who tried to vote multiple times (usually Republicans!) are caught and prosecuted.    If someone isn't an American Citizen, they don't vote - I'm not sure why there is such a worry that foreigners are voting!! I don't agree with you.   There is a big problem with Republicans redrawing district lines and making voting more difficult for minorities.   True, if someone is set on voting, they will do what they can to make sure they cast their ballot.  But if you make it hard - no- near impossible - to vote, there is nothing that someone can do.  There is nothing wrong with voting by mail - it makes it possible for people who can't get off work or who can't make it to the polls to vote.

text_initial: ‘Art’ is a broad term which encompasses all areas of creativity, and doesn’t refer simply to visual arts. Whilst science is often considered as fact driven, art and design can provide an opportunity to explore concepts and theories in a more engaging way – merging life learning skills with comprehension of principles and practice. Art is as important as mathematics and sciences.

This is known as the neuroscience premise, which states that scientific thinking is stimulated when learners undertake artistic activity – and this is why art is just as important as science when it comes to education.  Critical thinking is a key aspect of success in science, but whilst this provides people with the ability to grow decision making and vertically operated critical skills, it does not provide so many benefits in terms of innovation. Being artistic means, being able to relate science to the world.

text_initial: It's irrational to believe that there could ever be international enforcement of climate change policies. Different areas of the world need different things, and not all countries will benefit from one blanket policy.
text_all: It's irrational to believe that there could ever be international enforcement of climate change policies. Different areas of the world need different things, and not all countries will benefit from one blanket policy. While global warming is a global issue, different geographical areas require different policies. For example, policies surrounding factory pollution wouldn't benefit a rural area. All nations deserve to make their own individual policies for global warming that will best suit their environmental needs.  Each country is unique in its environmental needs. It would be very difficult for all nations of the world to agree on a singular climate change policy, especially when climate varies so much throughout the world. I believe climate change policies should be enacted, but they should be done with environmental differences in mind,

text_initial: Government surveillance is necessary to protect American lives because unless the governmental agencies around the country are aware of what is happening within our borders, they will never be able to stop potential threats. Think what could be accomplished in the way of stopping white supremacy like the Proud Boys if we knew what they were saying and more importantly, what they were planning.

Mass shootings and terror attacks like what happened on January 6 2021 would never need be an issue if the government is able to properly look out for the warning signs. Surveillance isn't simply some "Big Brother" type problem. This assumes no oversight on the governmental agencies responsible for the surveilling. With the proper regulation, private citizens private information, communication, and data are safe from the government. This only furthers my point. We already operate under a surveillance state, whether you realize it or not. The question remains who is getting surveilled and who is protected from the watchful eye of those that serve to protect us all. Putting the right checks in place to ensure that only those who are flagged as threats are looked at with the scrutiny they deserve.

text_initial: The US should not keep it as it is a relic of a colonial past. It was made when it was assumed the people were not educated in these matters and didn't have ready access to the information. When the popular vote is not winning there is an obvious problem in the process. The electoral college also leaves room to disenfranchise voters. This is due to gerrymandering.
text_all: The US should not keep it as it is a relic of a colonial past. It was made when it was assumed the people were not educated in these matters and didn't have ready access to the information. We should switch to using the popular vote to avoid and account for gerrymandering.

text_initial: The United States should have mandatory national service for all able-bodied, coming-of-age citizens.  The US's power - and by extension, the privileges and protections it grants its citizens - stems from its foreign relations and notable military power.  All citizens should be responsible for contributing to maintaining the US's power as it benefits the entire country. This is not dissimilar to other expectations held for citizens, including that 1) citizens follow the laws of the country to maintain a sense of peace and order for everyone, and 2) citizens pay taxes to contribute to public goods that benefit all.
text_all: The United States should have mandatory national service for all able-bodied, coming-of-age citizens.  The US's power - and by extension, the privileges and protections it grants its citizens - stems from its foreign relations and notable military power.  All citizens should be responsible for contributing to maintaining the US's power as it benefits the entire country. This is not dissimilar to other expectations held for citizens, including that 1) citizens follow the laws of the country to maintain a sense of peace and order for everyone, and 2) citizens pay taxes to contribute to public goods that benefit all.  My opponent appears to argue that the US should not have mandatory national service as, in the past, mandatory service has been used toward immoral ends (e.g., imperialism). However, nonmandatory service has often been used toward immoral ends, and the current nature of the American military means that servicemembers can sign up with "good" intentions and still be pulled into an "immoral" war. Thus, mandatory versus nonmandatory service does not stop one from participating in immoral actions on behalf of the country. Rather, we should create checks and balances that stop our country from using our military servicemembers for actions that citizens do not agree with, such as imperialistic and expansionist policies.  It is not possible to completely separate the impact of the US's global power on citizen's well-being versus the impact of the US's global power on political standing. They are inherently connected. Because the US is seen as a major - if not the foremost - world power, its citizens benefit immensely as far as access to global resources, education, and power.

text_initial: Animals should not be used for scientific research as they cannot consent to these experiments. To argue that it's okay to test on them for human gain depends on the assumption that humans are in some way superior. Animals have just as much right to avoid pain and suffering as we do.
text_all: Animals should not be used for scientific research as they cannot consent to these experiments. To argue that it's okay to test on them for human gain depends on the assumption that humans are in some way superior. Animals have just as much right to avoid pain and suffering as we do.  How do the benefits far outweigh the ethical concerns? Assuming our place on earth and the bodies we inhabit is essentially a lottery, would you agree with your own statements if you were a thinking, feeling dog born into a laboratory? Or a pig with the intelligence of a young child, as well as the need for social bonds and community?  For millennia, humans have existed alongside animals without keeping them trapped in laboratories. One could argue that living harmoniously with them, and not subjecting them to suffering, actually improves life for humans, as well. Living lightly on this earth, with compassion, improves our emotional well-being, which can in turn have excellent benefits for our health. And I would argue "the greater good" includes animal well-being as much as it does human health.

text_initial: Public College should not be tuition free because there are other projects that are more valuable to our society than "free college". Tax dollars should be used for greater causes. Public transportation, making cities more walkable, and public health prevention is where our tax dollars should be going.

Public transportation, making cities more walkable, and public health prevention is where our tax dollars should be going.  The students ability to afford college tuition doesn't fall on the state to pay. There are plenty of scholarships available for students that want to pursue a higher education. A student's lack of effort in exploring these options should not fall on the state for a solution.  In conclusion, Public college should be able to charge tuition. Allowing everyone to get public college paid for will leave a lot of valuable public projects un-attended. These projects benefit society as a whole not just young students. It would be beneficial to invest in projects that improve society as a whole.

text_initial: Ensuring fair elections is key to citizens trusting their elected institutions. This allows corruption to happen at a greater and greater scale further eroding our democracy. Ensuring fair elections will reinforce trust in our democratic system, getting more and better candidates to run for office and allowing us to improve our system. Either way can be a runaway train, one rail going towards collapse and the other going towards prosperity.

text_initial: It is important to the advancement of humankind that we focus on exploring space. For direct benefits, there will eventually be the colonization of new parts of space, allowing for much more land for humans to live and grow in, as well as natural resources that can be acquired in space. For indirect benefits, there are numerous technologies that are and have already been developed in pursuit of space that have had use outside of that direct application. It is an expensive endeavor, but ideally mankind would be able to work together so that we can all benefit in the path to space exploration.
text_all: It is important to the advancement of humankind that we focus on exploring space. For direct benefits, there will eventually be the colonization of new parts of space, allowing for much more land for humans to live and grow in, as well as natural resources that can be acquired in space. For indirect benefits, there are numerous technologies that are and have already been developed in pursuit of space that have had use outside of that direct application. It is an expensive endeavor, but ideally mankind would be able to work together so that we can all benefit in the path to space exploration. It is entirely possible to craft a budget that allocates a reasonable amount towards space exploration while funding the remainder of our commitments. Spending can also be cut (if necessary) from non-domestic spending and an overly expanded foreign policy plan to offset the spending on space research.\n\nIn regard to running into hostile life, that wouldn't be an issue for any reasonable period of time, since no hospitable planets that could support life have been located anywhere nearby. Ultimately, space exploration and research are something that started as a competition between the United States and USSR, with both sides fighting each other non-militarily to be the first to the moon. The drive to be the first to the moon led to many devices that might never have been developed without this pressure: solar cells, water filtration systems, satellites, improved tire technology and many others. These were not the goal, but they were created along the way.

text_initial: I think that all leadership positions should be filled based on merit.  If the best person for a particular position is a woman, then she should be chosen.  If not, then it should go to a man.  Using quotas inevitably creates problems, including the hiring of unqualified people, resentment by those who are qualified, and mediocrity.  Always choosing the best person leads to the best possible results.  Quotas should never be used to fill positions.
text_all: I think that all leadership positions should be filled based on merit.  If the best person for a particular position is a woman, then she should be chosen.  If not, then it should go to a man.  Using quotas inevitably creates problems, including the hiring of unqualified people, resentment by those who are qualified, and mediocrity.  Always choosing the best person leads to the best possible results.  Quotas should never be used to fill positions. The gap between what is possible for a woman, and what is possible for a man (in their careers) has become smaller and smaller over time, to the point where it is now essentially the same.  The Vice President of The United States is a woman.  The anchor on CBS Evening News is a woman.  The head of Twitter is a woman.  No quotas are needed. Using quotas may seem like it is the pathway to being fair, but it is really the pathway to unfair treatment of those who are the most deserving and qualified.  Using quotas leads to the placement of incompetent people.  I have seen this happen at several companies that I have worked for.  It leads to problems that can be time-consuming and costly.  Always choose the best person.

text_initial: I think online learning can be a suitable replacement for traditional in-person education, but only for certain groups of learners who are amenable to the online format, as well as only for certain fields of study. Obviously, certain subjects like the physical sciences, where one has to perform experiments, are practically impossible (at least currently) to do online. But any subject where there isn't a huge need for tactile learning, and mostly relies on visual or audio learning, can benefit from an online space. This proposition also depends on what we mean by "suitable replacement" -- as in, is it just merely sufficient, or is it actually better/an improvement? I think those are two different claims that require two different defenses. I'm just here to defend the claim that it's merely sufficient or on par with traditional in-person education for some people in some subjects.
text_all: I think online learning can be a suitable replacement for traditional in-person education, but only for certain groups of learners who are amenable to the online format, as well as only for certain fields of study. Obviously, certain subjects like the physical sciences, where one has to perform experiments, are practically impossible (at least currently) to do online. But any subject where there isn't a huge need for tactile learning, and mostly relies on visual or audio learning, can benefit from an online space. This proposition also depends on what we mean by "suitable replacement" -- as in, is it just merely sufficient, or is it actually better/an improvement? I think those are two different claims that require two different defenses. I'm just here to defend the claim that it's merely sufficient or on par with traditional in-person education for some people in some subjects. I think you meant to say "it is not possible* to sense this..." I think we have to delve a bit deeper and go a bit more fine-grained on exactly which subjects/educational topics require or significantly benefit from tactile/hands on learning. I think we will mostly disagree on the amount of topics/subjects that require this, and disagree on exactly how significant this benefit is. I also contend that while subtle, non-verbal communcation is important from educators, there are also the opposite cases where certain learners don't perform well with in-person environments (autistic peoeple, socially anxious peopkl I think ultimately, it would depend on the subject in question, and the type of learners. I also have to mention other benefits, such as ease of use (learners no longer have to spend time getting ready to look presentable, go outside, ride transportation, etc. -- all of which wastes time and energy). It is the same argument why many say working from home reduces pressure and improves work efficiency and quality -- some people can genuinely learn better from the comfort of their own home. We can also extend this argument to the myriad of disabled people who will also benefit.

text_initial:
I do not wish to categorically-deride Artificial Intelligence as "bad" for society. 

Any tool can be bad, if a person wishes to apply for it bad purposes.

Fire can help us make delicious, hot meals..
..Or Fire can raze an entire city (if not, the entire world).


One drawback of Artificial Intelligence is, it will help facilitate the loss of more jobs — employers, who rather "save on costs."

Another drawback is, AI can contribute towards *natural* intelligence's dwindling, the more people rely on it, instead of their own brains.
text_all:
I do not wish to categorically-deride Artificial Intelligence as "bad" for society. 

Any tool can be bad, if a person wishes to apply for it bad purposes.

Fire can help us make delicious, hot meals..
..Or Fire can raze an entire city (if not, the entire world).


One drawback of Artificial Intelligence is, it will help facilitate the loss of more jobs — employers, who rather "save on costs."

Another drawback is, AI can contribute towards *natural* intelligence's dwindling, the more people rely on it, instead of their own brains. AI can only learn by what we tell it.
Humans are the ones who must be the curators of Artificial Intelligence: not the AI, itself.

AI can only take actions which humans programme it to replicate.

If there ever comes a point when AI may equate human capability of Independent Thought, it has to be because a human finally managed to figure out how to programme such a feat.

AI is to be our supplement — not our replacement.

At some point, Humans will need to return to the fray. Indeed: only a person's bad wishes may make the AI tool bad.

Well..
..Nothing says, only Good People can wield tools.

Nothing cements the edict, that tools can only be used for good.

That, AI may only operate to our benefit.

The protocol which robots are supposed to follow, may be followed only when the creator deems it so.


No.
AI is not categorically-bad.
But, AI is not categorically-good, either.

Some have already forecast an AI takeover.
I'm not sure if that's a bad thing or a good thing.

Online learning allows people to work around their work schedules and continue the education.\n\nAdditionally, most classes these days rely less on route memorization, because with the advent of the internet, this type of learning is no longer necessary.  Old school testing is out because it was based on this type of learning.  Now adays essays and tests where have to have the skills to look up information is more practical. text_all:
In the modern world, and with the rising cost of education, any type of education that allows people to study and partake on their own terms and for less money is a good thing.  The average learner is no longer 18 years old.  Now it is people looking to increase their education for work advancement or for new careers.  Online learning allows people to work around their work schedules and continue the education.

Additionally, most classes these days rely less on route memorization, because with the advent of the internet, this type of learning is no longer necessary.  Old school testing is out because it was based on this type of learning.  Now adays essays and tests where have to have the skills to look up information is more practical.  Additionally, very few professions are not utilizing computers to do the work, so learning on a computer is likely a contributable skill.   Most online education is aimed at adult learners, not k-12.  

Online learning for young kids does make sure they feel part of a group.  There is a lot of emphasis put on contributing to chat rooms, critiquing each others work, and doing presentations to the class.  Online can be live as well where all the kids have their cameras on and can speak to each other.  In some instances, kids have a better experience being part of a group online when they can't participate in an in person school.  For some kids who are excluded at an in person school, the only group they can be accepted in is an online one Response: Real humans are all that online learning is.  Classes are not taught or taken by AI.  Teachers create the lessons, assign the work, and grade the work.  Additionally, in most classes the students are expected to review each others work as well.  There are often group projects assigned for the students as well.

Making education accessible for all is more important than making it in person, even if in person was better, which it isn't.

text_initial: Packing the Supreme Court is a dangerous game because of the precedent it would set.  Once a side has broken the taboo and packed a court, the other party will consider court-packing to be fair game and do it as well, as soon as they are in power.  Before too long, you would likely have an overly bloated court that was even more politicized than before (which is terrifying, since the current court is very overtly political).
text_all: Packing the Supreme Court is a dangerous game because of the precedent it would set.  Once a side has broken the taboo and packed a court, the other party will consider court-packing to be fair game and do it as well, as soon as they are in power.  Before too long, you would likely have an overly bloated court that was even more politicized than before (which is terrifying, since the current court is very overtly political).   Packing the court would lead to nothing but overtly political appointments.  Both parties would only appoint the most fervent believers of their dogma to the court.  Granted, the GOP is already doing that, but keeping the court at it's current size gives others a fighting chance at eventually getting appointments that are qualified rather than political.  Packing the court would give niether side a reason to do t The potential problem with this logic is that it would never stop.  Every time a different party took the presidency they would try to repack the court.  Maybe congress would be pushed into some form of court reform at some point, but how much damage would be done in the meantime?  Congress, generally speaking, is notoriously slow to respond to vital issues.
