text_initial: Yes, international agreements are essential for addressing global climate change. They provide a framework for cooperation, goal-setting, and accountability among nations. Without such agreements, it's challenging to make meaningful progress in reducing greenhouse gas emissions and adapting to climate change's impacts.
text_all: Yes, international agreements are essential for addressing global climate change. They provide a framework for cooperation, goal-setting, and accountability among nations. Without such agreements, it's challenging to make meaningful progress in reducing greenhouse gas emissions and adapting to climate change's impacts. While previous international agreements may not have fully halted climate change, they've laid groundwork and increased awareness. The failures of past agreements highlight the need for stronger commitments and enforcement mechanisms. Instead of dismissing international cooperation, we should improve and build upon existing frameworks to address the urgent global climate crisis effectively. International agreements are indeed just one part of the solution to climate change, but they play a crucial role in fostering cooperation and setting common goals. While past agreements may not have fully reversed climate change, they have laid the groundwork for collective action. We need both international agreements and tangible actions to address this urgent global crisis.

text_initial: The rich need to pay more taxes in order for society to function. There is tremendous wealth inequity around the world. The difference between the living standards between the poor and rich is staggering and only getting worse. This disparity cannot last as the poor will need to rise up to stay afloat.\n\nOnly by doing the moral thing of collecting more money from the very well-off can we maintain dignity and life for the poor. The difference between the living standards between the poor and rich is staggering and only getting worse. This disparity cannot last as the poor will need to rise up to stay afloat.

Only by doing the moral thing of collecting more money from the very well-off can we maintain dignity and life for the poor. Taxes are the right and ethical solution. As you point out, rich people do not depend on their income for actual living expenses. Therefore, a tax on that income will not degrade their living standards. For the poor people and countries that will benefit from the programs supported by taxes, however, it could mean the difference between life and death! Glad you have come to the understanding that the most rich people are insanely rich. Their money is way beyond what is necessary to live even an extravagant life. 

Taxing these rich people more will enable the poor to regain dignity and make themselves into a more productive class.

text_initial: Standardized testing does two things.  It tracks progress of students and it tracks the skill of the teachers. I would not want my doctor not to be tested and certified or my plumber for that matter. It is not perfect but it is the best that we have developed so far.

text_initial: One argument against this proposition is that paying government officials the minimum wage may not attract the most qualified and talented individuals to public service. Running for elected office or serving as a government appointee can be a time-consuming and demanding job, and individuals who are skilled and experienced in their fields may be less likely to serve if they are not compensated fairly for their time and expertise.
text_all: One argument against this proposition is that paying government officials the minimum wage may not attract the most qualified and talented individuals to public service. Running for elected office or serving as a government appointee can be a time-consuming and demanding job, and individuals who are skilled and experienced in their fields may be less likely to serve if they are not compensated fairly for their time and expertise.    The opponent argues that paying government officials the minimum wage helps them to better understand the financial struggles faced by many people and that this in turn helps them to make and enforce laws and policies that benefit the majority of people. However, this argument overlooks the fact that government officials are already expected to make decisions that are in the best interests of their constituents, regardless of their financial situation.\n Paying the minimum wage would at least ensure that government officials are not earning exorbitant salaries that are not commensurate with the average citizen's income. This would help to reduce the perception of corruption and cronyism, as officials would not be able to justify such high salaries without providing tangible benefits to the public.

text_initial:

Social media companies need to be regulated to eliminate hate speech on their platforms. They have shown that they cannot be trusted to regulate themselves because it goes against their profit making interests.

To me, this topic is very simple: you can't yell fire in an auditorium and expect not consequences for your actions.

To me, this topic is very simple: you can't yell fire in an auditorium and expect not consequences for your actions. We have come a long way since the American Revolution. We are no longer at the mercy of the British Empire. It is the responsibility of our government to protect citizens from a few actors such as social media or media companies in general. Without some limits on freedom of speech we are exposing our population, especially our youngest, to dangerous ideas that promote hate and violence. The government is always a few years behind when it comes to advancements of any kind in society. Donald Trump was hateful before he was treasonous.  Give a man with crazy ideas a platform and you get a divided society where citizens hate each other for no good reason other than being preached negative sentiments.

text_initial: The Electoral College should no longer be used. It only serves to uphold an undemocratic system where some voters have much more sway over who gets to become the next president, and others, like Republicans in California or Democrats in Texas, have no say in who becomes the next president. Further, the disuniformity in how states allocate their electoral votes only creates a messier process, with some states giving winner take all and others dividing votes by district. The purpose of the electors themselves is also moot, as the issue of "faithless" electors only causes more issues, especially when they can't even be used for their original purpose. Finally, the process of certifying the electors has been threatened as a political process and is now a point of weakness in eliciting a constitutional crisis.
text_all:
The Electoral College should no longer be used. It only serves to uphold an undemocratic system where some voters have much more sway over who gets to become the next president, and others, like Republicans in California or Democrats in Texas, have no say in who becomes the next president. Further, the disuniformity in how states allocate their electoral votes only creates a messier process, with some states giving winner take all and others dividing votes by district. The purpose of the electors themselves is also moot, as the issue of "faithless" electors only causes more issues, especially when they can't even be used for their original purpose. Finally, the process of certifying the electors has been threatened as a political process and is now a point of weakness in eliciting a constitutional crisis. The first point is a non sequitur, as it doesn't ensure each state "retains a voice", it gives disproportional representation to smaller states, but they are still "trumped" by states with larger populations.

You argue against a strawman that a "pure popular vote" is the only alternative. A more proportional or an automatic elector assignment are other possible changes.

It also does not build consensus when there is a split with the popular vote, fostering resentment. The focus on states over people is an argument to be made, but eliminating the voice of people in states where they are in a solid minority has not been addressed, which was the larger point.

The fact that faithless electors cannot affect the outcome of an election proves the point that appointing electors has no purpose.

Keeping the EC disenfranchises more.

text_initial:
Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico have large populations with millions of residents who lack full voting representation in the U.S. Congress. Granting them statehood would ensure equal representation for their citizens, aligning with the democratic principles of equal rights and representation.
Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico have large populations with millions of residents who lack full voting representation in the U.S. Congress. Granting them statehood would ensure equal representation for their citizens, aligning with the democratic principles of equal rights and representation.
Statehood for Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico would empower their residents to govern themselves more independently, make local decisions that directly impact their communities, and have greater control over their economic, social, and political destinies.
Both Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico have a long history of advocating for statehood and seeking equal rights and recognition within the United States. Granting them statehood would be a step towards rectifying historical disparities and honoring their aspirations for full inclusion in the American political system.
In conclusion, granting statehood to Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico aligns with principles of democracy, representation, self-determination, and equality, providing their residents with the full rights and responsibilities inherent in U.S. statehood.
text_all:
Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico have large populations with millions of residents who lack full voting representation in the U.S. Congress. Granting them statehood would ensure equal representation for their citizens, aligning with the democratic principles of equal rights and representation.
Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico have large populations with millions of residents who lack full voting representation in the U.S. Congress. Granting them statehood would ensure equal representation for their citizens, aligning with the democratic principles of equal rights and representation.
Statehood for Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico would empower their residents to govern themselves more independently, make local decisions that directly impact their communities, and have greater control over their economic, social, and political destinies.
Both Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico have a long history of advocating for statehood and seeking equal rights and recognition within the United States. Granting them statehood would be a step towards rectifying historical disparities and honoring their aspirations for full inclusion in the American political system.
In conclusion, granting statehood to Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico aligns with principles of democracy, representation, self-determination, and equality, providing their residents with the full rights and responsibilities inherent in U.S. statehood. While it is true that Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico may predominantly vote Democrat, the decision to grant statehood should not be based on political calculations alone. It is about upholding democratic principles, ensuring equal representation, and providing full citizenship rights to all residents, regardless of their political affiliations. Both regions have long sought statehood, reflecting the aspirations of their residents for self-determination and fuller participation in American democracy. Denying their rights to statehood overlooks their historical struggles and diminishes their quest for equality and recognition within the U.S.
Statehood for Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico can bring economic, social, and political benefits to the country. It allows these regions to have a stronger voice in national affairs, promotes economic development, and fosters a sense of belonging and empowerment among residents.

text_initial: Trans people should not be allowed to used restrooms based on their gender identity. It makes no sense, we have bathrooms in society for men and women and families. Trans people should have their own bathrooms. Not be forced to use the ones that are assigned to specific genders, leaving those genders to question their political affiliations and sexuality. Instead, it would make the most sense to create an additional bathroom space in every venue and public space for trans people specifically to be able to use the facilities, just as society provides additional space for families or those with special needs. When there is an accessibility issue in public spaces, the best option is to create an additional space so that everyone can maintain their safety and boundaries. These trans individuals should not be made to use the bathroom with cisgendered individuals who could hold prejudice or behave discriminately towards them.

text_initial: I believe that everyone deserves a decent quality of life, and in order for that to be possible, it is necessary that everyone be provided with a basic income from the government. Providing a basic income will drastically cut back on the need for current social support programs. It will also allow people to continue to live while working at a job that may not pay much as they look for better opportunities. It will also help the economy by allowing people to spend money that they otherwise wouldn't have, and by doing so, supporting local businesses and allowing them to grow.  In conclusion, a basic income provided by the government would be beneficial to the population and the country as a whole.
text_all: I believe that everyone deserves a decent quality of life, and in order for that to be possible, it is necessary that everyone be provided with a basic income from the government. Providing a basic income will drastically cut back on the need for current social support programs. It will also allow people to continue to live while working at a job that may not pay much as they look for better opportunities. It will also help the economy by allowing people to spend money that they otherwise wouldn't have, and by doing so, supporting local businesses and allowing them to grow.  In conclusion, a basic income provided by the government would be beneficial to the population and the country as a whole. Providing a basic income would be beneficial to the economy in several ways. It would allow many current social programs to be cut or greatly reduced, putting the money currently used for those purposes back into government coffers. It would increase the power of the dollar by allowing the ability of people to spend within their communities and providing more opportunity for local and national businesses, allowing them to grow. Those businesses would in turn invest back into the community. Many people earn much less than $1000 per month. So much less that it lands them in poverty, leading them to be unable to afford a place to live, medical care, food to eat, clothes to wear. It turns into a vicious cycle that holds them in poverty. They may not have the ability, training, or available childcare to get another job. There may not be another job available to them.

text_initial: The government is ballooning in size, getting into debt, taxes should be all capped at 10% and if that isn't enough then it means that the government should start living withing its means and cut back wasteful spending.\n\nUsually the rich has their wealth in investments (buy, borrow, die) and unrealized gains, trying to come up with a system to tax that will cause more problems than it solves.
text_all:
The government is ballooning in size, getting into debt, taxes should be all capped at 10% and if that isn't enough then it means that the government should start living withing its means and cut back wasteful spending.

Usually the rich has their wealth in investments (buy, borrow, die) and unrealized gains, trying to come up with a system to tax that will cause more problems than it solves. Most of the taxes go on government wasteful spending, I'm all for funding programs that help the needy but that's something that can be done with the current tax code if the government learns how to live within its means, stop funding foreign wars and useless programs. 

Also, laws to tax the rich will find their way to tax the middle class even more. The only way to help the middle class is to cap taxes (10% is a good figure), as mentioned before, the rich has their wealth tied to unrealized gains, trying to "fairly" tax those will also affect investments that people in the middle class do.

Also, the "fair share" that everyone keeps repeating is not defined, the goal posts will be moved again again until a satisfying definition of "fair" is achieved.

text_initial: Online learning is a lot cheaper than traditional learning. Education costs have skyrocketed in recent years, faster than most basic necessities. Its not great for students to have a lot of debt because it will make it harder to enter the workforce.\n\nMost knowledge you need for a job anyways is not learned from school. You get most of your knowledge from work experience. Traditional schooling doesn't make any sense when you consider the lack of utility. Also, with the intervention of the internet, it is a lot easier to be an autodidact than paying so much money to go to school.
text_all:
Online learning is a lot cheaper than traditional learning. Education costs have skyrocketed in recent years, faster than most basic necessities. It doesn't make any sense to send children to a traditional since they'll gain enormous amounts of debt. Its not great for students to have a lot of debt because it will make it harder to enter the workforce.

Most knowledge you need for a job anyways is not learned from school. You get most of your knowledge from work experience. Traditional schooling doesn't make any sense when you consider the lack of utility. Also, with the intervention of the internet, it is a lot easier to be an autodidact than paying so much money to go to school.  Working with peers happens best when you're working at a job. When you have a job, you actually build the skills you need to work with people. This isn't possible at traditional schools because courses are structured in a way emphasizes paternalism instead of independence and critical thinking - which are necessary at a job. It is true it is possible to make traditional schooling more accessible, however, that would be much more costly and time consuming to do. It would require billions of dollars in government spending to subsidize it, a radical structuring to tenure, and numerous laws reform occupational licensing so that can the supply of teachers can keep up with the of professors and teachers.

Online teaching doesn't have these barriers.

text_initial: There has been much debate in recent years since the covid-19 pandemic whether the quality of an online education is a suitable replacement for in-person instruction. With a few exceptions, I argue that in general it is an acceptable alternative to in-person learning. Professors are generally able to deliver identical course content, hold class synchronously or asynchronously, and test students on their knowledge with safeguards available to protect against cheating. The exceptions to this rule are programs which require a great deal of hands-on instruction such as chemistry or biology. I argue that online instruction also prepares students for the modern day working environment as many jobs have transitioned to a work from home format.
text_all: There has been much debate in recent years since the covid-19 pandemic whether the quality of an online education is a suitable replacement for in-person instruction. With a few exceptions, I argue that in general it is an acceptable alternative to in-person learning. Professors are generally able to deliver identical course content, hold class synchronously or asynchronously, and test students on their knowledge with safeguards available to protect against cheating. The exceptions to this rule are programs which require a great deal of hands-on instruction such as chemistry or biology. I argue that online instruction also prepares students for the modern day working environment as many jobs have transitioned to a work from home format.  While your argument highlights some issues that can arise in an online learning environment, there are ways to mitigate these issues. Students can meet with professors online 1 on 1, professors can ensure they are providing an adequate amount of feedback, and many jobs are now online so working in this format in college actually prepares them for such an environment. Requiring a cameras on policy can also reduce concerns about engagement and interaction. As long as these issues are mitigated and students who require additional supports are given those supports through online learning, this allows many more people to attend university who may be unable to due to time and/or distance con Many jobs have moved to an online format, and the only social interaction that occurs is through a digital format, or during occasional in-office days or conferences. I argue that distance learning prepares students to work in tis type of environment. Should online learning be the only option? Of course not, but the choice to utilize a digital option allows for more people who may be unable to attend university in a traditional format the opportunity to participate. Testing in an online environment and in person format are largely the same, so real world applicability issue isn't something unique to online learning.

text_all: Abortion should not be legal because human life ought to be preserved and protected by the state. Included in human life are the unborn. And while I can sympathize with women who feel unprepared or nervous about pregnancy, that sympathy does not override my belief that unborn life must not be killed. The state should certainly spare no expense on helping women through tragic situations like this, to ensure that both she and the child are well taken care of after the birth.

text_initial:
Space exploration is undeniably a worthwhile investment for humanity. Firstly, it drives technological innovation. Many of the technologies we use in our daily lives, such as GPS, satellite communication, and medical imaging, have their roots in space exploration research and development. By investing in space exploration, we push the boundaries of what's possible technologically, leading to advancements that benefit society as a whole.

Furthermore, space exploration fosters international collaboration and cooperation. Projects like the International Space Station (ISS) bring together scientists, engineers, and astronauts from various countries, promoting diplomacy and understanding across borders. This collaboration not only enhances our ability to explore space but also strengthens global partnerships for addressing common challenges on Earth.

Space exploration also expands our understanding of the universe and our place within it. Through missions to other planets, moons, and beyond, we gain insights into planetary science, astrobiology, and the fundamental laws of physics. These discoveries not only satisfy our innate curiosity but also have practical implications, such as understanding the potential for life beyond Earth and assessing threats from asteroids or comets.

Moreover, investing in space exploration inspires the next generation of scientists, engineers, and explorers. The pursuit of space exploration captures the imagination of people around the world, encouraging students to pursue careers in STEM fields and pushing humanity forward through innovation and discovery.

In summary, space exploration offers numerous benefits to humanity, from driving technological innovation and fostering international cooperation to expanding our understanding of the universe and inspiring future generations. As we continue to invest in space exploration, we not only push the boundaries of human knowledge and capabilities but also ensure a brighter future for all.
text_all:
Space exploration is undeniably a worthwhile investment for humanity. Firstly, it drives technological innovation. Many of the technologies we use in our daily lives, such as GPS, satellite communication, and medical imaging, have their roots in space exploration research and development. By investing in space exploration, we push the boundaries of what's possible technologically, leading to advancements that benefit society as a whole.

Furthermore, space exploration fosters international collaboration and cooperation. Projects like the International Space Station (ISS) bring together scientists, engineers, and astronauts from various countries, promoting diplomacy and understanding across borders. This collaboration not only enhances our ability to explore space but also strengthens global partnerships for addressing common challenges on Earth.

Space exploration also expands our understanding of the universe and our place within it. Through missions to other planets, moons, and beyond, we gain insights into planetary science, astrobiology, and the fundamental laws of physics. These discoveries not only satisfy our innate curiosity but also have practical implications, such as understanding the potential for life beyond Earth and assessing threats from asteroids or comets.

Moreover, investing in space exploration inspires the next generation of scientists, engineers, and explorers. The pursuit of space exploration captures the imagination of people around the world, encouraging students to pursue careers in STEM fields and pushing humanity forward through innovation and discovery.

In summary, space exploration offers numerous benefits to humanity, from driving technological innovation and fostering international cooperation to expanding our understanding of the universe and inspiring future generations. As we continue to invest in space exploration, we not only push the boundaries of human knowledge and capabilities but also ensure a brighter future for all. While it's true that space exploration incurs costs, the benefits far outweigh the expenses. Despite the notion that recent space explorations have been frivolous, it's essential to recognize the long-term impacts and indirect benefits that these endeavors bring.

Firstly, while some missions may seem less practical on the surface, they often contribute to fundamental scientific knowledge and pave the way for future breakthroughs. Even seemingly frivolous experiments can lead to unexpected discoveries and technological advancements that have far-reaching implications beyond their initial scope.

Additionally, the economic benefits of space exploration should not be underestimated. Investments in space exploration stimulate innovation and create new industries, leading to job creation and economic growth. For example, the commercial space sector has been booming in recent years, with companies like SpaceX, Blue Origin, and others driving innovation and competition in the market.

Furthermore, space exploration has practical applications that directly benefit society. Technologies developed for space missions often find applications in other fields, such as healthcare, transportation, and environmental monitoring. For instance, advancements in satellite technology have revolutionized communication, weather forecasting, and disaster management, saving lives and improving quality of life around the world.

Lastly, space exploration inspires and educates people of all ages, fostering a sense of wonder and curiosity about the universe. This inspiration motivates students to pursue careers in STEM fields, ultimately leading to a more scientifically literate and innovative society.

In conclusion, while space exploration may seem costly in the short term, the long-term benefits are undeniable. From driving scientific discovery and technological innovation to stimulating economic growth and inspiring future generations, investing in space exploration is a worthwhile endeavor that ultimately benefits humanity as a whole. While it's true that there are many research endeavors on the ground that also deserve funding, space exploration offers unique and unparalleled benefits to humanity that cannot be overlooked.

Firstly, space exploration drives technological innovation at an unprecedented pace. The challenges of exploring space push us to develop cutting-edge technologies that often have spin-off applications in various industries on Earth. These advancements not only improve our quality of life but also stimulate economic growth and create new opportunities for innovation.

Additionally, space exploration fosters international collaboration and diplomacy in ways that few other endeavors can match. Projects like the International Space Station demonstrate what humanity can achieve when we work together towards a common goal, transcending borders and political differences for the greater good of all.

Moreover, space exploration expands our understanding of the universe and our place within it. The knowledge gained from exploring other planets, moons, and celestial bodies not only satisfies our curiosity but also helps us address pressing challenges on Earth, such as climate change, resource management, and existential threats from space.

Furthermore, investing in space exploration inspires future generations and cultivates a culture of curiosity, innovation, and exploration. By sparking the imagination of young minds, space exploration encourages the pursuit of careers in STEM fields, ensuring a pipeline of talent that will drive progress and innovation for years to come.

In conclusion, space exploration is not just a worthwhile investment for humanity; it is essential for our continued progress and survival. By pushing the boundaries of what's possible, fostering collaboration and understanding, expanding our knowledge of the universe, and inspiring future generations, space exploration ensures a brighter and more prosperous future for all of humanity.

text_initial: I feel that the one thing bringing the world into severe climate change is the overreliance on fossil fuels and companies that produce them. By switching over to more reliable and alternative methods of energy, the United States has a chance to reduce the future harm done to the world and reverse some of the negative progress done by fossil fuel producers. This could include switching over to nuclear energy and creating programs to switch cars to electric for the most part.
text_all: I feel that the one thing bringing the world into severe climate change is the overreliance on fossil fuels and companies that produce them. By switching over to more reliable and alternative methods of energy, the United States has a chance to reduce the future harm done to the world and reverse some of the negative progress done by fossil fuel producers. This could include switching over to nuclear energy and creating programs to switch cars to electric for the most part. While I do agree that some uses for fossils fuels can't be avoided and I'm not really in favor of a complete ban, I think that providing easier ways to use alternative energy methods is a better way to combat climate change. With more people using electric cars, ones that use gas for whatever reason that the owner chooses won't be as prevalent in society. Additionally, if the better energy methods (better for the environment, that is) are made cheaper than traditional ones, more people would want to switch over and the big companies producing the fuels won't have as much of an impact on the world. I think that nuclear power is a very good thing when done right. The US has tons of trained professionals and creating more nuclear power plants can also create more jobs. Fossil fuels can't go away completely, but bringing new forms of energy, whether they be nuclear or some other form, can help supplement them in a way that makes the environment better in the long term. You could set aside fossil fuels for jets and cars, but leaving power for homes mostly nuclear seems like it would take some of the stress away. After all, transportation accounts for some of the smallest percentages of climate change's negative impact on the world. I think supplementing is a better idea, rather than complete abolition.

text_initial: Votes from residents in less populated states may be given more weight than those from residents in more populous states under the Electoral College system, which may lead to unequal representation. The idea of "one person, one vote" is compromised, and this may result in uneven influence in presidential elections.
text_all: Votes from residents in less populated states may be given more weight than those from residents in more populous states under the Electoral College system, which may lead to unequal representation. The idea of "one person, one vote" is compromised, and this may result in uneven influence in presidential elections. Smaller states may be somewhat protected by the Electoral College, but voters in these states have their voices disproportionately heard at the expense of those in larger states. As a result, there is an imbalance where a small number of votes in smaller states are worth more than a big number of votes in larger states. \n Currently, the Electoral College system offers undue weight to a small number of swing states, where candidates focus much of their energy because the outcome of the election is unpredictable in these states. This may result in policies that prioritize the needs of swing state voters over those of residents of non-swing states.\n

text_initial: Although women are over half of the population, they currently only take up a small percentage of leadership positions in companies and politics. Creating mandatory quotas for women in leadership positions would ensure that more women are hired, supported, and put into positions of leadership around the country.
text_all: Although women are over half of the population, they currently only take up a small percentage of leadership positions in companies and politics. Creating mandatory quotas for women in leadership positions would ensure that more women are hired, supported, and put into positions of leadership around the country.  Hostile work environments are already created when women are not properly supported at work. If the majority of the leaders at a company are men, it can be harder for a woman to find the support and mentorship she needs to rise to a leadership role. Mandatory quotas for women in leadership would make sure that women are given proper support and create overall work environments that are more accepting of women. It is true that the culture of current work environments contribute to the underrepresentation of women in leadership roles. As stated, there should be major changes in current work culture to change the dynamics and attitudes that lead to a shortage of women in leadership. Creating mandatory quotas for women in leadership positions could be one way to change the current dynamic and force companies to put more effort and money into programs that support women.

I also went to a school where uniforms were mandatory, and I disagree that they are helpful for poorer families. To sum it up, there is a need to give people equal opportunity to receive an education. We can do this efficiently by using a sliding scale to help those most in need.

text_initial: There should not be international agreements to enforce global climate change policies because they should be enacted within each individual country, rather than globally. There are various countries with holes in their ozone layer, and it should be their responsibility to fix it. Likewise, climate change more deeply affects certain countries with weather sensitive crops, or poorer weather. Those countries should do what they think is best to enact policies and help aid their citizens. Therefore, I believe that this proposition should be enforced locally rather than internationally.
text_all: There should not be international agreements to enforce global climate change policies because they should be enacted within each individual country, rather than globally. There are various countries with holes in their ozone layer, and it should be their responsibility to fix it. Likewise, climate change more deeply affects certain countries with weather sensitive crops, or poorer weather. Those countries should do what they think is best to enact policies and help aid their citizens. Therefore, I believe that this proposition should be enforced locally rather than internationally. It is not only chemicals that are causing climate change, but it is also machines and cars. Can we ask everyone to stop using those? That is very unlikely, especially on a global scale. The best solution would be for individual countries or states to pinpoint what is causing the most carbon emissions, or harmful agents into the atmosphere that are increasing climate change; then they can work on reducing this. Besides, there are always people that will not agree, and the fate of the world should not rest upon their shoulders.  Something is better than nothing. If people were to disagree to very beneficial things for the climate internationally, now no one can do them and this would only detriment the world further. Therefore, individual countries should be allowed to do what they want in order to bring about the best results for the cause.

text_initial: Government surveillance is necessary for national security because protecting national security requires the ability to find threats before they happen. There is a risk of national security threats from within the country. In order to find those threats, the government has to be able to monitor communication channels. This can include social media and private communication between citizens. Without surveillance, the government will essentially be blind to potential threats and will only be able to be rreactuve.
text_all: Government surveillance is necessary for national security because protecting national security requires the ability to find threats before they happen. There is a risk of national security threats from within the country. In order to find those threats, the government has to be able to monitor communication channels. This can include social media and private communication between citizens. Without surveillance, the government will essentially be blind to potential threats and will only be able to be rreactuve. While government surveillance seems very broad, it is not when it is only used for national security. Governments can have strict protocols for how the information gathered is used. Also, there is too much information for every citizen to be monitored closely. In most cases, the information will be viewed collectively, to look for patterns that indicate behavior that could threaten national security. Government surveillance is necessary to ensure that the nation remains safe. Without government surveillance, agencies responsible for the country's safety will have a very limited insight into developing threats. With surveillance, they can prevent greater harms to the country.

text_initial: Public College should be free because it gives every person the access to better tools, better careers and an inclusive education system that is not barred by socioeconomic class. Public tuition would also reduce the student debt in the country and allow young adults to start businesses and buy homes which will create more GDP, rather than living on modest income. Young adults start families later, and some dont start families at all because of student debt. The adults that do start families, end up having to take two jobs which results in children being neglected which begins a cycle of poverty and poor education in the country. Public tuition will help end socioeconomic racism against people of color and those with disabilities or other hardship from the ability to attend college. Of course people who want to pay for private college still can, but public education will result in an educated work force that is more productive, efficient and happy!

Many of top companies like Apple and Google were started and staffed by college educated adults, who had to pay. The possibility of their success, had they had free college, would be even more!

text_initial:
Hi Everyone, I strongly oppose the idea that felons should be granted the right to vote. 

Voting is a right reserved for law-abiding citizens who make meaningful contributions to the community. Felons, by definition, have broken our society's laws and are therefore ineligible to enjoy some rights, including the ability to vote. Allowing them to engage in the political process sends the incorrect message and undermines the importance of citizenship. 

Furthermore, restoring voting rights to criminals without adequate protections jeopardizes the integrity of our electoral system. Felons may still have strong interests that conflict with the public good, and their involvement in elections may distort the results in favor of their own agenda. 

While I recognize the significance of rehabilitation and reintegration into society, providing criminals voting rights contradicts the concepts of justice and accountability.

Retreating from these responsibilities can diminish our global standing and influence.

text_initial: Animals should not be used for scientific research because they are living beings who cannot consent to undergoing this testing. We do not use humans for research because it is dangerous, could cause injury/death, and moral reasons. These moral reasons also apply to animals, as they are living creatures with consciousness, identity, and feelings. In addition, information learned from animal testing does not always translate into humans, which creates a risk of faulty clinical trials because of the possibility for imprecise results. Lastly, most research and testing done on animals is performed merely out of curiosity and does not have any actual meaningful implications or scientific contributions.
text_all: Animals should not be used for scientific research because they are living beings who cannot consent to undergoing this testing. We do not use humans for research because it is dangerous, could cause injury/death, and moral reasons. These moral reasons also apply to animals, as they are living creatures with consciousness, identity, and feelings. In addition, information learned from animal testing does not always translate into humans, which creates a risk of faulty clinical trials because of the possibility for imprecise results. Lastly, most research and testing done on animals is performed merely out of curiosity and does not have any actual meaningful implications or scientific contributions.  If we are comfortable using animals for scientific testing because we need to use living things, then it should be entirely ethical to use humans for testing in the same ways. This would not only reduce the number of animals who are harmed as well as make scientific evidence ore broadly applicable with humans. There is no responsible or safe way to test on animals, because a majority of these experiments end in great injury or even death to the animals used. It is unethical to use living beings for potentially deadly scientific research when they cannot consent. While humans are used for scientific experiments, they are fully able to provide informed consent and knowingly acknowledge what they are participating in. Animals do not have the ability to do this, and we use them against their will, often killing them, for our own gain. We could reduce the amount of life lost meaninglessly if we did not rely on testing done on an entirely different species to advance our own scientific endeavors. The moral implications of using animals for scientific testing run parallel to those related to using humans for experiments (e.g., ethics, informed consent), and it calls to the forefront our perception of the value of life. Because the animals we use are not humans, we do not think their life is as valuable as ours, so we breed them and kill them for our own benefit.

text_initial: Standardized testing should be required in schools because there has to be a way to determine everyone's skill level. By having standardized testing, where every student studies the same material and is given the same questions and amount of time to take the test, we can use the results of the tests to better determine what needs to be taught more/less and who needs more help in certain areas. It is simply a good way to have a base standard to go from when determining the academic strengths and weaknesses of students.
text_all: Standardized testing should be required in schools because there has to be a way to determine everyone's skill level. By having standardized testing, where every student studies the same material and is given the same questions and amount of time to take the test, we can use the results of the tests to better determine what needs to be taught more/less and who needs more help in certain areas. It is simply a good way to have a base standard to go from when determining the academic strengths and weaknesses of students. Creativity is great to see in students, but so is learning how to handle challenges like standardized testing. If students are just allowed to give up they never will learn the value of pushing through tough times to come out the other side stronger and more knowledgeable, which is a critical life skill. Student's abilities can be determined by the tests and they can be given extra help where it is needed. By studying and preparing for these tests, students learn resilience, consistency and to trust in themselves.  I can appreciate your argument against standardized testing and that they do not capture the full picture of a student's potential. However, I do believe that with so many students and not much time in a school year to individually cater to each one, these tests can help paint a helpful picture of where each student struggles and excels. They can be a tool that can be used to tweak lessons, create smaller study groups based on skills, and in that way each student can essentially get the help and attention they need to succeed in both school and on any standardized tests. Thank you for your time today.

text_initial: Elected or appointed government officials should not be paid the minimum wage. A big reason for this is that the current minimum wage — whether it be state or federal — is not high enough to correspond with work of that caliber. In other words, given that these people are to represent regular citizens and engage in government activities, not many people will be enticed to apply with such a low-paying position.
text_all: Elected or appointed government officials should not be paid the minimum wage. A big reason for this is that the current minimum wage — whether it be state or federal — is not high enough to correspond with work of that caliber. In other words, given that these people are to represent regular citizens and engage in government activities, not many people will be enticed to apply with such a low-paying position. It is true that lowering a government official's salary all the way to the minimum wage would give them a better sense of what it is like to live as the average citizen. However, at that minimum wage per year, these officials would also be more susceptible than before to bribery and corruption. This already exists with the salary they have now, and would likely increase exponentially if they were paid the minimum per year! It is a great point that pay equality should be done across the board. If we really mean to include government officials in this group — just like food service workers for example — then we must value all occupations at their core. In other words, politics is not that different from any other occupation in the sense that: the more people are willing to pay for a job, the better candidates will show up for a position. text_initial: Artificial intelligence is not good for society.  One of the main reasons is the job loss that it was cause in a variety of industries.  Another reason is that the AI is only as "smart" as the person doing the coding.  AI only knows what people tell it to know and what it gathers online.  In today's political climate and with our nation being so divided, I fear that AI will be biased towards one side, simply because one side controls the narrative.  Further, AI tools do not have morals or ethics and are incapable of navigating complex issues that may arise that need a human touch.
text_all: Artificial intelligence is not good for society.  One of the main reasons is the job loss that it was cause in a variety of industries.  Another reason is that the AI is only as "smart" as the person doing the coding.  AI only knows what people tell it to know and what it gathers online.  In today's political climate and with our nation being so divided, I fear that AI will be biased towards one side, simply because one side controls the narrative.  Further, AI tools do not have morals or ethics and are incapable of navigating complex issues that may arise that need a human touch. I do acknowledge that AI can automate routine tasks, which is definitely a perk.  However, are companies still going to be paying employees a full salary when the "routine" part of their jobs have been removed?  I can foresee this leading to massive layoffs as companies are able to consolidate the workforce into smaller numbers.   Most people doing those "routine" jobs that can be automated by AI are likely not in the position to go back to school and be retrained to either 1) pursue a different career or 2) pursue a new AI-related job.  What do you do with those people?  Who will pay for this additional education?  How will these people afford to live during the time they are being retrained?  I feel like we will have a large amount of blue collar workers stuck with no path forward.
